articles.title: Efforts to save crumbling Washington Park cottage draw a step closer to reality with financial pledge from Roanoke council
articles.author: Samantha Verrelli
articles.body:
An advocacy group supporting Roanoke’s 19th-century Washington Park Caretaker’s Cottage has worked to raise about $20,000 to save the building, which has been falling further into disrepair for years.

The group now has almost eight times that amount of funding to move forward with plans for partial restoration of the property to turn it into an amphitheater — an idea that multiple people within the Friends of Washington Park Caretaker’s Cottage recently said they support as a feasible compromis

articles.body:

articles.title: Today is Local News Day. Help us celebrate.
articles.author: Cardinal Staff
articles.body:
Cardinal News is one of more than 1,300 newsrooms across the country today observing the first Local News Day.

Local News Day is a national day of action to reconnect people with trusted local news and empower newsrooms to grow, and is intended to spark a national movement that sustains local news for generations.

Since 2005, the U.S. has lost more than 2,500 newspapers and 60% of local reporters. Local News Day responds to this loss by spotlighting the essential role of local news and helping

articles.title: April set to hit dry spell after monthslong dry tilt
articles.author: Kevin Myatt
articles.body:
April showers bring May flowers. But April showers may be in short supply.

We are entering what is likely to be a prolonged bone-dry period in Virginia, following what has already been several months of below-normal precipitation that has left the majority of the commonwealth in moderate to severe drought on the latest U.S. Drought Monitor.

This dry spell is developing because of a significant re-shuffling of the prevailing atmospheric pattern over North America.

For much of winter, the weste

articles.title: Guitar festival celebrates its 25th year of bringing musicians from around the globe to Radford
articles.author: Tad Dickens
articles.body:
Summer breaks in the late 1970s found classical guitarist Robert Trent traveling from music college in Philadelphia across the Canadian border to Quebec, where he studied at summer music festivals.

“The teachers were from France and I had an immersive experience in music but also language and culture,” Trent said. “Those three summers transformed me as a musician and person, and I’ve been an advocate for international education ever since.”

Trent, a Radford University professor, wanted to brin

articles.title: If Virginia votes ‘yes,’ Democrats will take the lead in the national redistricting battle
articles.author: Dwayne Yancey
articles.body:
I can predict with confidence who will win Virginia’s special election on redistricting on April 21: Fairness will win.

I know this because both sides are claiming to be the fair side. We have “Virginians for Fair Elections” on the “yes” side and “Virginians for Fair Maps” on the “no” side.

articles.title: ALEA: Wreck forces lane closure on I-65 in Limestone County
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
A wreck Wednesday morning on Interstate 65 caused a lane closure for southbound traffic along the Interstate in Limestone County.

According to the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency (ALEA), the two-vehicle accident occurred at approximately 11:18 a.m. on Wednesday (April 8) and caused road blockage that required the closure of the right southbound lane near mile marker 358.

ALEA said the lane would remain closed “for an undetermined amount of time” as Troopers with the agency’s Highway Patrol Divi

articles.title: Bank Independent launches 2026 Food and Family Share Drive
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Bank Independent has launched Food and Family Share, a food collection initiative to benefit north Alabama families in need that began on April 6. For the past 12 years, Bank Independent has hosted the Food Share drive to help fight food insecurity in local communities.

Last year, the initiative expanded to include the Family Share component, collecting not only non-perishable food items but also baby diapers to support local families in need.

articles.title: ALEA Troopers complete Marine Patrol Boating School ahead of outdoor season
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Four Alabama Law Enforcement Agency (ALEA) Troopers have successfully completed the Agency’s Marine Patrol Boating School and are now prepared to serve and protect on Alabama’s waterways, just in time for the busy spring and summer boating season.

Three of the four Troopers were members of ALEA Trooper Class 2025-B who graduated from the Training Center in October of 2025. Following their graduation, each Trooper completed the Field Training Evaluation Program (FTEP) on Alabama’s roadways befor

articles.title: Lifelong learning: Athens State debuts ‘Career Advantage’ initiative for working adults
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens State University has launched a new learning initiative; one aimed at expanding educational and career training options for adults whose busy working lives leave little time for a traditional academic schedule.

The university bills its new Career Advantage initiative as “a comprehensive framework designed to better serve adult learners, strengthen workforce alignment, and position the institution at the forefront of higher education innovation.”

articles.title: Tornado response earns Athens Main Street statewide ‘Award of Excellence’
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens Main Street’s local outreach efforts in the aftermath of the December 28, 2024 tornado that struck downtown Athens has earned statewide recognition for the local nonprofit from the Public Relations Council of Alabama (PRCA).

The downtown Athens advocacy organization recently was named an “Award of Excellence” recipient from the PRCA for its communications response in the wake of the EF-1 tornado, which heavily damaged portions of the city’s historic center.

articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens Police Chief Anthony Pressnell recently earned Level II certification in the Certified Law Enforcement Executive Program (CLEEP), an administrative training program offered through Jacksonville State University’s Center for Best Practices in Law Enforcement.

Pressnell formally received recognition at the annual meeting of the Alabama Association of Chiefs of Police (AACOP) in Montgomery, after competing 80 hours of required training toward the certification.

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Gov. Kay Ivey today signed the General Fund and Education Trust Fund bills, her office announced.

“Throughout my tenure as governor and even before, we have worked hard to be fiscally conservative in our budgeting, making meaningful investments to benefit the people of Alabama,” Ivey said in a statement. “This Regular Session wraps up my 10th as governor, and I am proud of the fact that we have not once declared proration. articles.title: Southern Brew Thru celebrates 1-year anniversary Thursday
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Southern Brew Thru celebrated its one-year anniversary on April 9 at its location on Lindberg Avenue.

The local coffee drive thru is open Monday through Friday from 5:30 a.m. until 5:30 p.m., and on Saturdays from 7 a.m. until 1 p.m.

Atmore Area Chamber of Commerce board members and chamber ambassadors were present to mark the occasion.

articles.body:

articles.title: Dream comes true for 2 new homeowners
articles.author: Lisa Tindell
articles.body:
The dream of owning a home is out of reach for many people, but for two lucky, hardworking families, that dream became a reality on Tuesday afternoon with home dedication ceremonies.

Jessica Hardy and her children were given the keys to their new home on Rankin Street. The three-bedroom, two-bath home built by volunteers and contractors with the Escambia County Habitat for Humanity will be a safe, affordable home for the family for years to come.

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The annual Murder Creek Mud Run is making its return this weekend hosted by the City of Brewton and Phoenix Racing.

The event will begin with a start location at O’Bannon Park and will cover a course that takes runners through three miles of obstacles through woods and water through the E.O. Wilson Nature Adventure Park, Jennings Park and returning to O’Bannon Park for the finish.

articles.title: CCHS sweeps Holtville as part of week’s soccer action
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Chilton County High School’s soccer clubs kept in good form during the week’s action from April 2-8.

The week’s first action came on April 2 as the junior varsity and varsity boys’ teams hosted Spain Park High School. CCHS’ junior varsity team took control of the match from the opening whistle and dominated possession throughout. Eighth grader Alston Chen grabbed the opening goal of the match, and freshman Jose Tejada put the match away with a goal in the second half.

articles.title: Chilton area teams jockey for playoff spots during week’s action
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
With a three-game winning streak, Maplesville High School finished off with an area win over Billingsley High School, clinching home field for its area tournament. In the same three-game winning streak, Mollie Kayte Smith and Kennedy Reed had no-hitters in Maplesville’s doubleheader against Breakthrough Charter School.

articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
The home stretch of the 2026 high school baseball season is underway in Chilton County as teams jockey for playoff spots in key area matchups.

Chilton County High School dropped its opening Class 6A Area 5 game of the season, 12-0 to Calera High School on April 2. The next day, the Tigers swept the doubleheader led by Garrett Lawley’s impressive performance in game one going the full distance giving up only two runs. articles.title: Thorsby teachers, students have rare Swedish experience
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
Several Thorsby educators and students did an educational tour through New York and Washington, D.C. during their spring break from March 23-27. The highlight of the trip was a visit to the Swedish Embassy — which is very special given Thorsby’s Swedish heritage.

articles.title: Chilton students awarded scholarship from Poarch Band of Creek Indians
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Mylie Lucas of Jemison and Henry Roberts of Clanton were awarded scholarships by the Poarch Band of Creek Indians (PBCI) for their participation in Alabama’s livestock industry. The two students were awarded the scholarships in March.

The PBCI has a long history of supporting youth involved in the livestock industry in Alabama. Students from each of Alabama’s 67 counties are awarded scholarships from the PBCI to aid in future participation in youth livestock events.

articles.title: Chilton agencies assist in high-speed chase following Calera Walmart robbery
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Chilton County agencies assisted the Calera Police Department on April 5 after a robbery at Walmart led to a multi-county high-speed chase.

At around 2:20 p.m. on Easter Sunday, Calera officers responded to a call about a retail theft at Walmart. When they arrived, the suspects fled north onto U.S. Highway 31, then eventually merged onto Interstate 65 southbound. The suspects were driving recklessly and at high speeds. articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Thorsby High School senior lineman J.T. Green solidified the next steps in his academic and football journey by signing with Huntingdon University. The senior lineman joins a lengthy list of fellow Chilton County athletes who now call the Montgomery-area school home.

“It was great, and especially being one of the first people in my family to do it,” Green said. “There are not many people in my family that go off to play college ball, and it is definitely a dream come true.”

articles.body:
West Point’s boys and Cold Springs’ girls dominated the competition on Wednesday, securing their respective titles at this year’s Cullman County Championship.

Asa Sutter (triple jump, 110-meter hurdles, 300-meter hurdles), Malik Howell (shot put) and Rush Lamar (200-meter dash) secured first-place performances to help the Warriors finish with 170 team points and a second straight title.

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –The Cullman City Council at its Monday, April 6, meeting took a few moments to recognize winners of the local Elks Club 2026 Hoop Shoot.

Winners included Alex Wood of Holly Pond Elementary School, Avery Stricklin of Good Hope Elementary School, Kylie Minor of East Elementary School, John Carl Knight of Sacred Heart Elementary School and Paxton Plourd of Parkside Elementary School.

The 2026 Hoop Shoot National Finals will take place April 16-19 in Chicago.

articles.body:
BREMEN, Ala. –The 2026 Cullman County outdoor track championship was held at Cold Springs high school on Wednesday.

In the boys competitions, West Point ran away with a 170 point victory to repeat as county champions. Senior Asa Sutter earned three gold medals in the triple jump, 110mH and 300mH. articles.title: Know the difference between heat exhaustion and heat stroke
articles.body:
As spring heats up and gives way to summer, it’s important to know the signs of heat exhaustion and heat stroke.

Heat usually peaks between noon-3 p.m. so it’s best to avoid going outdoors during these hours. If you must, try limiting yourself to short bursts. Take breaks and be sure you are well hydrated.

As you hydrate, avoid caffeinated or alcoholic beverages. Be sure to drink water throughout stopping periods, but never drink ice-cold water too fast when you’re overheated. If you are overh

articles.title: Local Musician Spotlight: Case Graham
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –Case Graham is a local musician with an excellent reputation for his guitar skills. His style is a combination of rock and metal mixed with groove funk, layered with heavy jazz influence.

Though he knew from a young age that he wanted to play guitar, he said, it wasn’t easy.

At age 9 he told his parents he wanted to play, though they presented him with a challenge. He could play guitar, but only after two years of piano lessons. That was that! He set off on his journey of keys a

articles.title: Barefoot University Butterfly Buffet
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– On Friday, April 3, families gathered at the Sportsman Lake Park Wildflower Garden for a hands-on event called the “Butterfly Buffet,” hosted by Barefoot University.

Alex Bass, who leads Barefoot University with Rachel Phillips, explained that the activity helps children connect with nature in a fun and simple way.

“The Butterfly Buffet is a way to get them involved with their ecosystems at home. They can make little buffets for butterflies using different kinds of fruit and hop

articles.title: Sportsman Lake Park hosts annual Easter Egg Hunt
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Sportsman Lake Park held its annual Easter Egg Hunt early Saturday morning, April 4. This long-standing tradition keeps growing each year. The free event took place at the park playground and welcomed children of all ages for activities, photos with the Easter Bunny and lots of candy.

“We put this on every year for probably the past 20 years,” said Cullman County Parks and Recreation Marketing and Events Coordinator Sarah Morgan. “We always make sure it’s a free event. The Easter

articles.title: Jacobs proclaims April 12–18 Public Safety Telecommunicators Week in city
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Cullman Mayor Woody Jacobs has proclaimed April 12–18, 2026  Public Safety Telecommunicators Week in the city of Cullman, recognizing the critical role emergency dispatchers play in protecting lives and property across the community.

Public Safety Telecommunicators serve as the first point of contact during emergencies, answering calls, gathering vital information and dispatching police, fire and emergency medical services quickly and accurately. Their work ensures that first res

articles.title: Lanett commends heroic officer, recognizes Valley Haven
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
David and Annette Rowland of the Alabama chapter of the American Red Cross were at the Tuesday meeting of the Lanett City Council to present a commendation to Lanett police officer Cody Bolt for the heroism and quick thinking he displayed in saving the life of a 17-year-old gunshot victim in October 2024.

“He took action in extreme circumstances to save someone’s life,” said David Rowland. “We commend Cody Bolt for the actions he took on October 17, 2024. Bolt had been called to the scene of a

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Bill King: I’ve Never Been to Oklahoma, But I’ve Been to Spain
articles.author: Bill King
articles.body:
Yes, I know the songs says, “Well, I’ve never been to Spain, but I’ve been to Oklahoma,” but the opposite is now true for me. I have now visited Spain and every state on every side of Oklahoma, but not Oklahoma. Eventually, I’ll get to Oklahoma and Delaware to complete my 50 states. I must say that Spain was special and spectacular.

I know we’ve heard that the rain in Spain falls mainly on the plains. We spent most of our first week in Spain traveling along the Mediterranean coast in the southe

articles.title: Lanett council hears animal complaints
articles.body:
A Lanett resident asked to speak to the Lanett City Council on a public safety issue and was on the agenda for Tuesday’s meeting to discuss it. Craig Allen talked to the city council about the enforcement and effectiveness of the city’s present animal control ordinance.

Some 20 years ago, Lanett adopted a leash law following the death of a 70-year-old woman who had been attacked in Lanett by a pit bull.

In adopting that ordinance, Allen said, the city was taking a stand that something like tha

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Dean Collins: Getting Rid of Catastrophic Thinking
articles.author: Dean Collins
articles.body:
What if...

What if oil prices continue to rise?

What if the war causes long term harm to my retirement savings?

What if I can’t pay my mortgage?

What if… (fill in your worst nightmare).

We have seen many examples of catastrophic thinking in recent weeks. Just pick a topic and look at your favorite source of information, and you hear the panic and anxiety rise. You’ve probably had conversations with a family member that includes some catastrophic thinking. Over the last several weeks it’s li

articles.title: Mental health walk returns to Lanett
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
A free mental health walk is coming to downtown Lanett on Saturday, May 2. The event is being co-organized by two local advocacy groups, The Big Charlie Foundation, founded by Shemeka Brock, and the Bless the Streetz initiative, started by Courtney Cammon.

According to Brock, she started the Big Charlie Foundation to honor her brother, Charlie, and to teach the importance of mental, financial, physical, and spiritual well-being. The first walk was held in 2024 and was labeled a general health w

articles.title: LaFayette hears updates, resolves conflict
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The LaFayette City Council meeting on April 7 kicked off with addresses from several entities including representatives from LaFayette’s wastewater plant, representatives from Harmon Engineering and the director of Queen’s Place, Beverly Price.

Queen’s Place is a nonprofit organization based in Dadeville, Alabama, and seeks to expand its footprint in the state, beginning with Macon and Chambers Counties. Currently, their focus encompasses tutoring (with an emphasis on second and third grade lit

articles.title: Jennings runs for BOE District 4
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
Jody Jennings is running for the Chambers County School Board for District 4. He is currently the Republican incumbent running against Mary Terry in the May 19 election.

Jennings, a lifelong Chambers County resident, currently lives in the unincorporated area of Lanett. By trade, Jennings is a plumber who owns and operates a local business.

A core theme of Jennings’ candidacy is increased emphasis on career and trade pathways for students as an alternative to a purely academic career path. He

articles.title: Valley’s Carter served big role in Artemis II launch
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
When Houston has a technology problem, it’s often Crystal Carter’s job to fix it. The Valley native is the software mission assurance quality discipline lead at NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama. All flight software for NASA is developed in Huntsville, and it’s Carter’s job to keep it running.

“I am responsible for ensuring that the flight software is safe, that it is reliable, and that it meets all mission and regulatory requirements. So, I basically provide independen

articles.title: UNITE announces sixth annual HBCU scholarship
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
LaFayette-based nonprofit UNITE is once again offering scholarships to seniors graduating from Chambers County’s public high schools who are attending Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCU).

UNITE will award one $1,000 scholarship to a graduating senior from LaFayette, Lanett or Valley High School. The only criteria to be eligible for the scholarship besides being a graduating senior is applicants must show proof of acceptance and enrollment at a HBCU.

UNITE Executive Director Dr.

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Cathy Hunt: Ramblin' Through Spring Break
articles.author: Cathy Hunt
articles.body:
It’s time for another of my “ramblin’” columns, where, instead of focusing on one topic, I touch on a few different items which are currently occupying my brain space. I ask that you please refrain from making a wisecrack about how I always ramble, even though you might have a point.

My beloved mother turned 90 a few days ago, and we had a nice celebration with family and longtime friends. Mom was the belle of the ball, in close competition with her 1-year-old great-granddaughter, who was undau

articles.title: The Oaks Farm Dance supports local barrel racers
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
Get ready for an evening of music, dinner and excitement as The Oaks in LaFayette will host a fundraising event for two barrel racing competitors.

Organizer Barbie Belcher says the event will help two young ladies afford expenses related to barrel racing. She says the sport is costly, noting that entry fees, animal care, and training opportunities are among the top expenses encountered. Belcher’s granddaughter, Paislee Seals, and great niece, Chelsey Lisle, are up and coming in the sport and tr

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Lynn Gendusa: Meeting the Lord Through ‘Grandpa’
articles.author: Lynn Gendusa
articles.body:
“Mama, why was Jesus born at Christmas and dead by Easter?” I asked her when I was very young. I didn’t understand the concept of time; all I knew were the celebrations of his birth, and it felt like he was gone almost as soon as he arrived!

My mother explained as best she could to her daughter, who had the attention span of a flea. I am sure I was hopping to my next destination before she finished the story of Jesus.

I grew up attending Sunday School.  Once I realized that Jesus lived for thi

articles.title: Reed Foundation names annual Helping Hand winners
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Reed Foundation has announced the 2026 Helping Hand Award recipients, ahead of its fifth annual Community Gala. This year’s recipients are Chambers County Commissioner and Chairman Doug Jones, Curtis Lewis, Chris Clark and Madalyn “Penny” Lee.

Assistant Executive Director Clifton Autry said the Helping Hand Award is given to community individuals each year who deserve to be in the spotlight but often aren’t.

“[They are people] who are not putting themselves out front, boasting themselves,

articles.title: Luverne students experience Auburn E Day
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Students from Luverne High School visited Auburn University recently to participate in Engineering Day (E Day). The event is an outreach recruiting day for the college, allowing it's students to show off some of what they as part of the engineering programs.

“We took a group of 32 students from Luverne High School,” said Luverne science teacher, Bill Ossenfort. “We had sophomores, juniors and seniors attend.”

Students viewed nuclear power generation, chemical, aerospace, mechanical and compute

articles.title: CCA crowns pageant winners under Broadway lights
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Crenshaw Christian Academy crowned pageant winners recently during its Broadway themed event, "New York, New York" Contestants from grades K3 - 12th grade dazzled audience members under the Big Apple spotlight on Feb. 28, selecting winners from among CCA's most talented young women.

Winners crowned in the pageant are:

Little Miss Kindergarten Division: Chloee Smith, 1st Runner-Up; Laura-Ann Stewart, 2nd Runner-Up; Karson Lewis, 2026 Little Miss Kindergarten and Photogenic Winner and Emory Fowl

articles.title: What's Happening in Crenshaw County
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Visit our website at www.luvernejournal.com to view a live, interactive events calendar.

Art Club

The Luverne Public Library will host Art Club with Mrs. Janet for ages 9 - 18 on first Thursdays, 4 - 5 p.m. The next session is set for April 9.

Citizen Banquet

The 2026 Crenshaw County Citizen of the Year banquet is set for April 9. Doors open at 5:30 p.m., dinner at 6 p.m. Tickets are $25 per person for Chamber members, $35 for non-members or $175 for a table of eight. Call (334) 355-6200 for

articles.title: When evil becomes routine
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
What if the most dangerous wrongdoing doesn’t look like evil at all?

In a new “This Matters with Bill Britt,” Britt explores how harm becomes routine—hidden in systems, policies, and everyday decisions made without question.

Drawing on Hannah Arendt, he warns: the greatest threat isn’t rage—it’s compliance.

Subscribe to “This Matters with Bill Britt” onYouTube,Spotify,Appleor anywhere you get your podcasts.

articles.title: House passes bill criminalizing improper dog tethering
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
The Alabama House of Representatives passed Senate Bill 361 on Wednesday, despite opposition from a bipartisan group of rural lawmakers, to create new standards for tethering companion dogs.

Senator Garlan Gudger, R-Cullman, sponsored the bill, which would prohibit tethering dogs to a stationary point such as a stake, tree, pole or structure. The only legal way to tether a dog outside under the bill would be with a trolley system, where the dog is attached to a line running between two stationa

articles.title: Bill to close primaries moves to Senate on what could be session’s final day
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
An Alabama Senate committee on Tuesday gave approval to a bill that would close primaries, requiring voters to register with a party to participate.

House Bill 541 by Representative Ernie Yarbrough, R-Trinity, would require a voter to register with a party to participate in primary elections after January 1, 2027. Once assigned to a party, that voter could only switch party alignment up to 60 days before a primary.

The Alabama Republican Party has championed the bill, alleging that Democrats h

articles.title: Senate passes industry-to-classroom workforce tax credit bill
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Alabama Senate on Tuesday passed a workforce development measure designed to address shortages in career and technical education by creating a new tax credit program to bring industry professionals into classrooms as instructors.

House Bill 517, sponsored by Representative James Lomax, R-Huntsville, or the Talent Readiness and Industry Needs Act, establishes a targeted incentive to allow private employers to “loan” skilled workers to public schools and community colleges to teach in designa

articles.title: House OKs bill to exclude credit card transaction fees from sales tax
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
On Wednesday the Alabama House of Representatives passed Senate Bill 221, legislation sponsored by Senator Arthur Orr, R-Decatur, that would exclude credit card transaction fees from sales and use tax in the state.

Under the current policies of the Alabama Department of Revenue, merchants who charge credit card transaction fees in Alabama must pay sales and use tax on those fees. SB221 would overrule that policy through statute, officially exempting those fees from sales and use tax moving forw

articles.title: House approves roting rights restoration transparency bill
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
The Alabama House passed legislation on Wednesday seeking to streamline the voting rights restoration process for individuals released from incarceration.

Senate Bill 24, sponsored by Senator Linda Coleman-Madison, D-Birmingham, would require the Board of Pardons and Paroles to post “easily accessible” instructions for individuals seeking to have their voting rights restored. The bill would also require the board to notify the Alabama secretary of state with a list of individuals who have had t

articles.title: Opinion | Alabama Republicans are running scared
articles.author: Angelo “Doc” Mancuso
articles.body: By any measure, Alabama Republicans should be operating from a position of strength. They hold supermajorities in Montgomery, dominate statewide offices, and routinely win congressional seats by margins that would be unthinkable in most of the country. Yet instead of projecting confidence, the party’s recent actions reveal something very different: a shrinking circle of insiders tightening their grip on who gets to run, who gets to vote, and who gets to participate in the political process at al

articles.title: Opinion | Why the Union Pacific–Norfolk Southern merger threatens Alabama
articles.author: Ed Oliver
articles.body:
Alabama’s economy runs on rail.

From the farms of the Tennessee Valley to the industries of Birmingham and the docks at the Port of Mobile, more than 3,000 miles of freight rail knit the state together. That network allows producers in places like Northeast Alabama and along Lake Guntersville to move goods efficiently to global markets, turning crops, timber and raw materials into economic growth.

That system works because it is competitive. And that’s exactly what’s at risk.

A proposed merge

articles.title: Opinion | Closing Alabama’s primaries won’t strengthen the GOP; it will weaken accountability
articles.author: David Donaldson
articles.body:
As a native Alabamian from a long line of Republicans, I’m deeply troubled by House Bill 541 and the push to close Alabama’s primaries. I attended the Senate committee hearing on Tuesday and had the privilege to speak. I was struck by the gap between the arguments offered by proponents and what Alabama’s election data actually shows.

Advocates for HB541 insist the primary is merely a private party selection process. The “real election” where all voters get their say is in November, they argue.

articles.title: Gov. Ivey announces record CHOOSE Act applications for 2026–27 school year
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Wednesday announced that Alabama’s education savings account program—the CHOOSE Act—drew a record number of applicants in its second year.

The application window closed at midnight March 31, with 48,927 students applying, according to the state.

Ivey said she has prioritized Alabama students throughout her tenure and will continue to champion their success.

The 2026-27 CHOOSE Act application deadline closed with 29,341 applications representing 48,927 students. That inclu

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs Sarah Marsh Heaven’s 27 Camp Safety Act into law
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Wednesday signed House Bill 381, the Sarah Marsh Heaven’s 27 Camp Safety Act, reinforcing her commitment to ensuring the safety and preparedness of Alabama’s summer camps.

Sponsored by Representative David Faulkner, HB381 aims to improve camp safety throughout Alabama by requiring plans to address a range of situations in which campers and staff could be endangered, including severe storms, flash flooding or other natural or man-made disasters.

“Keeping children safe must

articles.title: Alabama joins A Home for Every Child initiative
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Wednesday joined Administration for Children and Families Assistant Secretary Alex J. Adams and Alabama Department of Human Resources Commissioner Nancy Buckner to sign aproclamationformalizing a renewed partnership between Alabama and ACF within the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, according to a news release from the governor’s office.

The release said the agreement reinforces a shared commitment to improving outcomes for children and families across the stat

articles.title: Opinion | Why your dog might be smarter than you
articles.author: Tom Greene
articles.body:
There are two kinds of people in this world: people who love dogs and people I don’t understand. See, dogs are truly one of life’s greatest gifts and I’ve often wondered if they have more to teach us than we have to teach them.

My dog treats every meal like it’s the finest meal in the history of food. Same bowl. Same food. Same place. Every single day. He acts like Chef Wolfgang Puck personally prepared it just for him. Sometimes he’ll look up at me in between bites, as if to say, “can you beli

articles.title: Inmates up for parole will finally be able to attend their own hearings
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
At Alabama parole hearings, one person has always been conspicuously absent: the person whose fate the board is deciding.

That will change next year if Gov. Kay Ivey signs SB240, which unanimously passed the House of Representatives on Tuesday. The bill would allow incarcerated people to attend their parole hearings remotely and give a statement to the board.

Rep. Chris England, D-Tuscaloosa, sponsored the bill, and Sen. Will Barfoot, R-Pike Road, carried it in the Senate.

SB240 is one of two

articles.title: House passes bill revising Alabama sex education standards
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Alabama House of Representatives passed legislation that would revise the state’s public school sex education law to teach sexual-risk avoidance instead of comprehensive sex education.

Senate Bill 209, sponsored by Senator Shay Shelnutt, R-Trussville, moved to the House floor through Representative Susan Dubose, R-Hoover. Dubose said the measure builds on existing law rather than replacing it.

“This bill simply updates our sex ed curriculum,” she told lawmakers. “We are maintaining abstine

articles.title: Pamela Casey makes case for prosecutor-led AG office on This Matters with Bill Britt
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
What should Alabama’s attorney general be—a politician, or a prosecutor?

In a newThis Matters with Bill Brittinterview, Republican candidate Pamela Casey argues the job demands real courtroom experience, not political ambition, while raising concerns about money and influence in the office.

Drawing on two decades as a prosecutor, Casey makes her case for independence, accountability and equal justice.

Subscribe to “This Matters with Bill Britt” onYouTube,Spotify,Appleor anywhere you get your

articles.title: Rep. Sewell calls Trump’s behavior in war with Iran “increasingly erratic and unhinged”
articles.author: Chance Phillips
articles.body:
On Tuesday, President Donald Trump threatened to wipe out Iran in aTruth Social post. Less than twelve hours later, heannounceda two-week ceasefire will begin, contingent on Iran no longer blocking the Strait of Hormuz.

“A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again,” the president wrote in the morning. “I don’t want that to happen, but it probably will.” Several members of Congressdescribedthe post as threatening the genocide of the Iranian people. Pope Leo XIVcalled it

articles.title: Senate committee advances bill to create Second Amendment sales tax holiday
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
The Alabama Senate Finance and Taxation Education Committee passed House Bill 360 on Tuesday, advancing legislation to create an annual Second Amendment sales tax holiday in the state.

State Representative Chris Sells, R-Greenville, sponsored the bill. It would exempt purchases of firearms, ammunition and eligible hunting supplies from state sales and use tax during the last weekend in August. It would also allow counties and municipalities to opt in and exempt those sales from local sales and

articles.title: House passes bill aimed at discretion, fairness in parole revocation procedures
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
The Alabama House of Representatives passed legislation Tuesday aimed at giving the Alabama Board of Pardons and Paroles more clarity and discretion in parole revocation cases.

Senate Bill 254, sponsored by Senator Sam Givhan, R-Huntsville, would give the board the authority to hold hearings to review and choose whether to reinstate parole when it has been revoked over a new charge that is later dismissed or reduced to a lesser offense.

When a person on parole is arrested for a new offense but

articles.title: Legislature passes Alabama Law Enforcement Officers’ Family Scholarship
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Legislature on Tuesday awarded final passage to the Alabama Law Enforcement Officers’ Family Scholarship Act.

Governor Kay Ivey and House Speaker Nathaniel Ledbetter, R–Rainsville, who have both championed this legislation over the past two sessions, shared their satisfaction with the legislation passing.

“Our law enforcement put it all on the line every day they go to work, and so do their families. I am proud we will support long-serving law enforcement families through dependent scholar

articles.title: Manufacture Alabama announces 2026 “Coolest Thing Made in Alabama”
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Manufacture Alabama announced Tuesday that the Racing Honda Odyssey by Honda has been named the 2026 “Coolest Thing Made in Alabama” after the statewideManufacturing Madness competitionconcluded.

The winning product was revealed during an awards ceremony held April 7 at the Center for Commerce in Montgomery, where Ellen McNair, secretary of the Alabama Department of Commerce, presented the award.

The event brought together leading manufacturers, elected officials and community partners to cele

articles.title: Rep. Aderholt announces USDA rural development grants for 4th District
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Representative Robert Aderholt, R-Alabama, on Tuesday announced two Community Facilities Grants for Alabama’s Fourth Congressional District through the U.S. Department of Agriculture Rural Development.

The USDA Community Facilities Grants were awarded to Marion and DeKalb counties and will be used to directly benefit public service buildings and improve the quality of life in rural communities, according to Aderholt’s news release.

DeKalb County—Northeast Alabama Community College: $273,188 to

articles.title: US House Democrats call for Congress to come back into session for Iran war debate
articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
Rep. Glenn Ivey, D-Md., leads a press conference at the U.S. Capitol on Tuesday, April 9, 2026, surrounded by House Democrats who were speaking out against the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran. (Photo by Ashley Murray/States Newsroom)

WASHINGTON — House Democrats on Capitol Hill Thursday slammed President Donald Trump’s rhetoric on Iran as “beyond the pale” and urged House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., to reconvene Congress and rein in the president’s war powers.

articles.author: Kelcie Moseley-Morris
articles.body:
The number of births in the United States has continued to slowly decline or remain flat since 2015, and the fertility and birth rates among teenagers continues to fall by much larger margins. (Photo by Rebecca Rivas/Missouri Independent)

New data from the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention shows fertility and birth rates continue to fall across the U.S., while cesarean section procedures increased and preterm birth rates remained flat.

articles.body:
Alabama state Sen. Kirk Hatcher, a Democrat, speaks outside the Alabama State House in March against a Republican-sponsored bill that could allow the state to take control over Montgomery's police department. Sen. Greg Albritton, R-Atmore, walking across the floor of the Alabama Senate on April 1, 2026, in Montgomery, Alabama. The Senate on Wednesday concurred with House changes to the fiscal year 2027 General Fund budget, sending it to Gov. Kay Ivey. (Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector)

The Alabama Senate on Wednesday sent a $3.7 billion 2027 General Fund budget Gov. Kay Ivey.

articles.author: Andrea Tinker
articles.body:
Sen. Arthur Orr, R-Decatur, speaking to senators at the Senate Finance and Taxation Education Committee meeting on April 1, 2026, about the fiscal year 2027 Education Trust Fund budget in the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. (Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector)

articles.body:
Then-Brig. Gen. Jerome Gary Cooper is seen with his daughter Shawn at her graduation from USMC Officer Candidate School at Quantico, Va. in 1985. Gary Cooper, who rose to the rank of major general and died in 2024, fought to create an inclusive military. (Photo courtesy of the Cooper family)

articles.author: Anna Barrett
articles.body:
Sens. April Weaver, R-Hoover, and Jabo Waggoner, R-Vestavia Hills, speaking on the Alabama Senate floor on Feb. 17, 2026, in Montgomery, Alabama. articles.author: Anna Barrett

Rep. Reed Ingram, R-Pike Road, standing in a committee room in the Alabama Statehouse on Feb. 25, 2026, in Montgomery, Alabama. articles.body:
Senate President Pro Tem Garlan Gudger, R-Cullman, speaking at a lecturn on the floor of the Alabama Senate on Feb. 25, 2026, in Montgomery, Alabama. The Alabama Legislature Wednesday gave final approval to a bill making it illegal to tether a dog outside without adequate food or shelter. (Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector)

articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
Armed police patrol as Iranians gather in Tehran's Revolution Square after the United States and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire, on April 8, 2026 in Tehran, Iran. (Photo by Majid Saeedi/Getty Images)

WASHINGTON — The United States and Iran both claimed victory Wednesday, a day after agreeing to a two-week conditional ceasefire, though doubts loomed following continued strikes across the Gulf nations and an indication by Iran that it will continue to control the Strait of Hormuz, a major pa

articles.title: Alabama Legislature votes to extend time crime victims can file claims
articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
Rep. Russell Bedsole, R-Alabaster, stands on the floor of the Alabama House of Representatives on March 19, 2026 at the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. The Legislature Tuesday gave final approval to a bill he filed to extend the deadline for crime victims to apply for aid. (Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector)

The Alabama Legislature Tuesday gave final approval to a bill extending the deadline for crime victims to file a claim with the Alabama Crime Victims Compensation Commission.

HB 255

articles.title: Louisiana judge preserves telehealth abortion access provision for now, puts case on hold
articles.author: Kelcie Moseley-Morris
articles.body:

A federal judge in Louisiana upheld telehealth access to abortion medication in a decision issued Tuesday afternoon, pausing the case until the U.S. Food and Drug Administration completes a safety review of the drug, mifepristone.

Sen. Greg Albritton, R-Atmore, stands in the Alabama Senate on April 2, 2026 at the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. Sen. Andrew Jones, R-Centre, speaking to Sen. Shay Shelnutt, R-Trussville, on the floor of the Alabama Senate on Feb. 17, 2026, in Montgomery, Alabama. (Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector)

articles.author: The Observer Staff
articles.body:
BY NOAH GRIFFITHFOR THE OBSERVER

AUBURN — Auburn University’s wheelchair basketball team captured its first national championship victory Saturday, a milestone more than a decade in the making.Since the program’s inception in 2015, head coach Robb Taylor has built the program from the ground up with a culture based around high character, hard work and great pride. articles.title: First Metro Bank donates $250K to hospital
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Russellville Hospital has received a $250,000 donation from First Metro Bank through a state tax credit program.

“All rural hospitals are struggling,” said Chris Ware, Russellville Hospital chief executive officer. “This is the [Alabama] Legislature’s way to help by allowing this tax credit.”

The contribution was made possible through Alabama’s Rural Hospital Tax Credit Program, which allows businesses and individuals to contribute to eligible rural hospitals in exchange for a d

articles.title: 3 from Vina face drug charge
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
VINA — Three people are facing a drug trafficking charge after members of the Franklin County Sheriff’s Office found 35 grams of crystal and liquid methamphetamines in a shed on the 6400 block of Alabama Highway 24, just north of Vina.

Sheriff’s Sgt. Kyle Palmer said their office received a tip Thursday that Haze Weatherford, 23, was living in the shed and had drugs inside the shed.

After getting a search warrant, members of the sheriff’s office arrived on the scene and found Weatherford and t

articles.title: PC grad had role in Artemis II launch
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — Noah Williams stood in a grassy field at Kennedy Space Center on April 1 about seven miles from the Artemis II launch pad. It was the Phil Campbell High School graduate’s 28th birthday and it was a very special day.

Williams spent a great deal of time in Cape Canaveral, Florida, over the 18 months as he worked on the design and assembly support for the boosters that powered the Artemis II launch. Williams, an engineer, is part of the Booster Integration Team based in Ogden, Utah

articles.title: NWSCC ‘ready to assist’ Navy’s plans
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
MUSCLE SHOALS — U.S. Rep. Robert Aderholt said last week he expects Northwest Shoals Community College to get some of the federal government’s future funding to expand maritime workforce training.

NWSCC was not included in the first round of funding announced March 30 at the National Maritime Museum of the Gulf. Bishop State Community College in Mobile, Coastal Alabama Community College in Bay Minette, Reid State Community College in Evergreen and Lurleen B. Wallace Community College in Opp wer

articles.title: Angler drowns in pond
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — A 63-year-old man was found dead March 30 by a resident of the property and pond the man regularly fished in off Franklin County Road 8.

Allen Ergle, who was buried April 2, was found with a ladder on top of him in the pond where he had been fishing, according to Franklin County Sheriff’s Sgt. Kyle Palmer.

“He was out there fishing and got his line in a tree, and we think he used the ladder to try to untangle his line and lure from the tree,” Palmer said. “We think the ladder s

articles.title: Locals react to US’s 10-day space flight
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Rocky Stone, former Russellville High School principal, called last week’s Artemis II launch a “milestone” in the United States’ space exploration efforts.

“We just launched the most powerful rocket ever built, and it went off flawlessly,” he said.

Stone, who taught astronomy during part of his time at RHS, said the launch rekindled memories of his skipping school to watch launches of the Mercury, Gemini and Apollo eras.

“It really was exciting,” he said of the Artemis II launc

articles.title: Gray hired as UNA director of bands
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
FLORENCE — Joseph Gray has been named the next director of bands for the University of North Alabama.

He will also serve as an associate professor of music.

Provost and Executive Vice President of Academic Affairs Brien Smith touted Gray’s familiarity with UNA’s band program, students and marching band as one of the criteria that made him the right fit for the position.

Gray previously served as associate director of bands at UNA from 2019 to 2025. His specialty is trumpet. He worked as an as

articles.title: Alabama plays part in travels to moon
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Four astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission are on the seventh day of a 10-day test flight around the Moon and back.

That’s something that hasn’t been true in more than 50 years — not since NASA’s last lunar mission, Apollo 17, in December 1972.

At that time, shrinking budgets, declining public support and a lack of a realistic “what next?” led NASA under the Nixon administration to scrap the remaining Apollo missions and move on to the next thing — the space shuttle.

The shuttle program had

articles.title: Protect local deposits which power growth
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Most conversations about new digital payment tools often miss a crucial reality: When money exits community bank deposits, local lending is directly impacted. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent emphasized: “In a fractional banking system, deposits finance lending. When deposits flow out, the ability to have capital to lend is greatly diminished.”

This isn’t just rhetoric from Washington, it’s the financial foundation for every Alabama community. Local deposits transform into credit, fueling the s

articles.title: Meeting highlights service, awards
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Members of the GFWC Book Lovers Study Club reported more than $2,700 was raised for community causes, and the chapter received multiple awards during the GFWC Alabama North District Spring meeting held at Faith Presbyterian Church in Huntsville.

Club membrers joined women from across north Alabama for the meeting, where reports, recognitions and district business were presented.

Guest speaker

Leila Mowery of the St. Jude Clinic in Huntsville about options for families to receive specialized c

articles.title: Waypoint Church hosts Easter egg hunt
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE – Waypoint Church held an Easter event at Sloss Lake Friday afternoon.

The free event included photos with the Easter bunny, music (including children singing on stage), food and inflatables.

Lily Riley reaches for bubbles from a bubble machine.

Rabbits were on hand for those who wanted to pet or watch them. For the children, an Easter egg hunt meant prizes inside eggs. Adults had the opportunity to win door prizes.

Kori Johnson and her husband, Dylan Johnson, are youth leaders

articles.title: Band turns life’s stories into songs
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
For the band OTIS, the road isn’t just for touring and performance. Between shows, in parking lots and back rooms, the band gathers stories from the people they meet, weaving them into a catalog shaped as much by lived experience as by musical influence.

Those moments have become the lifeblood of OTIS’s songwriting.

When the Kentuckybased quartet returns to Muscle Shoals this month for a performance at Champy’s Famous Fried Chicken, they’ll bring with them their collection of stories gathered

articles.title: La Catrina opens in Phil Campbell
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — Downtown Phil Campbell has a new restaurant. La Catrina had its official grand opening last week.

Owner Ernesto Gayton said he chose the location after a friend mentioned to him that downtown Phil Campbell no longer had a restaurant.

Gayton said he has one other restaurant, La Mina in Burnsville, Mississippi, and a food truck, La Cantrina.

“I’m looking forward to serving the community,” he said. “We have a lot of food choices, and I hope everyone will enjoy eating here.”

The

articles.title: Turning her battle in blessings
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
At four years old, Rachael “RayRay” Weir learned a word most adults hope never to hear — leukemia.

Now eight years old and still in treatment, she has turned her fight with acute myeloid leukemia into a ministry — one rooted in faith, generosity and a simple desire to make sure no child feels alone in the middle of a hard battle.

RayRay’s Ray of Faith Foundation began with inspiration from her grandmother, Gina Weir, who has long served in ministry as well and serves as the pastor of Heritage

RUSSELLVILLE – About 200 Franklin County high school seniors took part in an “Adulting 101” event at the Franklin County Career Technical Center in Belgreen.

The program included rotating through interactive sessions covering financial literacy, how to plan travel, networkwing, the pros and cons of renting versus owning a home, and how to read a tape measure.

articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville Lady Golden Tigers spent a portion of spring break participating in the Pleasure Island Showdown tournament held at the Gulf Shores Sportsplex.

RHS (12-13-1) played six games over three days, going 1-4-1 in their outings.

First up was a 3-3 tie with the Chattanooga Christian (Tennessee) Chargers. Russellville’s Khristyan Huerta had a triple and Chloe James added a double in the game. The only RBI for Russellville was by Bri McCulloch.

articles.title: Golden Tigers have won 9 of last 10
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville baseball team has won four consecutive games and nine of their last 10 contests.

The Golden Tigers defeated Scottsboro 6-0 on March 31 in a game held at the University of North Alabama. Ty Engelthaler pitched a complete game shutout recording seven strikeouts. The senior right hander allowed only two hits and issued no walks.

RHS got two hits each by Tristan Ray and Tripp Cleveland. One of Cleveland’s hits was a triple. Houston Askew added a double. RBIs were by Ray, Brayden E

articles.title: Red Bay shuts out Lamar County
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Red Bay Tigers didn’t allow a run while sweeping the Lamar County Bulldogs in an area series. The Tigers beat the Bulldogs 8-0 and 1-0.

In game one, Red Bay broke a scoreless tie in the bottom of the third inning on a two-run homer by Jeremiah Thorn. The Tigers led 3-0 at the end of the third. The Tigers proceeded to score in each of the remaining innings to go on to win 8-0.

Carson McGee allowed just two hits and struck out nine in the win.

Jaxson Swann and Landyn Lewey both doubled.

Softball action was limited during spring break.

Phil Campbell beat Haleyville 9-7 and Tharptown picked up a

16-2 win over Brilliant.

Belgreen traveled to Gulf Shores to compete in the Pleasure Island Showdown tournament held at the Gulf Shores Sportsplex.

Tharptown 16 Brilliant 2

articles.title: PC sweeps Colbert County
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Phil Campbell Bobcats got their area games off to a great start with an easy sweep of the Colbert County Indians. The Bobcats beat the Indians 11-1, 18-3, and 13-0.

In game one, the Bobcats sent ace Isaac Duboise to the mound. Duboise allowed just four hits and one run, struck out eight, and walked just two batters.

articles.title: Lady Hornets upset Selma during senior night
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Senior Night is often defined by sentiment, but for Dallas County, it was defined by a breathtaking comeback.

Trailing by six runs early, the Hornets orchestrated a massive 11-run explosion in the second inning to roar past Selma 13-9 on Monday, April 6. The victory served as the perfect tribute to the four Hornet seniors honored after the game, Kensley Browder, Jaidyn Dixon, Janera Cowan and Na’Kaiya Stubbs.

What looked like a blowout in favor of the Saints quickly transformed into a Hornet h

articles.title: Senators capture another state tennis championship
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY – Propelled by a historic performance from its girls’ roster and a comeback title in the boys’ division, Morgan Academy dominated the 2026 AISA State Tennis Tournament this week at Lagoon Park Tennis Center, officially securing the AISA Girls State Championship.

The victory adds another chapter to one of the most storied coaching tenures in AISA. Under the leadership of Morgan Academy head coach Baxter Stinson, the Senators have now claimed 16 state championships across his 25 years

articles.title: Dallas County recognizes Child Abuse Prevention Month
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The Dallas County System of Services (DCSOS) is proudly recognizes Child Abuse Prevention Month this April with a series of community-focused events, awareness initiatives, and the official launch of its newly redesigned website.

Throughout the month, DCSOS will join organizations across the country in raising awareness about the importance of strengthening families, supporting children, and preventing abuse and neglect through education, engagement, and community partnership.

articles.title: Poll: Carl leads, Marques close behind in AL-1 Republican primary
articles.body:
Alabama’s 1st Congressional District Republican primary is shaping up to be acompetitive racewith less than six weeks until the May 19 election, as former Congressman Jerry Carl and State Rep. Rhett Marques (R-Enterprise) battle for the open seat.

The seat became open when incumbent U.S. Rep. Barry Moore (R-Enterprise) chose to run for U.S. Senate rather than seek reelection. Winning the Republican primary in the heavily red district is tantamount to winning the general election.

articles.title: School prayer, Pledge of Allegiance amendment headed to Alabama voters
articles.body:
The Alabama Senate passed a constitutional amendment Wednesday requiring public schools to recite the Pledge of Allegiance and allowing students to initiate and lead prayer, sending the measure to Alabama voters for final approval.

HB511, sponsored by State Rep. Reed Ingram (R-Pike Road), passed the Senate 30-0 without discussion. The House approved it 94-3 on March 10 after nearly two hours of debate.

Because the measure is a constitutional amendment, it required a 60 percent supermajority in

articles.title: DeBoer says Tide needs to ‘take ownership’ in order to improve run game
articles.body:
TheAlabama Crimson Tide under Kalen DeBoerhasstruggled to run the ball with consistencyover the last two years, but down the stretch of this past season was particularly a struggle for the offense on the ground.

It’s been one of the most widely discussed narrativessurrounding the team this springand looking at their chances this fall.

DeBoer knows he and the staff have to get it fixed if Alabama is going to get back to the top. He was asked about it directly during a recent interview with “The

articles.title: Alabama Legislature passes Beau’s Law, banning unlawful dog tethering statewide
articles.body:
The Alabama Legislature gave final approval Wednesday toSB361, known as Beau’s Law, a bill that bans the unlawful tethering of dogs in Alabama, sending it to Governor Kay Ivey for her signature.

The bill, sponsored by Senate President Pro Tem Garlan Gudger (R-Cullman), makes it illegal to tether a dog to a stationary object, including trees and poles, without providing adequate shelter, food, or water.

Dogs tethered outside must be provided shelter with a roof, walls, and floors; enough space

articles.title: UAB solidifies national leadership in healthcare education with top rankings across multiple programs
articles.body:
TheUniversity of Alabama at Birminghamis strengthening its position as a national leader in healthcare education, with multiple graduate programs earning top rankings in the latestU.S. News & World Report Best Graduate Schools list.

A total of 17 UAB graduate and specialty programs ranked among the top 20 in their respective disciplines, including the university’s Master of Science in Health Administration program, which retained its No. 1 ranking in the nation for Healthcare Management, the Un

articles.title: Caroleene Dobson: ‘We still have a lot of work to do when it comes to election integrity’
articles.body:
Alabama Secretary of State candidate Caroleene Dobson thinks that more work needs to be done to secure elections in the Yellowhammer State.

Dobson, a Montgomery real estate attorney and former Republican nominee for U.S. Congress, discussed an election integrity plan Wednesday on WVNN’s “The Yaffee Program.”

articles.title: Angelo “Doc” Mancuso op-ed: Alabama Republicans are demanding loyalty instead of earning votes
articles.body:
By any measure, Alabama Republicans should be operating from a position of strength. They hold supermajorities in Montgomery, dominate statewide offices, and routinely win congressional seats by margins that would be unthinkable in most of the country.

articles.body:
As the Alabama Legislature enters its final days of the 2026 regular session,budgetleaders say lawmakers are taking a cautious approach—holding spending growth under 1% and prioritizing long-term stability over short-term gains despite strong state revenues.

And despite millions of dollars in outside spending and the weight of Washington influence, it is tightening into a true contest driven by the people of this state.

U.S. Rep.Barry Moore (R-Enterprise) on Tuesday announced a slate of 56 county coordinators spanning Alabama, marking what his campaign describes as a broad grassroots organization in his bid for the U.S. Senate.

The Barry Moore county coordinators list includes representatives from counties stretching from Mobile to the Tennessee Valley, with multiple coordinators in several of the state’s largest counties.

articles.body:
Alabama Extension Week runs from April 12 through April 18 and the Pike County Extension Office is inviting the public to engage with its employees during the celebration.

Pike County Extension Director Abby Peters is encouraging Pike County residents to stop in and speak with its staff.

articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Troy’s UPS Store has moved to a new location in the Southland Village Shopping Center.

After years at its S. Brundidge Street location, the UPS Store has moved into the location of the former Cool Vapors store. UPS Store owner Andrew Reeves said a big part of the reasoning for the move was the potential for more foot traffic.

“Where we were (on S. Brundidge Street) most of the businesses over there were just office buildings or appointment-only offices,” he said. “We weren’t really getting any

articles.title: Troy non-profit AAI hosting free community self-defense event
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Troy-based Amplified Advocacy Initiative (AAI) will be hosting a free community-self defense event in Troy on April 12 in recognition of Child Abuse Prevention Month and Sexual Assault Awareness Month.

The free public event is designed for participating ages four and up and will feature four one-hour breakout sessions tailored by age group. The goal for the event is to provide practical, age-appropriate self-defense instruction, while also creating a space for education, awareness and confidenc

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs two bills aimed at strengthening public safety
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey signed two bills into law on Thursday aimed at strengthening public safety as she signed both House Bill 98 and Senate Bill 233.

HB 98, also known as the Alabama Law Enforcement Officers’ Family Scholarship Act, establishes a scholarship program for the dependents of law enforcement officers in Alabama. The law is designed to provide tuition help to the spouses and children of long-serving law enforcement officers.

The bill provides up to $3,000 in tuition assistance per

articles.title: Troy Softball sweeps West Georgia in midweek doubleheader
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans (25-14) swept the West Georgia Wolves (11-30) in a midweek non-conference softball doubleheader on Wednesday.

Troy started the day off with a 2-0 win in game one as pitcher Esmee Ames (4-1) pitched a full-game shutout, striking out eight batters and giving up just one hit with no runs to earn her fourth win in the circle this season. At the plate, Megan Loftis was the only player for either team to collect multiple hits along with an RBI. Kalee Rochinski and Kiley Huffman score

articles.title: Local Softball: Pike Lib again lands at No. 4 in Top 10, Zion Chapel tops Goshen
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Pike Liberal Arts Lady Patriots landed at No. 4 on the ASWA Class 2A Softball Top 10 this week after throttling Luverne on April 7.

The Lady Patriots moved to 13-4-2 on the season after a 19-3 win. Cam Brown had a big day offensively for Pike, hitting a grand slam home run with six RBIs and one run. Mary Holland Stephens also went 3-for-4 at the plate with a double, two RBIs and two runs. Sarah Strickland also went 2-for-4 with two runs and a double. Madison Nelson stole three bases along w

articles.title: Goshen, Ariton, Pike County and Charles Henderson notch dominant baseball wins
articles.body:
All of the baseball teams in The Messenger’s coverage area have picked up wins on the diamond, including Pike Liberal Arts and Ariton High School who have made the ASWA Class 2A Top 10 again.

This week, Ariton landed at No. 3 in the Top 10 after surpassing 20 wins on the season. The Purple Cats (20-5) have won six straight games after dominant victories this week over Trinity Presbyterian and Providence Christian. On April 5, Ariton beat Trinity 13-3.

Wells Ganey led Ariton at the plate, going

articles.title: Troy Baseball run-rules Florida A&M
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans (16-17) picked up a non-conference win at Riddle-Pace Field on Wednesday with a 12-2 seven-inning victory over the Florida A&M Rattlers (15-15).

Troy earned nine hits as a team and also walked 12 times with an additional three free bases on HBPs. Troy native Drew Nelson earned his first start on the mound this season, striking out three batters and giving up no hits or runs in his three innings. Nelson also showed his two-way ability by driving in three runs at the plate.

“Any

articles.title: Troy Regional CEO Rick Smith receives national leadership in healthcare award
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Recently, Troy Regional Medical Center CEO Rick Smith was presented with The Baldrige Foundation Award for Leadership in Healthcare.

The Foundation for the Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award exists to strengthen the competitiveness of organizations and improve community well-being through performance excellence. The Foundation Leadership Award for Excellence was established to recognize leaders in business, nonprofit, government, healthcare, education, cybersecurity and community who provi

articles.title: Memories from the Past Cruise-in & Quilt Show Saturday in Ramer
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
Every now and then, there’s a Cruise-In that’s a car show and a good bit more.

That’s what visitors round about Ramer, will appreciate and enjoy at the “Memories from the Past Cruise-in & Quilt Show” from 11 a.m. until 2 p.m. on April 11, with an April 19 rain date.

The entry fee is free.

The men can enjoy the Cruise-In while the women will be “hanging” around the Quilt Show. That’s not to say there won’t be a lot of going back-and- forth between the two.

Those who plan to enter the event sh

articles.title: Open Mic Night is Friday at The Studio
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
An invitation to all vocalists, instrumentalists, small ensembles, poets, storytellers, comedians, jugglers, puppeteers, actors, writers — whatever the talent, are invited to share at Open Mic Night on Friday from 6:30 until 8 p.m. at The Studio.

Those who want to “mike-up” should get to The Studio between 6 and 6:15 p.m. to be on the list to perform. The show starts at 6:30 p.m. All performances must be family-friendly and no longer than 10 minutes.

Scott Michaels and Maggie Ford will host Op

articles.title: Simmons signs cheer scholarship with Troy University
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
Pike Liberal Arts School senior Kinsley Simmons signed a cheer scholarship with Troy University on Wednesday morning.

Simmons was the first student at PLAS that was recruited and offered a cheerleading scholarship by any university. Simmons was a member of PLAS’s two-time state championship and national championship winning cheer team.

“I’m really so excited about signing with Troy University,” Simmons said. “It’s an honor to be chosen. I can’t wait for the fall. It’s going to be a big change

articles.title: National gas price average climbs above $4
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
This month, the national average for a gallon of gas was more than $4 per gallon for the first time since August 2022.

On Wednesday, the national average climbed to $4.16 per gallon, which was a dime higher than last week, 71-cents higher than a month ago and 92-cents higher than last year.

According to AAA, the increase in the past month is due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East which caused crude oil prices to surge past $100 per gallon. According to AAA, the increase in crude oil pr

articles.title: USDA designates Pike County, 29 others primary natural natural disaster areas
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On Tuesday, Alabama Commissioner of Agriculture and Industries Rick Pate announced that the USDA has designated 30 Alabama counties, including Pike County, as primary natural disaster areas due to recent severe drought conditions.

The counties considered natural disaster areas due to drought include Pike, Barbour, Bullock, Butler, Chambers, Choctaw, Clarke, Coffee, Conecuh, Covington, Crenshaw, Dale, Elmore, Escambia, Geneva, Greene, Hale, Henry, Houston, Lee, Lowndes, Macon, Marengo, Monroe, M

articles.title: Birmingham’s Walk At Lunch Day Is Back, Residents Encouraged to Get Moving
articles.body:
In February 2025, Teri Sewell made a commitment to move more.

Instead of just pulling things from the printer at her desk, she took extra steps to walk to a printer in another location at her office. She cut out sugar. And when officials at her office at Legion Field started a walking wellnes program, she looked into that, too.

As the weeks passed, she shifted from a sedentary lifestyle to one filled with more steps, more water and better eating habits. She also didn’t eat anything past 8 p.m.

articles.title: One Night Only: Elias Hendricks Brings ‘Classical Soul by Candlelight’ Home to Birmingham
articles.body:
By Sym Posey |The Birmingham Times

By all measures, Elias Hendricks has spent a lifetime preparing for this moment. On April 25, the Birmingham-born operatic tenor and creative producer will premiereClassical Soul by Candlelight, a one-night-only event blending opera and soul music in an immersive, black-tie setting at the historic Harambe Room.

The performance marks Hendricks’ first solo debut of the project in his hometown, even as the show itself has already traveled the world. “This is jus

articles.title: New Alabama Law Targets Birmingham’s Vacant Properties with Registry, Fees
articles.body:
A new law passed by the Alabama Legislature this week will require owners of vacant properties in Birmingham to register, maintain, and pay fees for buildings sitting empty for more than three months.

The legislation creates a vacant property registry aimed at holding property owners accountable and speeding up the process for addressing dilapidated buildings across the city.

Under the law, the registration fee is $250 with a 150% increase per year, capping at $1,000.

“There are parts of the

articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Jonah Inman and Knox Mauldin each homered twice, Max Carver and Griffin Wright each pitched gems, and Cullman picked up a road sweep of Jasper on Thursday.

articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
The Cullman Veterans Hall of Fame is seeking nominees to welcome into its 2026 class.

The nomination deadline is Thursday, July 16, 2026. Anyone can make a nomination and organizers note that no candidate should be reluctant to be considered just because their military service might not have included a combat tour.

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Cullman County Child Development Center (CDC) Parent Teacher Organization (PTO) will host its fourth annual Cullman Family Expo this Saturday, April 11, from 9 a.m.- 4 p.m. at the Cullman County Agricultural Trade Center.

Expo Chair Jennifer Hodges said this is a free community event that is rain or shine and a celebration of family and community.

“Admission is completely free, and the event is open to all families and individuals in the Cullman area and beyond,” Hodges said

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The annual Cullman Regional Foundation Gala will take place on Saturday, April 25, at 5:30 p.m. at The Venue at Cotton Creek.

Gala XXV’s theme is Silver Jubilee: 25 Years of Impact.  The evening will feature a lovely dinner and special program, along with a silent and live auction.

articles.title: Obituary: Nancy Carol Ergle Hudgins
articles.body:
Funeral service forNancy Carol Ergle Hudgins, 72, of Cullman will be at 2 p.m., Monday, April 13, 2026, at Cullman Funeral Home Chapel with interment in Bethlehem Cemetery, Rev. Steve Rodgers officiating.  The family will receive friends from noon-2 p.m.

Cullman Funeral Home is honored to serve the family.

articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Luverne Public Library recently held their annual dinner theater production, one of the library’s largest yearly fundraisers. This year’s performance, "Steel Magnolias" was held on March 24 at the Luverne United Methodist Church DEI Center with a preview presentation on March 23 to accommodate patrons of the sell-out show.

More than $1.5 billion in taxes—including a grocery tax with no end in sight—remained on the books. articles.title: Casey secures child predator conviction amid AG campaign
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
A Blount County jury found Johnny Chandler, 57, guilty on multiple charges in the 2024 abduction and assault of a 10-year-old girl, delivering a major conviction for District Attorney Pamala Casey as she campaigns for Alabama attorney general.

Chandler was convicted of first-degree rape, first-degree kidnapping, two counts of sexual abuse of a child younger than 12, first-degree unlawful imprisonment and obstructing governmental operations.

The child was reported missing in the early morning h

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs Alabama law enforcement safety, scholarship bills
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Thursday signed House Bill 98, establishing a scholarship program for dependents of law enforcement officers. She also signed Senate Bill 233, which increases penalties for people who attempt to elude law enforcement, often creating dangerous situations for officers and the public.

Ivey said public safety is paramount. During her State of the State address this year, she outlined SB233 as a priority for the 2026 Legislative Session.

“Too often when criminals attempt to fle

articles.title: Alabama lawmakers pass farm utility tax relief bill
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Alabama Legislature on Thursday gave final passage to Senate Bill 159 by Senator Jack Williams, R-Wilmer, and Representative Danny Crawford, R-Athens.

Senate Bill 159 exempts certain agricultural operations from Alabama’s utility gross receipts and utility service use taxes. The bill removes taxes on natural gas and electricity used in commercial aquaculture aeration systems, greenhouses, pivot irrigation systems and poultry houses. The exemption will take effect September 1, 2026.

Crawfor

articles.title: CK Cattle named Alabama’s Farm of Distinction
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
CK Cattle in Lowndes County was named Alabama’s 2026 Farm of Distinction during the Alabama Farm-City Awards Luncheon in Auburn on April 9 for its commitment to stewardship, support of young farmers and community impact.

Chuck and Katie Madaris manage the three-generation cattle operation in Hope Hull alongside their family. CK Cattle produces Angus, Chiangus and SimAngus seedstock cattle, as well as commercial cattle.

As Farm of Distinction winners, the Madaris family will receive an engraved

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs Alabama’s General Fund, Education Trust Fund bills
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Thursday signed the General Fund and Education Trust Fund bills.

Ivey said her administration has worked to remain fiscally conservative while making investments to benefit Alabamians.

“Throughout my tenure as governor and even before, we have worked hard to be fiscally conservative in our budgeting, making meaningful investments to benefit the people of Alabama,” Ivey said.

Ivey said the 2026 Legislative Session marked her 10th session as governor and noted Alabama has n

articles.author: Anna Barrett, Andrea Tinker
articles.body:
Republican members of the Alabama Senate gathered in the corner of the Senate chambers on April 9, 2026, in the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. Thursday was the final day of the 2026 Regular Session, and the upper chamber had many controversial bills on its agenda including a bill to close the state's primary elections, which had Republicans divided. (Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector)

The Alabama House of Representatives Thursday passed a bill requiring school districts to display the Ten Commandments in various locations in schools.

An election worker hands out “I Voted” stickers at the Main Library in Salt Lake City on Election Day, Tuesday, Nov. 5, 2024. (Photo by Spenser Heaps for Utah News Dispatch)

Millions of women could face new challenges to voting under President Donald Trump’s SAVE America Act, which would require voters to prove their citizenship before casting a ballot.

articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
First lady Melania Trump makes a brief statement to deny any connection with late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein on April 9, 2026. (Image via White House livestream)

WASHINGTON — First lady Melania Trump said Thursday she was “never involved in any capacity” with the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and that “baseless lies” about her are being circulated.
