articles.title: CRK wildlife cruise coming to West Point Lake
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Chattahoochee Riverkeeper invites the community to an upcoming West Point Lake Wildlife Cruise to learn more about the local ecosystem that calls West Point Lake and the Chattahoochee River home. The wildlife cruise will be held on Saturday, April 18, from 9 a.m. to 11 a.m. and will depart from Pyne Road Park, located at 4481 Roanoke Rd., LaGrange.

The cruise will take attendees aboard the West Point Lake Floating Classroom (WPLFC), which uses hands-on lessons to teach the planet's future stewa

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Lynn Gendusa: The War Within America
articles.author: Lynn Gendusa
articles.body:
Wars rage across the world, but our most important battle may be right here at home, not on foreign soil – but within our own hearts, our institutions, and our communities.

Are we at war with ourselves? Has the pursuit of power and profit begun to overshadow the values that once held us together?

History reminds us that great civilizations often decline not just from external threats, but from within – through division, excess, and a loss of humility and shared purpose. If a nation focuses mor

articles.title: Residents invited to meet candidates
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The Greater Valley Area Chamber of Commerce (GVACC) is hosting a candidate meet-and-greet on April 30, ahead of the primaries on May 19. The event is designed to give residents direct access to the candidates without the constraints of traditional debates.

According to GVACC Director Carrie Wood, multiple candidates across both parties and several offices have been invited to participate. Wood listed candidates from Alabama House District 37, two District 5 candidates running for the Chambers C

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Andrah P'Lonas: AI in the Classroom: Why Schools Should Provide Every Student with a Digital Tutor
articles.author: Andrah P'Lonas
articles.body:
Across the country—and right here in our local schools—education is facing a new reality. Artificial intelligence is no longer a future concept. It is already here, already being used by students, and already changing how learning happens.

The question is no longer whether students will use AI. The real question is: Will schools guide that use—or ignore it?

For years, schools have provided students with structured learning tools such as textbooks, online portals, digital libraries, and platfor

articles.title: Commission gives cleanup updates, honors Valley Haven
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body: During the comments from elected officials at Monday’s county commission meeting, Commissioner Sam Bradford reminded the public of the commission's annual recycling effort to collect household items that may require specific disposal, preventing citizens from simply disposing of them in household trash. Bradford announced that the City of Valley has allowed the county commission to participate alongside them, only requiring minor expenses for supplies and labor. The recycling event began Monday,

articles.title: Eugene "Gene" Hendrick, Jr. - Obituary
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Eugene "Gene" Hendrick Jr, 81, a resident of Brantley, passed away unexpectedly at his home on Sunday, April 12. Funeral services for Gene will be held at 11 a.m. on Thursday, April 16, from the Chapel of Turners Funeral Home with Rev. Rocky Pierce officiating. Interment will follow in Bullock Friendship Cemetery with Turners Funeral Home of Luverne directing the services.

Gene was a born artist with a gift for creativity in so many different formats. He developed his own sign painting business

articles.title: Michael Anthony Jones - Obituary
articles.body:
Michael Anthony Jones, 78, a resident of Luverne, passed away peacefully in his home on Saturday, April 11, while surrounded by his loving family. A graveside service for Mike was held at 2 p.m. on Monday, April 13, in Luverne Emmaus Cemetery with Brother Clay Crum officiating, Doug Brown and Tom Perdue delivering eulogies and Turners Funeral Home of Luverne directing the service. One of Mike’s final requests was for everyone to come “just as you are.”

Mike, better known as Milso to his friends

articles.title: USDA designates primary natural disaster areas
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Alabama Commissioner of Agriculture and Industries Rick Pate announced that USDA Secretary Brooke Rollins has designated a total of 30 Alabama counties as primary natural disaster areas due to recent drought. Twelve additional counties were listed as contiguous disaster counties.

“These drought conditions are placing significant stress on Alabama farmers and ranchers, impacting crops, pastures and livestock across much of our state. We strongly encourage producers to contact their local USDA of

articles.title: This week in history: 2013
articles.author: Amy Lewis
articles.body:
A look back at pages from The Luverne Journal this week in 2013 reflects a community balancing student achievement in the arts with competitive momentum on the baseball field. The stories this week include a play by the Luverne Drama Club and an update on high school baseball.

Drama club prepares for first production

The Luverne Drama Club was putting the finishing touches on its first-ever major performance, “Hank Morgan: A Day in the Life,” scheduled for April 21 in the Luverne auditorium. T

articles.title: Pate attends 'A Great American Agriculture Celebration'
articles.author: Laura Jean Mitchell | SPECIAL TO THE LUVERNE JOURNAL
articles.body:
Alabama Department of Agriculture and Industries (ADAI) Commissioner Rick Pate recently attended the “A Great American Agriculture Celebration” held on the South Lawn of the White House. He joined agricultural leaders, President Donald Trump and USDA Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins, farmers and industry representatives from across the country to recognize the vital role agriculture plays in feeding and fueling America.

“I was honored to attend the Great American Agriculture Celebration

articles.title: Rep. Lomax contrasts tax cuts with Mo Brooks’ legislative record
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
Incumbent state Representative James Lomax, R-Lomax, is drawing a sharper line against challenger Mo Brooks, framing the Republican primary as a test of competing tax records—and the consistency behind them.

In a press release issued on Tax Day, Lomax positioned the race as a clear contrast between his recent support for tax cuts and Brooks’ earlier legislative history.

“On Tax Day, the contrast couldn’t be clearer,” Lomax said. “I am proud to fight every single day to cut taxes and keep more

articles.title: Gov. Ivey showcases healthcare legislation signed into law
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
Alabama Governor Kay Ivey highlighted a slate of healthcare legislation from the 2026 legislative session on Wednesday that she has signed into law.

Ivey highlighted nine pieces of legislation aimed at improving healthcare access and delivery, which were passed during the 2026 legislative session, which ended last week.

The governor described the newly enacted measures, which range from Emergency Medical Services reforms to an attempt to restrict items eligible to be purchased with Supplementa

articles.title: Alabama sets June execution date for Jeffery Lee
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
Alabama Governor Kay Ivey has formally scheduled the execution of Jeffery Lee, a death row inmate convicted for a 1998 double murder during a pawnshop robbery in Orrville.

The execution is set to take place during a 30-hour window beginning at midnight on June 11, 2026, and ending early June 12.

Lee was convicted in the December 12, 1998 killings of Jimmy Ellis and Elaine Thompson at Jimmy’s Pawnshop in Dallas County. Prosecutors said Lee shot and killed both victims during the robbery.

A thi

articles.title: Alabama Cannabis Coalition reflects on mixed results from legislative session
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
TheAlabama Cannabis Coalitionsaid there were mixed results during the 2026 Legislative Session, which saw several pieces of cannabis-related legislation fail to pass into law. While some bills would have further restricted the sale and use of hemp-derived products in the state, others could have rolled back the criminalization of cannabis in Alabama.

“The 2026 session made one thing clear—Alabama is still having the conversation, but we are not yet taking action,” the organization said in a pre

articles.title: ACLU of Alabama applauds parole reforms, but warns Alabama’s prison crisis remains unaddressed
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The ACLU of Alabama on Wednesday praisedAlabama lawmakers for approving “much-needed legislation to improve the pardons and parole process” during the 2026 Legislative Session, but advocates caution that lawmakers stopped short of implementing the reforms needed to significantly address the state prison system’s flaws.

TheACLU of Alabamaapplauded the passage of these bills while warning that lawmakers “did little to address the systemic failures driving overcrowding and violence in Alabama’s pr

articles.title: Energy Institute of Alabama celebrates Lineman Appreciation Day
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Energy Institute of Alabama, or EIA, on Wednesday recognized the state’s lineworkers during a Lineman Appreciation Day event at Dixie Electric Cooperative in Montgomery.

When weather affects Alabama, lineworkers are often the first to respond to restore power to affected communities—replacing damaged poles, repairing downed lines and clearing the way for others to assist.

“Most of us don’t realize how hard of a job it is to keep the lights on,” EIA President and CEO Clay Scofield said. “Bu

articles.title: Innovate Alabama launches $15M fund to retain, scale early-stage companies
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Innovate Alabama on Wednesday announced the launch of theCapital Access Fund, a $15 million initiative designed to help retain and grow Alabama’s early-stage companies while unlocking private investment.

The fund will make investments typically ranging from $100,000 to $250,000 per company to help founders bridge the gap from pre-seed to Series A funding. It requires at least a 1-to-1 private match from corporations, individuals or venture firms. The fund also will reinvest returns to support i

articles.title: Opinion | What a remarkable time to be alive
articles.author: Tom Greene
articles.body:
Despite all the chaos coming out of Washington, D.C. these days, this is a remarkable time to be alive.

Imagine you just woke up from a coma. The last memory you have is from 1981. The Iranian hostages were just being released after 444 days in captivity. You also vaguely remember that NASA was about to launch something called the Space Shuttle. In the first hour, you try to catch up on the news. There’s a lot to take in, but you learn that astronauts have just traveled 252,756 miles to reach t

articles.title: Opinion | Is religious liberty still for all?
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
There was a time in American politics when attacking someone’s religion was understood to be disqualifying. Americans may not have always agreed on faith. But the standard existed because they agreed on something more fundamental—that belief itself was not a political weapon.

That boundary is now giving way.

What began as broad, often careless political attacks on Islam—usually dressed up in the language of security or cultural concern—has expanded into something far more revealing. When rheto

articles.title: Protestors call for firing of killer corrections officer
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
Alabamians gathered Tuesday outside St. Clair Correctional Facility to demand the immediate termination of Lieutenant Roderick Gadson—a senior corrections officer exposed as a killer in the HBO documentary “The Alabama Solution.” They also demanded accountability for all other officers who have committed abuse inside Alabama prisons.

During the demonstration, which took place along the public roadway near the facility entrance, affected families and community members called attention to a patte

articles.title: Former International Motorsports Hall of Fame bookkeeper indicted
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall announced last week that a Talladega County grand jury has indicted a former bookkeeper for the International Motorsports Hall of Fame, accusing her of misusing funds tied to the state-connected institution.

The woman charged, Karen Lakey, 56, of Munford, is accused of using her position for personal financial gain. Prosecutors allege she improperly obtained $159,477.55 from the Hall of Fame while overseeing its books, directing the money to herself or to

Montgomery Mayor Steven Reed and Montgomery Police Chief James N. Graboys giving press an update on the Oct. 4 mass shooting on Oct. 20, 2025, at the Montgomery Police Department in Montgomery, Alabama. articles.body:
Gov. Kay Ivey Wednesday set a June execution date for Jeffrey Lee, 50, convicted of the 1998 murders of Jimmy Ellis and Elaine Thompson. (Alabama Department of Corrections)

Gov. Kay Ivey Wednesday said she had scheduled a June 11 nitrogen gas execution for Jeffrey Lee, 50, convicted of the murders of Jimmy Ellis and Elaine Thompson in 1998.

articles.body:

LOACHAPOKA — A Lee County Sherrif’s deputy is in stable condition following a shooting near Loachapoka. Lee County Sheriff Jay Jones confirmed the deputy is being transferred to UAB via helicopter for further evaluation by a specialist. Jones said the deputy is in stable condition at this time.

articles.title: Property rezoning clears way for retail building
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The Russellville City Council approved the rezoning of a Lawrence Street property during its April 6 meeting.

The council voted to rezone property at 1393 Lawrence Street East from M-2 industrial to B-2 general business, allowing for retail use.

City clerk Belinda Miller said one of the property owners is Juan Garcia.

Building Inspector Bill Fuller said the change was requested to allow for construction of a building with multiple retail suites.

The site is located near a head

articles.title: Drone contraband is becoming a problem
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Area law enforcement officials say they support the idea of more authority to stop drones from delivering contraband into jails. Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall recently joined attorneys general from 20 other states in asking the federal government to allow state and local officers to intercept or disable drones near correctional facilities.

The request comes as drones are being used in some parts of the country to drop drugs, weapons and cell phones into prisons. Current

articles.title: Oliver: Too many children are being abused
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Franklin County deputies investigated 85 cases involving child and sexual abuse in 2025.

“For a county the size of Franklin County, that is way too many,” Sheriff Shannon Oliver said. “Even one case is way too many.”

Some of those cases resulted in children being placed in foster care. Currently, there are 67 children living in foster care in Franklin County. Oliver said those cases involved sexual abuse, physical abuse, neglect, and chemical endangerment.

The Russellville Poli

articles.title: Sentencing delayed again in manslaughter trial
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Brandy Dowdy will have to wait even longer to learn how long she will serve in prison after her sentencing was delayed for the second time.

Franklin County District Judge Brian Hamilton on Friday granted a motion from Dowdy’s attorney to continue her sentencing beyond its initial April 28 scheduling.

Dowdy, 42, was charged with two counts of manslaughter in the March 2022 deaths of Michelle Ann Sheeks and Jacquline Summer Beard, an employee with the Alabama Department of Public

articles.title: Disruption of water service is expected Thursday
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — Water service for customers in areas along College Road is expected to be disrupted Thursday as crews install a new meter and vault for Franklin County Water.

The work is scheduled between approximately 9 a.m. and 3:30 p.m., although Water Superintendent Jordan Steele said the outage may not last that long.

Steele said the project is part of routine system maintenance and involves installing an updated meter.

“At some point, the water will be shut off,” Steele said. “It’s not

articles.title: Garden club hosts plant, bake sale
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RED BAY — The Red Bay Garden Club held its annual plant and bake sale Saturday at the high school greenhouse to raise funds for projects across the city.

“We put everything back into the City of Red Bay to beautify our community,” club member Tracie Clark said.

She said the club uses those funds to maintain the Hoyt Keeton Walking Trail, care for flower beds, and support landscaping efforts in public spaces.

Members plant and maintain flowers throughout Red Bay, including downtown planters, B

articles.title: Obituaries
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Lynda Carol MayfieldApril 6, 2026

Lynda Carol Mayfield, 81, of Russellville, passed away April 6 at her home.

She was born on January 12, 1945, to Jack and Marie Porter. Lynda loved spending time with her family and cherished every moment with those she loved.

She worked at Wagon Wheel in Russellville for over 40 years as a fashion stylist. Lynda took great pride in caring for her clients and helping them plan their outfits for special occasions.

She was active in the study groups at First M

articles.title: Has the city on a hill lost its shine?
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Ronald Reagan used the “Shining City on a Hill” as a metaphor for the United States as a beacon for freedom and democracy in the world. Joe Biden often spoke of the soul of America being under threat, that our belief in fairness and unalienable rights had at times fallen short, but until now had never been in doubt.

Are we still the shining city, and is America’s soul still intact? Two personal stories shed some light on this.

The Florence-Lauderdale County Alabama Public Library

Thanks to a

articles.title: Delta Kappa Gamma learns gardening tips
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Our April meeting of Delta Kappa Gamma at Calvary Baptist Church in Russellville featured a lively and practical program by Trace Barnett, a native of Brilliant who is known for his work in food, entertaining and design.

Barnett, who holds a degree in food and nutrition from the University of Alabama, shared ideas from his work as an event planner, blogger and cooking instructor.

He spoke about making cooking, entertaining, and gardening feel manageable instead of overwhelming.

Drawing on his

articles.title: One abused child is one too many
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Sheriff Shannon Oliver is right.

Even one abused child in Franklin County — or anywhere in Alabama — is one too many. And yet in 2025, Oliver’s deputies investigated 85 cases involving child and sexual abuse.

“For a county the size of Franklin County, that is way too many,” said Oliver.

He’s right. It’s a sobering statistic that should rattle every resident of our county. Even more bothersome is the fact that already in 2026, the Russellville Police Department has made three arrests related t

articles.title: TVA president, CEO announces retirement
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Less than a year after he was named president and CEO of the Tennessee Valley Authority, Don Moul told members of the board of directors he will be retiring effective July 1.

Moul’s announcement also comes less than a month after President Donald Trump sent a memorandum to TVA Board of Directors instructing the board to establish a maximum total annual compensation limit of $500,000 for all of its employees.

Moul reportedly made $5.7 million in 2025 to lead the country’s largest public utility

articles.title: Students’ art selected for State Capitol exhibit
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The art of three Russellville Elementary School students is on display at the Alabama State Capitol through April 28.

Khloe Ball, a fourth grader, and fifth graders Charlee Knight and Lindsay Rush participated in a new statewide art program for students in grades 3-5 presented by the Alabama State Council on the Arts.

Annie Willis, who teaches art at Russellville Elementary, said it was exciting to see the students’ work recognized at the state level.

“Several RES students comp

articles.title: Vina wins county track meet for fourth year
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Vina Lady Red Devils won the 2026 Franklin County track championship. It was the school’s four straight county track championship.

Sophomore Samantha Elders placed first in 110meter hurdles, tied second in high jump and placed third in long jump.

Sophomore Olivia Crum placed first in triple jump, first in 300-meter hurdles and third in 100 meters.

Junior Kenzie Elrod placed second in 100 meters, third in110 meters hurdles and fourth in 4×100 relay.

Junior Lana Bell placed first in discus

articles.title: Phil Campbell, Tharptown get softball wins
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Early in the week, Phil Campbell and Red Bay battled it out in a pre-Franklin County Tournament contest with the Bobcats coming out on top 9-6.

The game was tied 2-2 after one inning, but the Bobcats scored on one in the bottom of the second to take a 3-1 lead. Both teams went scoreless through the next two innings before an explosion of runs in the fifth.

Red Bay scored four in the top half of the inning to take a 6-3 lead. Phil Campbell responded in the bottom half of the inning with six run

articles.title: Comedian shares tailor-made laughs
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
His voice has made him one of the most watched, respected, and consistently viral comedians in the world.

For Ali Siddiq, comedy isn’t just about landing a punchline. It’s also about connecting with his audiences through stories that people can recognize themselves being in.

While it’s a craft he began perfecting around the age of 19 from the confines of a jail cell, his comedic journey — and the desire to tell jokes grounded in his personal truth — began much sooner from the sofa in his paren

articles.title: They’re still in the game in their 80s
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
On a bright morning at the Cypress Lakes Tennis Courts in Muscle Shoals, Mary McCoy and Ann Culberson ready for their first serve. The two women in their 80s may make light fun of practice, but by every measure they are still very much in the game.

This weekend, the pair will travel to Tuscaloosa to compete in a state qualifier for the Senior Olympics with hopes of advancing to the 2027 national games. If you ask them, the journey there matters just as much as the outcome.

“I picked up the rac

articles.title: RHS sweeps Lawrence County; 13 of last 14 games are wins
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville baseball team remained undefeated in the area with a sweep of the Lawrence County Red Devils. The Golden Tigers have won 13 of their last 14 games and are now 16-13 for the season.

The Golden Tigers dominated Lawrence County with a 25-1 win on April 7. Russellville scored six runs in the second inning, nine in the third and 10 in the fourth.

Brayden Entrekin hit two home runs and Bryson Cooper also homered. Russellville got two doubles from Tripp Cleveland and one each by Tris

articles.title: Tharptown topples Red Bay to win county tourney
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RED BAY – In a weekend defined by grit and late-inning heroics, the Tharptown Wildcats weathered a resilient Red Bay comeback to capture the Franklin County softball tournament championship.

The Wildcats went 4-1 over the two-day stretch, ultimately defeating the host Tigers twice to secure the title.

The Wildcats’ road to the trophy began Friday with a narrow 5-4 victory over Phil Campbell. Tharptown jumped out to a commanding 4-0 lead by the middle of the third, fueled by Paisleigh Carr, who

articles.title: PC sweeps Sheffield; Red Bay wins 3
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Phil Campbell Bobcats swept the Sheffield Bulldogs last week to improve to 4-0 in area play.

The Bobcats topped Sheffield 10-1 in game one and 13-3 in game two on Senior Night.

Hagen Raper had a double, triple, two RBIs and scored twice to lead the Bobcats at the plate. Isaac Duboise had two hits and three RBIs.

Chandler Benford doubled in a run and scored twice. Shooter Motes had an RBI triple. Miles Duboise had two hits and scored a run. Bentley Morgan had a single, double, two RBIs and

articles.title: RHS softball goes 4-4 for the week
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville softball team had a busy week winning four of eight games, including a trip to Cullman for the two-day Bearcat Bash.

The Lady Golden Tigers visited Belgreen on April 6 and got a 7-5 win over the Lady Bulldogs.

Alexia Groce hit a home run for Russellville while Bri McCulloch, Chloe James and Khristyan Huerta collected doubles. Groce and Huerta led the team with two RBIs each.

Kaylee Suggs pitched six innings for RHS and Taitum Bishop was in the circle for the last inning.

Rus

articles.title: Faye Smith Stone
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body: Faye Smith Stone, 87, of Linden, Alabama went to her eternal home on Sunday, December 28, 2025. She passed peacefully at home surrounded by family. She was born November 7, 1938 in Stanton, Alabama to Newton Allen Smith and Myrtle Clarice Wells Smith. Upon moving to Linden in 1971, she worked beside her husband, Johnny Stone, at the Linden Dairy Queen. Together, they built a legacy that would remain forever in the hearts of the community. Her greatest love was that of her family. She was a devot

articles.title: Lady Senators fall short in three-game series against Southern Academy
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Morgan Academy honored its senior class with a thrilling opening victory Monday, April 13, but the Senators ultimately fell short of their postseason seeding goals after dropping a road doubleheader to Southern Academy on Tuesday, April 14.

The three-game set served as a critical point for the Senators’ season. Entering the week, Morgan Academy needed to secure two wins in the three-game series to clinch the second seed for the upcoming area tournament.

The week began with a celebration of lea

articles.title: Morgan suffers three-game shutout to Southern
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Despite a challenging three-game series against Southern Academy, the Morgan Academy Senators have officially secured the second seed for the upcoming region tournament.

The Senators dropped all three contests to a powerhouse Southern squad, but their performance throughout the season earned them the necessary standing to move forward in the postseason.

The series opened Monday, April 13, at home with an emotional Senior Night ceremony. The program honored 11 seniors, Alex Johnson, Travis Jone

articles.title: Barry Moore ‘cautiously optimistic’ Congress can pass ICE funding through reconciliation
articles.body:
U.S. Rep. Barry Moore (R-Enterprise) remains hopeful that Republicans in Congress can pass several of President Donald Trump’s priorities through a reconciliation package.

Republican leaders have hinted in recent days that they plan on trying to pass funding for Department for Homeland Security (DHS), more money for Immigrations and Customs Enforcement (ICE), and pass the Save America Act through a reconciliation bill.

The reconciliation process would allow the legislation to pass without havi

articles.body:
U.S. Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-Auburn) has had enough of the crime problem in Alabama’s Capital city and wants to be the state’s next governor in order to clean it up.

The senator, who is currently the leading candidate in Alabama’s gubernatorial race, discussed the issue Wednesday on “The Rightside” with Allison Sinclair and Amie Beth Shaver, presented by Yellowhammer News.

articles.title: GOP delegation: Alabamians are seeing savings on Tax Day
articles.body:
Members of Alabama’s Republican Congressional delegation are using Tax Day to promote the bigger refunds coming to most Americans due to the Trump tax cuts.

Trump’s Working Families Tax Cut was passed by Congress and signed by the President earlier this year. The average taxpayer in Alabama will see a tax cut of $2,843.

articles.body:
U.S. Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-Auburn) marked tax day Wednesday by highlighting the no tax on overtime provision he helped shepherd into law, saying more than 25 million Americans have already filed for the exemption.

articles.body:

Mo Brooks, serving as a County Commissioner in 2009, is seen smiling at a Huntsville news camera, talking about a teenager named James Lomax entering his first political campaign:

“When I see this youth coming forward, it makes me thankful and hopeful for what our future is going to be like.”

articles.body:
FormerAlabama Crimson Tide quarterback Ty Simpsonis preparing for theNFL Draft next week, and all offseason long, the question as to whether or not he should be a first round selection looms.

WhileSimpsonmay or may not hear his name called next Thursday night as a first round pick, he clearly is not lacking in any kind of confidence as he gets set to begin his career in the pros.

articles.title: Alabama Democrats’ biggest legislative victory in 2026 was making Montgomery far less safe
articles.body:
Alabama Republicans tried, and failed, to create a law that would require Montgomery and Huntsville to meet a certain standard for police staffing.

This was obviously called racist, despite the mayor of Huntsville being white, but that ploy worked, and Montgomery’s price is more crime.

Dale Jacksonis a thought leader for Yellowhammer News and hosts a talk show from 5-9 a.m. weekdays onWVNN.

The Alabama Legislature approved a new law that will incorporate federally-designated Primary Care Health Professional Shortage Areas into the decision making process for medical scholarships.

At its meeting on Tuesday, the Troy City Council entered into a project agreement with Lost Pizza Co. that will see the restaurant come to Troy in the future.

The council unanimously approved the project agreement, which would see the city reimburse Lost Pizza Co. for improvements it makes in its building in the amount of one-cent per each dollar of revenue the company makes in excess of $750,000, subject to city sales tax received by the city from sales at the restaurant during the applicable

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Auditions for the Greater Escambia Center for the Art's (GECA) upcoming production, "Murder on the 518," will be held Saturday and Sunday, April 18-19 at 2 p.m. each day.

Everyone age 16 and older, regardless of acting experience, is encouraged to audition.

articles.title: Innagural BirmingHIM Men’s Fashion Week Focuses on Community, Clothing, Health
articles.body:
By Ameera Steward |The Birmingham Times

After completing his 2025 collection, fashion designer and celebrity stylist Perry Varner wanted to throw a party to celebrate the release of his clothing line. But as word got out and more men wanted to be involved, Varner — an Alabama native, author, and all-around socialite — felt called to do more. The idea for BirmingHIM Men’s Fashion Week was born.

Birmingham is invited to a week focused on fashion, community, and the importance of men’s health. Ev

articles.title: City of Birmingham Again Urges Judge to Force Return of Fluoride to Water System
articles.body:
The city of Birmingham is again pressing a Jefferson County judge to order Central Alabama Water to restore fluoride to its drinking water system, arguing in a new court filing that the utility violated state law, created a public health risk and concealed the removal from the public.

The filing comes after Circuit Judge Frederic Bolling said on April 2 that Central Alabama Water likely failed to comply with Alabama’s notice requirement before stopping fluoridation, but indicated heintended to

articles.title: Teams locked into state playoffs, still alive in week’s baseball action
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Teams locking themselves into the state playoffs and keeping their chances alive were all a part of last week’s baseball action across the Chilton County area from April 9-15.

Thorsby High School went 5-0 during the week’s action, including locking itself into the Class 3A state playoffs with a sweep of Greensboro High School on April 13. Rebels’ pitchers did not allow a hit over both games of the doubleheader, and they remain unbeaten in Class 3A Area 7.

Jemison High School locked itself into

articles.title: Flippin’ Out — Trade show hosts over 700 people at Jeff State
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
The Flip Trade Show, a trading cards and memorabilia show, made its way to Jefferson State Community College in Chilton County for the first time on April 11.

“Well, The Flip show is one of the biggest sports cards and collectibles shows in the Southeast,” said Chilton County Commissioner Roger Perkins. “They’ve had a lot of shows in the area like Pelham, Dothan, and all of these places… I reached out to them to tell them we had a prime space for something like this.”

The show offered sports c

articles.title: Area sweeps and more in week’s softball action
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
Chilton County area softball teams saw their area play heat up as both Chilton County and Maplesville High Schools both completed sweeps against their area opponents.

CCHS completed the sweep by beating Calera High School in shutout fashion 15-0. Maplesville completed the sweep, defeating Verbena High School in an 8-2 battle.

Thorsby won a Class 3A Area 8 game against B.B. Comer High School 10-0, cementing themselves in second place.

Jemison High School had a successful week of softball, taki

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Cold Springs’ Black tosses no-hitter against Meek
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Ayden Black had it all working on Thursday night.

The Cold Springs senior struck out five in a six-inning no-hitter to propel Cold Springs to a 10-0 victory over Meek.

Black also shone at the plate, registering four hits and two RBIs in the first game of the doubleheader. He added two hits and two RBIs in the nightcap, a 17-7 Cold Springs win.

articles.title: PREP ROUNDUP: Fairview downs Brewer to claim 1st area title since 2017
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Fairview’s varsity baseball team defeated Brewer 8-2 on Thursday to secure the program’s first area title since 2017.

Tanner Isbell homered and struck out seven in a complete game to help propel the Aggies to the top of the Class 5A, Area 15 standings.

See more local roundup below.

Thursday — April 16

Prep Baseball

Addison 5, Sumiton Christian 2

Cold Springs 10, Meek 0

Lindan Hill:2-for-5, 2 RBIs

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Blistering bats propel Cullman to sweep of Mortimer Jordan, area title
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Cullman needed two wins on Thursday to set off a worthy celebration.

The third-ranked Bearcats accomplished exactly that, powering past Mortimer Jordan behind a sizzling offensive performance at Bill Shelton Field to dominate the doubleheader — 15-5 and 14-1 — and secure a Class 6A, Area 13 championship.

The Black and Gold, which lost to the Blue Devils on Tuesday to necessitate the must-win scenario, improved to 23-6 with the sweep.

“Really proud of the guys tonight,” coach Brent Patterson s

articles.title: Child Development Center celebrates opening of new classrooms
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Cullman County Schools celebrated the completion of one of its many new building project on Thursday, April 16. Child Development Center students and staff were joined by local and state officials to cut the ribbon on a new $2.4 million classroom addition that replaces mobile units with permanent learning spaces. The new building, which adds 8 new classrooms to the campus, can also be used as a storm shelter.

“This is way overdue, but I’ll tell you — because of our board’s vision for Cullman Co

articles.title: PREP GOLF: Good Hope boys, West Point girls claim county titles; Peppers, Sutter secure medalist honors
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Good Hope’s varsity boys and West Point’s varsity girls bested the competition at this week’s Cullman County golf tournament to collect their respective team titles.

The Raiders — led by Cody Stevens, Carsyn Boorsma, Paxton Cornelius and Brody Brown — carded a two-day total of 687 to squeak past Vinemont (689) and West Point (691).

Cold Springs’ Ty Peppers secured medalist honors for a second straight year with a two-day score of 153.

The Warriors, meanwhile, captured a fifth straight title —

articles.title: Community theater receives $750K ARC grant for building renovation
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Cullman’s new community theater just got a boost to its renovation plans. Appalachian Regional Commission (ARC) has awarded a $750,000 grant to the City of Cullman to support the project in the city’s downtown district.

The funding will be matched by $750,000 in local investment, bringing the total project cost to $1.5 million.

“This is a tremendous investment in Cullman and in the future of our local economy,” said Congressman Robert Aderholt, who made the announcement in a press release. “Pr

articles.title: Vinemont proposes community cleanup day
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
The town of South Vinemont discussed the possibility of implementing a community-wide “clean up day” during a work session preceding its regular council meeting on Tuesday, April 7.

Town Clerk Kayecea Sasser told the council that she has been contacting the Cullman County Sanitation Department on a near-weekly basis to assist residents, most of whom she said were elderly or single mothers, with the disposal of large, more arduous items such as old appliances or tree limbs.

Implementing a more

articles.title: Vinemont potter Renée Hoffman to return to this weekend’s Bloomin’ Festival
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
There’s something special about holding a piece of pottery that has truly been shaped by hand—and visitors to this year’s Bloomin’ Festival will experience just that as local artisan Renée Hoffman returns with her beautifully handcrafted pottery.

Working from her home studio in Vinemont, Alabama, Hoffman has spent the past four years transforming raw clay into functional works of art designed to be used, loved, and passed down. Each mug, bowl, and decorative piece goes through an intentional, h

articles.title: Beneath the Bark: Chainsaw artist to demonstrate craft at Bloomin’ Festival
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Visitors to the 42nd Annual Bloomin’ Festival Arts and Crafts Fair at St. Bernard Abbey will have the opportunity to watch raw logs transformed into imaginative works of art by chainsaw sculptor Corey Worden of Wetumpka, Alabama.

Worden’s journey into carving began long before the roar of a chainsaw ever echoed through a workshop. As a young boy growing up in Morris, Connecticut, he watched his grandmother quietly whittle wood and create delicate relief crafts. Fascinated, he picked up a knife

articles.title: PREP SOCCER: Meharg scores twice as Cullman seals area with 3-0 win over Mortimer Jordan
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –After the girls team blew out the Blue Devils 8-0 to seal their area, Cullman’s boys took the field to face off against Mortimer Jordan in the final game of the season.

In their prior matchup, Cullman took a 6-1 victory, and while the Bearcats only scored half as many points this game, it was in their control start to finish.

To open the scoring, Bearcat forward Carlos Medina snuck one by the Blue Devils goalkeeper in the 33rd minute of the first half. Then, just a minute and a

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Cullman collects area doubleheader sweep of Mortimer Jordan
articles.body: CULLMAN, Ala. –After falling just short in their area game at Mortimer Jordan on Tuesday afternoon, the Bearcats hosted the Blue Devils in a huge area doubleheader on Thursday night. Game one saw Mortimer Jordan get out to a quick 5-0 lead going into the bottom of the fourth, but in the blink of an eye, Cullman’s offense quickly exploded in the next two innings, scoring seven in the fourth, and they added eight more runs in the fifth to help seal their 15-5 win over the Blue Devils. Cullman jump

articles.title: Katie B. Kirkwood Architectural and Interior Design opens in Cullman
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –The ribbon was officially cut onKatie B. Kirkwood Architectural and Interior Design, a residential design studio in downtown Cullman, on Friday, April 10.

Said the Cullman Area Chamber of Commerce: “Katie B. Kirkwood Architecture brings a thoughtful, creative approach to design, offering services that blend functionality with beautiful, intentional spaces. Her work reflects a strong attention to detail and a passion for helping clients bring their vision to life.”

Kirkwood thank

articles.title: Op-Ed: Judge the book by its cover
articles.body:
In recent years, the Corporate Memphis style has been widely used for advertising. This form of art is known for its “low-effort” presentation. It is highly popular among platforms such as Google and Facebook.

Corporate Memphis displays pops of color and disproportional anatomy and objects. People in this design will have minimal to no facial expressions. Because of this, these designs are seen as insincere and ingenuine. They gained traction during the 2010s and increased in popularity around

articles.title: Ivey signs Alabama Work Zone Safety Act
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY, Ala.–Governor Kay Ivey on Tuesday signed Senate Bill 341, the Alabama Work Zone Safety Act. This comes just ahead of National Work Zone Awareness Week.

The Alabama Work Safety Act, sponsored by Sen. Josh Carnley, R-Ino, and Rep. Mike Kirkland, R-Scottsboro, establishes a pilot program to authorize the procurement, installation and operation of a photographic speed enforcement system within an interstate highway work zone.

“Currently in Alabama, there is roadwork ongoing in all 67 c

articles.title: Clothed in Cullman set for April 30
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The annual Clothed in Cullman fundraiser, which supports Curt’s Closet, will take place on April 30 at The Venue at Cotton Creek.

The popular “Heads or Tails” game is now a staple of the fundraiser. This interactive game gives guests a chance to win over $700 in local gift cards and brings a fun, lively energy to the night.

“Heads or Tails is the part of the night where you can feel the whole room loosen up,” said Curt’s Closet Founder Ashley Wilson. “It’s just pure fun, and hon

articles.title: Agriplex hosting summer camps
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– This summer, the North Alabama Agriplex is giving kids a chance to try new adventures. Along with popular returning programs, kitchen-based activities have been added.

Executive Director Rachel Dawsey explained that the new Kitchen Camps are possible because of the Agriplex’s teaching kitchen, which opens May 1. With this upgrade, staff can offer more hands-on cooking and baking than before.

Sewing Camps are also coming back after a break during construction, thanks to strong in

articles.title: Randall Hogan indicted on murder, abuse of a corpse charges in connection with 2020 disappearance of Jeffery Wilhite
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– A Cullman man on April 13, 2026, was indicted on charges of murder, abuse of a corpse and tampering with physical evidence. Randall Dewayne Hogan, 70, is facing the charges in connection with the April 2, 2020, disappearance of Vinemont resident Jeffery Glenn Wilhite, 59.

Hogan was originally arrested in connection with the case following an Oct. 30, 2023, grand jury indictment for murder; however, shortly before his November 2025 trial date, the State dismissed the case without

articles.title: Obituary: Roger Whitehead
articles.body:
Roger Whitehead, 77, of Baileyton, Alabama, passed away on Tuesday evening, April 14, 2026. Mr. Whitehead was born on June 19, 1948, to the late Fate Arthur and Mary Lou Hill Whitehead in Marshall County, Alabama.

Roger is preceded in death by his parents; two brothers, Milton Whitehead, and Billy Whitehead; as well as his sister, Doris Dean McMurry.

Those left to cherish the many memories of Roger include his son, John Roger Whitehead; brother, Lowell Whitehead, and numerous nieces and nephew

articles.title: Obituary: Kimberly Lancaster Thomas
articles.body:
With heavy hearts, but the assurance of eternal life in Heaven, we share the passing of our beloved Mother, Granna, Aunt, Sister, Daughter, and Friend, Kimberly Lancaster Thomas. She departed this life on April 15, 2026, surrounded by her family, and entered the gates of Heaven.

Funeral Services will be held on Saturday, April 18, 2026, at 10 a.m. at Hanceville Funeral Home Chapel. The family will accept friends and family on Friday, April 17, 2026, from 5 p.m. until 8 p.m.

Kim dedicated her l

articles.title: Obituary: Douglas Maze Ponder
articles.body: On April 15, 2026, the patriarch of our family, Douglas M. Ponder, died at his home in Cullman, Alabama. He was born in the Crane Hill community to Howard and Leona Ponder, November 16, 1928. He was preceded in death by his parents, a sister, Norma Strimmel, and brother, Harless Ponder.Doug was the epitome of a family man. He took great pleasure in camping under the stars and spending time on the lake with his boys. His memories of family trips were happily shared whenever anyone would listen.As

articles.title: FAITH: Cullman’s Zoe Snider talks religious influences, making a positive impact on her community
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –Cullman junior basketball player Zoe Snider thanks God for the many blessings that He has brought into her life, and that has made her journey even more inspirational. She leans on a couple of Bible verses to help her get through everyday life.

“God has meant so much to me, and He has helped me through some of my hardest times in my life. I know that He will always be there for me. He has changed my life in the best way, and walking in faith has brought so much joy into my life,”

articles.title: Wallace State finishes third at ACCC Women’s Golf Championship
articles.body:
BREWTON –Wallace State placed third at the ACCC Women’s Golf Championship held at Juniper Creek in Brewton.

Shelton State won the team championship, followed by Jefferson State in second. Wallace State finished third in the final team standings.

Mallory Brooks led Wallace State individually, finishing in second place overall at 3-over par for the tournament.

Jazmin Conn placed 12th overall at 17-over par, giving Wallace State two golfers in the top 12 of the final standings.

Maggie Chastain

articles.title: Che Sollievo Spa & Salon open downtown
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Che Sollievo Spa & Salon opened its doors on Sunday, April 12, in downtown Cullman.

Co-owner Amy Benson said the name “Che Sollievo” is inspired by the Italian phrase for “what a relief,” reflecting the experience the team hopes to create for guests.

“We want people to come here and, when they leave, feel like, ‘Oh, what a relief,’” Benson said.

The spa and salon offers a variety of services, including hair, nails and facials, along with retail products and a salt room. Planned

articles.title: Pet of the Week: Franny will find her way into your heart
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Franny, The Cullman Tribune Pet of the Week, isn’t the loudest dog in the yard, and she doesn’t demand attention the moment you walk in. Instead, she is a doggo quietly waiting to be discovered, a hidden gem with a mountain of love just beneath the surface.

A terrier/Lab mix estimated at around 6 years old, Franny has a calm, steady energy that makes her easy to be around from the very start. She doesn’t overwhelm; she invites. Spend just a few moments with her and she’ll show yo

articles.title: 2 indicted in connection with 2020 Cullman County double homicide; 1 pleads guilty
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Two individuals have been indicted in connection with the Aug. 15, 2020, murders of Levi Benjamin Lawrence, 24, of Cullman, and Dalton Matthew Bannister, 36, of Blountsville.

A Cullman County grand jury on Monday, April 13, 2026, returned indictments against Tyler Dwight Dooley, 32, of Arab, and Samantha Star Robertson, 25 of Cleveland, Alabama. Dooley and Robertson are each facing two counts of murder.

Court records show a third defendant, Jacob Andrew Drane, 27, of Cullman, pl

articles.title: Beau’s Law sets new standards for dog care ahead of October implementation
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– A newly signed state law sets baseline standards for how dogs must be cared for in Alabama, with supporters calling it a long overdue step to prevent neglect before it escalates into abuse.

Known as Beau’s Law, the legislation was signed by Gov. Kay Ivey during the 2026 legislative session and focuses on ensuring dogs receive basic necessities, including food, water, shelter and safe living conditions. The law is set to take effect Oct. 1, 2026.

“This bill is about basic care, n

articles.title: West Point plans StoryWalk project
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The City of West Point is working to get a StoryWalk along the riverbanks of the Chattahoochee River for children and families.

The StoryWalk would be a free interactive outdoor experience where children and their families can read a story as they walk along the river trail, similar to the StoryWalk completed for The Thread in LaGrange by Get Troup Reading. Pages of a children’s book will be placed on signs along the trail, with open-ended prompts and questions at each station to expand critica

articles.title: Chambers County works to clean up streets
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
This month, Alabama PALS (People Against a Littered State) is hosting a “Don’t Drop It on Alabama” campaign, and Lanett and Valley have caught the spirit of it.

This past Saturday, members of the Lanett Restoration Committee were joined by employees of the city’s Cemetery and Grounds Department and local volunteers in picking up litter along Phillips Road and Southwest 30th Street (a short-cut road from Highway 50 back to Phillips). Chambers County Commissioner Sam Bradford, who heads up the lo

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Bill King: I Fell in Love with Ronda
articles.author: Bill King
articles.body: Now, before you let your mind go where it shouldn’t, this Ronda is not a woman. Ronda was the next city we visited in Spain. It is a place of stunning beauty, but it is a deeply-divided city. The water of the Guadalevin River separates the city as it runs through the basin of El Tajo Gorge more than 300 feet below the ancient city. Jesus, in his Sermon on the Mount, talked about a city that is set on a hill and cannot be hidden. Ronda is such a city. It is clearly visible from below and from far

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - James Patterson: Valleyan Celebrates K-Pop
articles.author: James Patterson
articles.body:
Submited by James Patterson, a 1973 Valley High graduate and writer in the Washington, D.C. area.

An exhibit at the Korean Cultural Center (KCC) in Washington D.C. explores the evolution of the South Korean music phenomenon called K-Pop. It is an eye-filling, exciting, and enjoyable exhibit for all ages.

According to Britannica.com, K-Pop is a term for “idol music” produced by Korean artists trained by entertainment companies to become pop music stars. K-Pop includes upbeat pop, dance music, a

articles.title: Kiwanians host annual golf tournament fundraiser
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Luverne Kiwanis Club held their seventh annual golf tournament on Wednesday, April 8, at the Luverne Country Club. The tournament is the club’s biggest fundraiser providing money for scholarships for students from all four schools in Crenshaw County.

“The tournament was very successful with 45 people signing up to play,” said Kiwanis Club member Sue Blackman. “Everyone playing received door prizes and a lunch of hamburgers, hotdogs, chips and desserts.”

The tournament was organized by Jon

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Visit our website at www.luvernejournal.com for up-to-the-minute information.

* The Alabama-Crenshaw Baptist Association will host its monthly food pantry on Saturday, April 18, 9 a.m. at First Baptist Church in Luverne.

* The Alabama-Crenshaw Baptist Association will host its April Missions Meeting April 20 at Brantley Baptist Church. The meal is at 6 p.m. with service following at 6:45 p.m. Representatives from Sav-A-Life and Gift of Love will speak on those ministries.

* A First Responder

articles.title: Luverne first graders present 'Circus, Circus'
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The 43rd annual first-grade play will be held in the Luverne High School auditorium on April 30 at 6 p.m. The first-grade play is an event sponsored by the Dee Turner PTA and presented in honor and memory of Elaine McDougald Goodwin, a life-long educator and member of the Luverne family.

This year's play is "Circus, Circus." Preparation for this event began early in the school year and students are now eager to share their play with the public.

After Mrs. Goodwin lost her battle with cancer, t

articles.title: Willie Ruth Compton Massey - Obituary
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Willie Ruth Compton Massey, 92, a resident of Dothan, passed away at Southeast Health in Dothan on Tuesday, April 14. Funeral services for Ruth will be held at 2 on Saturday, April 18, from the Chapel of Turners Funeral Home with Brother Clay Crum officiating. She will then be laid to rest beside her husband in Mt. Ida Cemetery with Turners Funeral Home of Luverne directing the services.

Ruth was a proud graduate of Troy University, where she earned her Bachelor’s in Education, and later Auburn

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Visit our website at www.luvernejournal.com to view a live, interactive events calendar.

Cooking Council

The Luverne Public Library will host a Cooking and Youth Council with Mrs. Charlotte and Mrs. Kristen for ages 9 - 12 on third Thursdays, 4 - 5:30 p.m. The next session is set for April 16.

Book Club

The Word Nerds book club will meet Thursday, April 16, at the Luverne Public Library. All adult reading enthusiasts are invited to participate.

Historical Museum

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs tax relief bill, state grocery tax eliminated for two months
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Wednesday signed House Bill 527, putting into law a new package of overtime income tax deduction and a two-month suspension of the state sales tax on groceries.

HB527, sponsored by Representative James Lomax, R-Huntsville, and carried in the Senate by Senator Arthur Orr, R-Decatur, changes the Code of Alabama section that lays out the general rules for deductions allowed to individual taxpayers and creates a temporary break on the state’s 2 percent tax on food.

In 2023, Al

articles.title: Moore outraised, outspent Marshall in the first quarter of 2026
articles.author: Chance Phillips
articles.body:
Quarterly campaign finance reports filed by candidates for Alabama’s open Senate seat on Wednesday show that Congressman Barry Moore significantly outraised and outspent Attorney General Steve Marshall during the first months of 2026.

Both candidates are running for the Senate seat that opened up when Senator Tommy Tuberville announced he would be running for governor. President Donald Trumppublicly endorsed Moore’s campaignthis past January.

Marshall’s quarterly report states that the current

articles.title: Opinion | The ignorance is killing people in Montgomery
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
The cost of ignorance can be quite high—deadly, in fact.

If you doubt that, consider what is happening in Montgomery, where a convergence of several types of ignorance is driving up violence and simultaneously eliminating any meaningful response to it.

To be clear, Montgomery is not Alabama’s most violent or deadly city. Depending on the day, it’s not even in the top five, sometimes outside of the top 10. But it continues to draw the attention of lawmakers, media and politicos who have a veste

articles.title: RFK Jr., Terri Sewell clash over Black youth remarks
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. entered into a heated exchange with U.S. Representative Terri Sewell, D-Alabama, during his testimony before the U.S. House on Thursday.

During the House Ways and Means Committee hearing, Sewell pointed to remarks Kennedy made in 2024 that the health secretary repeatedly falsely denied making.

Sewell highlighted comments made during a podcast interview two years ago in which Kennedy suggested that Black youth prescribed ADHD, antidepres

articles.title: City of Birmingham announces 2026 Small Business Week events
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
The City of Birmingham announced a series of events for Small Business Week 2026, scheduled for May 3 through May 9, to connect local entrepreneurs with resources to grow their businesses.

“At a time when entrepreneurs are navigating rising costs, limited access to capital, and increasing competition, the City of Birmingham is taking bold action,” city officials said in a recent announcement. “From May 3 through May 9, the City will host Small Business Week 2026, an initiative designed to conne

articles.title: Lumpkin secures New South Alliance backing in HD 60 race
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Alicia Escott Lumpkin, a candidate for Alabama House District 60, received an endorsement this week from the Jefferson County chapter of the Alabama New South Alliance.

The group said its endorsement reflects Lumpkin’s positions on issues including working families, voting rights and community engagement.

“I am incredibly honored to receive the endorsement of the Alabama New South Alliance,” Lumpkin said. “Their work has been critical in advocating for everyday people and expanding civic engag

articles.title: Opinion | Alabama’s four-year record of tax relief
articles.author: James Lomax
articles.body:
Over the past four years, the Alabama House Republican Caucus has worked to reduce the tax burden on the people of this state in meaningful, lasting ways. Over $1.5 billion in tax cuts have been enacted since 2022. These have not been a single sweeping gesture, but through deliberate, session-by-session work based on specific pressures that Alabama families, workers, seniors and small business owners face every day.

The results are worth reflecting on. We reduced the state grocery tax from 4 pe

articles.title: Opinion | Investing in Alabama’s coal communities is investing in our future
articles.author: Matt Woods
articles.body:
For generations, coal communities have helped power Alabama and our nation. Alabama families built their lives around these jobs, and entire rural economies grew because of them. But today, even as the energy landscape evolves, those same areas still carry the economic burden of that legacy.

That’s why I sponsored Senate Bill 155, the Renewing Coal-Impacted Communities Act, to make sure communities like ours in Coal Country are not left behind.

SB155 is built on a simple principle: when Alabam

articles.title: Woman arrested for wearing phallic costume to Fairhope protest acquitted
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
Six months ago, Fairhope police tackled Renea Gamble to the ground after she refused to remove an inflatable penis costume to a No Kings rally while displaying the sign “No Dick-taters!”

Wednesday, Gamble walked out of a hearing on the related charges an acquitted woman.

“Free speech wins,” Gamble exclaimed upon exiting the building. “It doesn’t end at the city limits.”

Fairhope police had charged Gamble with disorderly conduct and resisting arrest. According to reports from people who viewed

articles.title: Christian Coalition of Alabama adds Perry Hooper Jr. to board
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
The Christian Coalition of Alabama announced Wednesday that it has appointed Perry Hooper Jr. to its board of directors.

Hooper has been a mainstay of the Alabama Republican Party, serving five terms as a state lawmaker from 1984 to 2003. He is the son of Perry Hooper Sr., the first Republican elected to the Alabama Supreme Court since Reconstruction and who served as its 27th Chief Justice from 1995 to 2001. Hooper served as Chairman of Trump Victory in Alabama. He is president of Hooper and A

articles.title: Confidence without knowledge is reshaping how we think
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Everyone has an opinion. That’s nothing new. What’s new is how certain everyone sounds.

In the latest installment of “This Matters,” Alabama Political Reporter editor Bill Britt explores how confidence—untethered from knowledge—is reshaping public discourse, and why being supported now matters more than being right.

Subscribe to “This Matters with Bill Britt” onYouTube,Spotify,Appleor anywhere you get your podcasts.

articles.title: Criminal justice groups praise Alabama Legislature’s parole reforms
articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
Darryl Littleton, Gabrelle Simmons and Hal Nash of the Alabama Board of Pardons and Paroles listen to people’s testimony during a hearing on Tuesday, July 15, 2025. articles.title: Small Luxuries That Quietly Define Personal Style
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Personal style rarely announces itself through grand gestures or dramatic overhauls. It builds slowly through dozens of small, deliberate choices, the watch strap that catches afternoon light, the texture of a well-chosen fabric, the barely-there detail someone notices only at close range. These quiet decisions carry more weight than any single statement piece ever could. They communicate taste without volume, confidence without performance, and intention without effort.

The most stylish people

articles.title: Alabama high school basketball to enter shot clock era in 2026
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
The pace of Alabama high school basketball is set for a permanent shift following a unanimous vote by the AHSAA Central Board of Control to mandate a 35-second shot clock beginning with the 2026-27 season.

The new rule, which applies to both boys’ and girls’ varsity programs, is designed to modernize the game and eliminate stalling tactics. While the discussion of shot clocks started two years ago, the board chose to wait until the new reclassification period began for full implementation.

articles.title: Fraud EDU: Fake surveys; fake summons – how Regions Bank is helping consumers fight back
articles.body:
In today’s digital world, scammers constantly invent new ways to exploit trust, fear, and our natural desire to share opinions or resolve problems quickly.

Two particularly insidious schemes—fake court summons and phony online surveys—target everyday consumers, aiming to steal personal information, financial details, or even install malware.

articles.title: Tuberville takes Senate floor to warn Alabama cotton farmers are ‘in the fourth quarter’
articles.body:
U.S. Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-Auburn) took to the Senate floor this week to warn that Alabama’s cotton farmers are facing a crisis, urging Congress to act before the industry’s struggles become irreversible.

Alabama is one of the top cotton producers in the country, generating more than $400 million in annual revenue for the state. Tuberville said farmers have gone four to five years without turning a profit, with the cost of planting exceeding what they receive when they sell their crop.

articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
A Canoe man was arrested April 17 on possession of drug charges, according to the Atmore Police Department.

Public Information Officer Maj. Chance Thompson said on the department’s Facebook page that the APD narcotics/vice division conducted a traffic stop in the area of Highway 21.

“During the course of the stop, further investigation revealed the individual, Bobbie Jo Kelton of Canoe, to be in possession of methamphetamines,” Thompson said. “Additionally, three firearms were located inside t

articles.title: Atmore City Council adopts resolution for $2.2 million line of credit for Atmore Community Hospital on April 17
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
The Atmore City Council adopted a resolution for a joint $2.2 million working capital line of credit for Escambia County Alabama Community Hospital Inc. (ECACH) during a special-called meeting on April 17.

The resolution is No. 2026-14, and authorizes the city of Atmore to approve a joint guarantee with the Poarch Creek Indians for a line of credit at United Bank for ECACH conducting business at Atmore Community Hospital.

The terms of the line of credit are 18 months interest only on a WSJ Pri

articles.title: Northview tops Baker 14-2 in Class Rural District 1 semifinal, advances to district title game vs Jay
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Northview defeated Baker, Fla. 14-2 in the Class Rural, District 1 semifinals Thursday night at Central School.

The Chiefs rallied from an early deficit, scoring two runs in the third inning, six runs in the fourth and five runs in the fifth.

Grayden Sheffield went 3-for-4 with two RBIs and a run to lead the Chiefs against Baker. Taylor Curtis went 2-for-3 with two RBis and two runs; and Luke Chavers went 2-for-2 with an RBI.

Grayson Burns and Cole Davis had three RBIs each for the Chiefs.

W

Shown in the front row from R to L: Aiden Wiggins, Lauren Dortch, Jayceon Grace, Ty’Derious Hall, Chase Milliken and Makenzie Evans. articles.title: Atmore chamber thanks Alabama Power linemen for National Lineman Appreciation Day
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The Atmore Area Chamber of Commerce recently paid of a visit with the Alabama Power crew headquarters in Atmore to express its appreciation to the company’s linemen for National Lineman Appreciation Day, which is Sat., April 18.

According to Alabama Power officials, chamber leaders thanked the linemen for their service and treated them to ice cream.

National Lineman Appreciation Day was established on April 18, 2013, by U.S. Senate Resolution 95, recognizing the vital contributions of linework

articles.title: Judy C. Strickland (with picture)
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Judy C. Strickland, 77, of Atmore, passed away on April 15, 2026.

Judy was preceded in death by her parents, Faye Jackson and Ethel Queenie Mitchell; her husband, David Strickland; her daughter, Sheila Thomas; her sister, Ann Jackson; and her brother, Donnie Jackson.

articles.title: Birmingham to Host Weeklong Series of Events for Small Business Week 2026
articles.body:
The Birmingham Times

Birmingham will host a series of events May 3-9 as part of Small Business Week 2026, offering resources, workshops, and networking opportunities for local entrepreneurs.

For more than 60 years, the U.S. Small Business Administration has celebrated National Small Business Week to recognize the contributions of entrepreneurs and small business owners. Birmingham Small Business Week 2026, which coincides with the national observance, is designed to connect local business owne

articles.title: Birmingham Stallions Prepare for Home Opener Saturday Against Orlando Storm
articles.body:
The Birmingham Stallions joined their fan base for a pep rally Friday night as the team prepares for their home opener Saturday against the Orlando Storm.

The energy at the rally made one thing clear: Birmingham is ready for game day.

“I need them and the shirtless section and everybody being as loud as possible,” said Stallions head coach AJ McCarron.

The fan club, known as the Horsemen, led the charge at the rally.

“This team looks like it’s here to stay, and if Birmingham gets behind it,

articles.title: Rep. Sewell Presses RFK on 2024 Comments He Made Suggesting Black Children Should Be ‘Re-Parented’
articles.body:
Washington D.C. —During a Thursday House Ways and Means Committee hearing, U.S. Rep. Terri Sewell (AL-07) questioned Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. about his previous comments suggesting that Black children with ADHD should be “re-parented.” Sewell pressed the Secretary to clarify and account for those remarks, which he now falsely denies.

In her opening, Sewell noted that the Secretary has made “a number of outlandish and frankly disturbing comments — both before and

articles.title: Clanton’s Alabama Power linemen, coaching duo bring teamwork from truck to ballfield
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
By Anthony Cook | Alabama Power

Andy Gore and Tyrone ‘TJ’ Morris work for Alabama Power at the same crew headquarters in Montgomery. But this year, that partnership went from the bucket truck to the baseball field when the two started assistant coaching the same youth league baseball team in Clanton.

Morris says this is the first year of many to come.

The coaching partnership was born out of a desire to “step in to help, and by understanding that being around the youth was bigger than our ego

articles.title: CCS hosts junior, senior students for spring career fair
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
All junior and senior students in Chilton County got the opportunity to peek into their futures at the Chilton County Schools’ spring career fair at Jefferson State Community College Chilton-Clanton Campus on April 9.

The event allowed junior and senior students from all CCS schools to immerse themselves into local business and industry as professionals from all careers came together. The businesses provided valuable insight into a range of different career paths available to students after gra

articles.title: Happy Coppedge — Senior golfer claims first county golf title of career
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Chilton County High School and senior golfer Logan Coppedge were the standouts from the 2026 Chilton County Golf Tournament held at Clanton Country Club on April 14. Coppedge captured the boys’ low medalist for this year’s tournament — his first tournament win.

Coppedge helped lead CCHS to the team tournament title as the Tigers’ team combined for the lowest team score on the course.

Isabella High School’s Rylea Ellison was the low medalist for the tournament on the girls’ side.

IHS was the l

articles.title: Kiwanis Club hosts annual Spring Carnival in Clanton
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
The Kiwanis Club of Clanton hosted its annual Spring Carnival from April 14-18. Kids and parents filed in to enjoy rides, games with prizes and different types of carnival food, from funnel cake to rolled ice cream to fried food.

The Kiwanis Club of Clanton is a community organization that raises money to benefit children in Chilton County from the carnival’s proceeds.

articles.title: Master Gardener plant, flower sales underway, continues April 18
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
After a successful first day, the Cullman County Master Gardener’s annual Spring Plant Sale will wrap up Saturday, April 17-18. However, if you’re looking for something to grow, get there early.

“We had around 3500 plants and I’d say we’ve already sold 60 percent,” said Master Gardener Brad Mitchell with just a couple of hours to go Friday afternoon.

Mitchell said the group’s Wildflower Sale was the biggest yet with only a few flowers remaining. He was expecting another record sale for the Spr

articles.title: From wood shop to toy shop: How Vinemont’s Bob and Christina Gingrich turn old pallets into something new
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Whiling the day away in his backyard wood shop, like he does most days since his retirement, Vinemont’s Bob Gingrich is always looking for new ways to make something old into something new. To turn something abandoned into something loved. To create something that can bring someone joy, one board at a time.

Gingrich, 68, spent decades teaching sixth grade at Vinemont Middle School — and coaching girls high school basketball. He loved getting to spend time with the children in the classroom to s

articles.title: A ‘Twisted Sisters’ adventure awaits at this weekend’s Bloomin’ Festival
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
When Burgundy Alleman of Ogden, Utah rolls into Alabama this spring, she won’t just be bringing jewelry—she’ll be bringing stories, laughter, and a little bit of family magic.

Burgundy is part of a lively group known as the “Twisted Sisters,” a creative family of artisans who specialize in breathing new life into old treasures. Their specialty? Transforming antique silverware into playful, eye-catching jewelry and art pieces that make people stop, smile, and sometimes even tear up.

Their journ

articles.title: Expansions progressing at CDC, Hanceville Middle School
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Cullman County Schools officials marked the district’s ongoing growth on Thursday, April 16, holding two ribbon-cutting ceremonies for new and planned facility expansions. Alabama State Superintendent of Education Dr. Eric Mackey was also on-hand for both events.

At the Cullman County Child Development Center, school leaders gave an update on a $2.4 million building addition, scheduled to be completed in the spring of 2026. The project will add eight classrooms, a storm shelter a

articles.title: Center of the Arts Community Theatre receives $750K boost from ARC
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala- Congressman Robert Aderholt, R-Alabama, announced a $750,000 grant from the Appalachian Regional Commission (ARC) to the City of Cullman to support the renovation of a new community theater center in the city’s downtown district.

The funding will be matched by $750,000 in local investment, bringing the total project cost to $1.5 million.

“This is a tremendous investment in Cullman and in the future of our local economy,” said Aderholt. “Projects like this community theater will n

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Various county school students on Tuesday, April 14, met at The Venue at Cotton Creek for the Family, Career and Community Leaders of America (FCCLA) Cullman County Poultry Cook-off.

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Addison celebrates Senior Night with a 5-1 win over Vinemont
articles.body:
ADDISON, Ala. –Addison hosted the Vinemont Eagles for Senior Night on Friday, and were able to send their seniors out with a 5-1 victory.

Addison sent their ace Kiah Lake to the mound and the Eagles got him in a jam in the first inning. After a Bulldog error and a hit batter, the Eagles had runners on first and second with one out. Eagle Randy Torres drilled a ground ball to ‘Dawg third baseman Stone Talley who stepped on the bag for the force out and threw to first to complete the double play.

articles.title: Bloomin’ Festival this weekend!
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Bloomin’ Festival returns this weekend to the grounds of St. Bernard Abbey and Preparatory School, offering unique arts and crafts, demonstrations, food, live music and more.

Festival Director Joyce Nix said there will be over 160 booths with handmade items like birdhouses, jewelry, clothing, pottery, soaps and candles. Live demonstrations will take place throughout the event.

“We have such a big selection,” Nix said. “You name it, it’s here.”

A graveside service is incomplete at this time for Micheal Edward Lindsey, 57, of Cullman.

Cullman Funeral Home is honored to serve the family.

Mr. Lindsey passed away Wednesday, April 15th, 2026, at Grandview Medical Center.  He was born August 2, 1968, to Edward James and Betty Jean Smith Lindsey.  Micheal enjoyed playing golf, reading, and watching movies.  He was an Alabama football fan.  He worked at several restaurants over the years, and he loved to cook.

articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Springwood boys soccer team made it 17-0 on Friday in Montgomery, bringing home a state championship title to celebrate the win.

Springwood did it in style, beating Evangel Alabaster 4-1 in a lopsided victory with the Wildcats controlling the field throughout the game. Goals were contributed by Nova Hoang, Lucas Albani, Nico Burriel and Cooper Key. Receiving All-Tournament honors were Felipe Moura, Nico Burriel, Thor Papera and Lucas Albani.

articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
Thursday was Impact Day at Point University, a day of community service throughout the local area. More than 400 students, faculty and staff were out in West Point, Lanett and Valley working together on a variety of projects to benefit local citizens.

“It’s been a phenomenal day,” West Point Mayor Steve Tramell told The Valley Times-News Thursday afternoon. “They’ve been getting more needed work done than we could do in a month of Sundays.”

West Point City Manager Ed Moon was with a group of s

articles.title: Clark Interiors transitions to new ownership after 35 years
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
For nearly four decades, Clark Interiors has been a familiar presence along Highway 29 — a place where families bought their first living room sets, chose bedroom suites for new homes and returned as their households grew.

This spring marks the end of an era for the longtime Lanett business. Owners Bedele Clark and her husband, Doug, are retiring and passing the space to Alexander’s, another family-run furniture store.

The decision came down to timing, Bedele said.

After 35 years of sales and
