articles.author: Loran Smith
articles.body:
AUGUSTA – A four-inch putt is not so dramatic, unless it represents $4.5 million dollars cash on the barrelhead, and untold future currencies—dollars, pounds, and euros—a second Green Jacket, a prominent status in the record books, and perks unlimited; when that happens, a tap-in is just as monumental as a twenty-footer that dances across the green to the cup and brings forth unending pandemonium.

While there is always comforting decorum at Augusta, which has the classiest environment and legac

articles.title: West Point hears updates about West Point Elementary
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
West Point Elementary aims to make kids learners and productive members of society. Working with stakeholders, school leaders want to provide a safe, supportive and encouraging learning environment for West Point’s children to make them smarter and overall better people.

Principal Keneithia Cook spoke to the West Point City Council on Monday about how the school is promoting that environment.

She said the school is part of the statewide Positive Behavioral Interventions and Supports (PBIS) sys

articles.author: Dean Collins
articles.body:
How do you feel after a big victory? We get a bird’s eye view of many victory moments when watching big events on our streaming devices. We usually see big smiles, hugs, champagne bottles being spewed in every direction and then the various speeches which sometimes capture tears of joy.

articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
On May 30, the Valley Walmart parking lot will be filled with pristine paint jobs, polished chrome, and loud engines as it hosts its inaugural open-class car show. The local store is partnering with the Children’s Miracle Network (CMN) and its affiliate hospital, Piedmont Columbus Regional, to raise funds to support local pediatric services.

The U.S. Forest Service’s plan to close scores of research stations could threaten the nation’s wildfire readiness, many foresters fear, and erode decades of work to understand timber production, soil health, pests and diseases, watersheds and wildlife.

articles.title: Selma Housing Authority earns score of 93% from HUD
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
The Selma Housing Authority (SHA) has been officially designated as a "High Performer" by federal regulators following a recent evaluation of its rental assistance programs.

The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) awarded the agency a 93% SEMAP score for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2025, a mark that places the SHA among the top-performing housing agencies in the country.

This score is determined by the Section Eight Management Assessment Program (SEMAP), a federal ov

The annual award recognizes a UA graduate whose career reflects excellence across the communication disciplines represented within the college. Brooks received the honor April 2 in Tuscaloosa.

articles.body:
BOYKIN –The renowned quilters of Gee’s Bend will open their yards to the public Saturday, April 25, for the Gee’s Bend Spring Yard Show, a community-led event showcasing the region’s rich heritage.

The event will take place from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. along County Road 29 in the Boykin community. The Spring Yard Show allows visitors to view quilts and handiwork displayed traditionally on clotheslines and porches while interacting directly with the artists.

Mr. Douglas was born on April 11, 1946, in Selma, Alabama, to Reverend A.B. and Geneva (Brown) Douglas. He was baptized at a young age after declaring his belief in Christ as his Savior, and he remained steadfast in his faith for the rest of his life.

State and local leaders gathered Friday for a ribbon-cutting ceremony marking the completion of the project, which officials say is designed to accommodate children of all abilities while enhancing one of the park’s most frequently used areas.

articles.body:
This will be a great political year, with some fantastic, interesting, and close contests. However, there will be only one hotly contested opencongressional seatthis year in the Heart of Dixie. The other six incumbent congressmen will coast to reelection, either unopposed or with token opposition.

articles.body:
Tensions are rising in the Republican primary for Alabama House District 20, as State Rep. James Lomax and formerCongressman Mo Brookstrade sharp accusations over their respective tax records.

In a Tax Day press release, Lomax’s campaign framed the race as a clear contrast, highlighting what it described as more than $1.5 billion in tax cuts supported during his first term while accusing Brooks of a long history of backing tax increases during his time in the Alabama Legislature.

April is the month for the annual Membership Drive for Friends of the Troy Public Library. All who love books and enjoy reading are invited to become a member or renew their memberships.

Each membership will aid and encourage activities that promote the Troy Public library, including the much-loved summer reading program.

articles.body:
Donations are being taken for the Pike County Department Human Resource (DHR) Adult Services at either Troy Piggly Wiggly ‘front office until May 15.

The donations are being taken in memory of Blake Carnley.

Items needed, and greatly appreciated, include dish and laundry detergent, paper towels, toilet paper, bath towels, bug spray, cleaning supplies, deodorant, shaving cream, razors, soaps, shampoos and toothpaste and brushes.

articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
The Johnson Center for the Arts is commemorating the 250th anniversary of the United States through reflections on home place and personal patriotism.

Andrea Pack, JCA executive director, said this exhibition invited participants to create pieces that reflect the landscapes, memories and everyday moments that shape our sense of home.

articles.title: Obituary, Friday, April 17, 2026: Donald Lamar Griffin
articles.author: Submitted Article
articles.body:
Donald Lamar Griffin, 86, of Grady, AL, passed away in his home on Tuesday, April 14, 2026.

After his early schooling, including time at the old Pine Level School—now the site of Sikes and Kohn’s country mall—Donald enlisted in the Army National Guard at age seventeen. In 1964, he transferred into active-duty service with the United States Navy, where he trained and served as an Electronic Technician. It is with great sadness the family of Melba Jean Farmer, 82 years old, went to be with our God on March 22, 2026, at Lynn Care Nursing Home, Front Royal, Virginia.

Graveside memorial ceremony will be held at 11:00 a.m. Tuesday, April 21, 2026, at Lakeview Cemetery, Brundidge, AL, where her ashes will be interred.

Ms. Farmer was born December 21, 1943, at Edge Memorial Hospital, Troy, AL.

articles.title: Alabama suspends state grocery tax in May and June
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Beginning in May, Alabamians will get a two month break from state taxes at the grocery store.

On Thursday, Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey signed House Bill 572, which provides an income tax deduction for qualified overtime pay up to $1,000. The bill also suspends the state’s grocery tax for two months beginning in May.

The bill was sponsored by Rep. James Lomax, R-Hunstville, and Sen. Arthur Orr, R-Decatur. In 2023, the Alabama Legislature passed a bill that reduced Alabama’s grocery tax from 4 percen

articles.title: Moore holds double-digit lead in latest Peak Insights poll
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
According to pollster Erik Iverson, U.S. Rep. Barry Moore is pulling away from the competition in the hotly contested race to become Alabama’s newest Senator.

Current U.S. Sen. Tommy Tuberville is currently running for Alabama’s governor and will vacate his seat in the U.S. Senate. Tuberville’s decision led to a rare open seat in the U.S. Senate as well as elevated U.S. Sen Katie Britt to the status of Alabama’s senior senator. Britt will not run for re-election until 2028.

Moore, currently in

articles.title: Troy native selected for prestigious national American Civic Tradition Fellowship
articles.author: Submitted Article
articles.body:
Troy native Quintarries Upshaw, currently Teacher of the Year at Workman Middle School in Pensacola, Florida, has been selected as a 2026 fellow for the American Civic Tradition Fellowship, a prestigious national program hosted by the Jack Miller Center in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

Upshaw was selected asone of only eight educators nationwide, chosen to represent his region through this highly competitive fellowship — an achievement that reflects both his professional excellence and the strong

articles.title: Local Softball: Pike Lib wins area crown
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Pike Liberal Arts Lady Patriots (16-4-2) secured a Class 2A, Area 4 Regular Season Championship this week.

The Lady Patriots kicked off the week with a non-area 14-4 win over Brantley on April 14. Sarah Strickland went 2-for-3 at the plate with a double, one RBI and one run, while Shelly Stephens went 3-for-4 with a triple, two RBIs and four runs. Allie Booth drove in two runs and scored one run, while Mary Holland Stephens drove in a run and scored two. Yaley McLendon pitched five innings

articles.title: Local Baseball: Charles Henderson wins area crown
articles.body:
The Charles Henderson Trojans secured a Class 5A, Area 3 Championship with a two-game series sweep over Andalusia this week.

CHHS began the series with a 9-1 win over Andalusia on April 14. Trenton Bryan continued his hot streak at the plate in the win, going 2-for-3 with a home run, three RBIs and one run. articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Limestone County Schools superintendent Dr. Randy Shearouse andschool board members recognizedCreekside Primary SchoolandCreekside Elementary Schoolthis week each for recently being named a two-time Blue Ribbon Lighthouse School.

articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
The City of Athens’ fundraising efforts are already in the pink ahead of this year’s Relay for Life event — thank in no small part to a special pair of “Pink Ladies.”

The Pink Ladies — the dynamic duo of Jackie Jackson and Kay Burlingame — helped the city’s Relay team set a new fundraising record at this year’s Celebrity Waiters Night fundraiser, which featured an A-list of locally famous Athens “celebrities” that included Mayor Ronnie Marks, Athens Police officer Michael Stainbrook (in full Sp

articles.title: Volunteers turn out for Main Street Atmore's Clean Up Day April 18
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
More than 20 volunteers participated in Main Street Atmore’s Downtown Clean Up Day on April 18.

Volunteers donned neon vests, a grabber tool and garbage bags and walked the boundary cleaning up trash within Main Street’s district.

Recently, MSA received supplies through the PALS (People Against a Littered State) and Main Street for the clean up day.

articles.title: Mason helps lead Northview past Jay for district title Friday night
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Northview defeated Jay, Fla. 2-1 Friday night to claim the Class Rural, District 1 championship at Central School.

With the victory, the Chiefs improve to 18-8 overall, and advance to the regional tournament.

Bryant Mason earned the win for NHS on the mound. He struck out eight and allowed no earned runs on two hits in 5.0 innings.

During the game, Mason also helped the Chiefs tie th egame in the bottom of the third inning on an RBI single to centerfield.

Northview broke the tie in the fifth

articles.title: Wallace State flag football team caps inaugural season with perfect record, Tiger Town Showdown title
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Wallace State’s flag football team completed a perfect inaugural season on Saturday, defeating Clemson 13-12 in the championship game of the Tiger Town Showdown at Nettles Park.

The Lady Lions finished the year 26-0 after going 4-0 in the tournament hosted by Clemson University. Wallace State opened pool play with a 67-0 win over NC State before defeating Clemson 39-18 to earn the top seed for bracket play. First responders have a difficult job; their efficiency determines how community members recover from life’s most devastating events, a pressure most cannot fathom. Since the men and women in these positions play a crucial role in day-to-day life, Huguley Nutrition has decided to honor them with a friendly competition.

articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Oak Bowery Volunteer Fire Department invites the community out to its first food truck event this Saturday, April 18.

The fire department plans to rent out the site of its future station at the corner of Hwy. 147 and CR 22 to food trucks on a regular basis, particularly Fridays and Auburn Game Day Saturdays in the fall because Hwy. 147 is a popular thoroughfare for people traveling to Auburn for football games.

articles.author: L. Michelle Moore
articles.body: With the deadline for filing our 2025 taxes having just passed, it seems like the right time to talk with each other about affordability and the federal budget. It stays on a lot of people’s minds, including mine. When I used to walk to work at the White House Office of Management and Budget, I would pass an elderly woman every day in Lafayette Park who had crocheted a pie chart of the federal budget onto her umbrella. articles.title: Free movie nights coming to Valley
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Valley Parks and Recreation is bringing “Cinema in Centerfield” to Langdale Ballfield this May.

The rec department will be showing family-friendly movies for free on May 9, May 16, May 23 and May 30. Showings will be a mix of animated and live-action films. The movies will start at sunset on the field at City Park, located at 5801 24th Ave., Valley. Families are encouraged to bring their own lawn chairs, blankets and snacks, though snacks will be available for purchase.

articles.body:
With April 15th behind us, it’s worth noting whether money, or at least lower taxes, buy you happiness. In this study, I examine the states with a special measure of tax burden calculations developed by WalletHub, as well as another study done by WalletHub titled the State Happiness Index. articles.title: Diversicare pinks out for beloved employee
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
It wasn’t an average workday at Diversicare of Lanett on Tuesday as the employees wore hair bonnets and pink in honor of CNA Chuchundra Maddox who is battling breast cancer for the second time.

Her colleagues at Diversicare came together to celebrate her as she battles chemo and the related hair loss, hoping to give her strength, peace, joy and love. They also wanted to remind Maddox that she is not alone in the fight.

“We’ve come together before, and we will continue to stand in love and unit

articles.title: Luverne Tigers continue building winning baseball season
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Luverne Tigers are continuing the 2026 baseball season under head coach Greg Evans. Currently at 10-and-11 the Tigers are building a winning season.

“This is drastically different from last year,” Evans said. “We only won three games last year. We have grown up a lot.”

The Tigers played Charles Henderson over spring break and lost 16-to-10.

“They are an extremely good team,” Evans said. “It was a competitive game on our side.”

articles.title: The best is yet to come
articles.author: R.A. Mathews
articles.body:
Years ago, a buttery-yellow cat appeared at my back door with a gray kitten, both shivering. Each had big, hungry eyes. Of course, I kept them.

A few months later, the kitten left.

I can still hear Buttercup sitting on my wall, meowing all day long, searing, sorrowful, endless pleas for the kitten to return. Yes, it was pitiful.

Time passed and the kitten did come back, but Buttercup turned a cold shoulder, unwilling to forgive the desertion. There’s a story like this in the Bible.

articles.body:
Despite all the chaos coming out of Washington, D.C. these days, this is a remarkable time to be alive.

Imagine you just woke up from a coma. The last memory you have is from 1981. The Iranian hostages were just being released after 444 days in captivity. You also vaguely remember that NASA was about to launch something called the Space Shuttle. In the first hour, you try to catch up on the news.

articles.title: USDA restructuring closes Huntsville Forest Service research center, shifts Alabama operations to Auburn
articles.body:
A USDA restructuring of theU.S. Forest Servicewill result in the closure of a research and development facility in Huntsville, with agency operations in Alabama shifting to a new state office in Auburn.

The move is part of a broaderreorganizationannounced March 31 by the U.S. Department of Agriculture, which includes relocating the Forest Service’s national headquarters to Salt Lake City and consolidating research operations nationwide.

articles.title: Huntsville’s Madison County Farmers Market returns for 2026 season
articles.body:
The Madison County Farmer’s Market will open for the 2026 season on Saturday, April 18.

The market is located at 1022 Cook Avenue in Huntsville. It will operate every Saturday from 8 a.m. to 2 p.m, and opening day will include live music.

The market is known for locally grown and homemade goods. Vendors sell fresh fruits and vegetables, along with items like honey and baked goods. Seasonal options include corn, tomatoes, peppers and okra.

articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans celebrated the closing of spring football practices with the annual T-Day Spring Game at Veterans Memorial Stadium on Saturday.

After using a unique scoring system in last season’s T-Day, the Trojans went back to a more traditional scrimmage style. The team split into offense and defense with a few different ways for the sides to score.

Birmingham Fire Department says too many homes across the city still do not have working smoke detectors — even though they are available for free.

The warning comes in the aftermath of a triple-fatal house fire, where officials confirmed the home did not have a working smoke detector.

“There should not be a household in the city of Birmingham that does not have a working smoke detector. It’s just that simple,” said Birmingham Fire Lieutenant Catina Williams.

articles.body:
The UABSchool of Education and Human Scienceswill host a free Family Literacy Night on Thursday, April 30, from 5-7 p.m. The event will highlight simple, practical strategies families can use to support reading at home.

Elementary and early childhood teacher candidates will lead hands-on literacy activities tailored to each grade level, and parents will leave with tools they can use to keep children engaged in reading throughout the summer break.

articles.title: Bloomin’ Festival returns to St. Bernard
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Arts and crafts were on display Saturday at the 42 Annual Bloomin’ Festival at St. Bernard. The outdoor event features the handcrafted works of talented artists and craftsmen, music and festival food. A highlight of the event is the grand prize giveaway on Sunday, April 19, featuring a brand-new 2026 Buick Encore. Additional prizes include a Dyson Cordless Vacuum Cleaner, Blackstone Outdoor Griddle and Apple AirPods 4. Tickets for the prizes are six for $25 and may be purchased online.

articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Two Wallace State Community College students were recognized by the Alabama Community College System at its 31st annual All-Alabama Academic Team Recognition Program on Friday, April 10, at the Embassy Suites in Birmingham-Hoover.

Brooke Drummond of Eva and Matthew McLeroy of Double Springs each earned $1,000 scholarships from the Paths for Success Foundation that can be used at any public or private college of their choice.

articles.title: Ivey signs bill giving tax break on overtime, suspending state grocery tax May 1- June 30
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY, Ala.– Governor Kay Ivey on Thursday signed House Bill 527 into law. The legislation, sponsored by Rep. James Lomax, R-Huntsville, and Sen. Arthur Orr, R-Decatur, provides an income tax deduction for qualified overtime compensation, up to $1,000. HB527 also suspends the state’s portion of the grocery tax for two months beginning in May.

“Alabamians are the best workers out there. I applaud Rep. James Lomax and Sen. Arthur Orr for working with us to provide tax relief to those employe

articles.title: COLUMN: I fell in love with Ronda
articles.body:
Now, before you let your mind go where it shouldn’t, this Ronda is not a woman. Ronda was the next city we visited in Spain. It is a place of stunning beauty, but it is a deeply divided city. The water of the Guadalevin River separates the city as it runs through the basin of El Tajo Gorge more than 300 feet below the ancient city.

Jesus, in his Sermon on the Mount, talked about a city that is set on a hill and cannot be hidden. Ronda is such a city. It is clearly visible from below and from fa

Concord News

articles.title: Blazers complete spring drills with annual Spring Showcase
articles.body:
BIRMINGHAM, Ala. –The UAB Blazers football team closed out spring practice Saturday with its annual Spring Showcase at Legacy Pavilion, wrapping up the program’s 15th and final session of the spring.

Fans in attendance got a look at a condensed scrimmage format that featured first-team units competing against each other for several drives, along with second-team reps and situational work that included red zone, two-point conversion and two-minute drills.

articles.title: Auburn concludes spring practice with first A-Day scrimmage of Golesh era
articles.body:
AUBURN, Ala. –After a Tiger Walk reminiscent of an SEC gameday, Auburn concluded spring practice with the first A-Day scrimmage of the Alex Golesh era Saturday in front of 34,276 at Jordan-Hare Stadium.

“Beyond grateful for what that Tiger walk crowd was for our kids,” Golesh said. “For me to get off the bus and it look like that was incredible. It was great to get a gameday environment. I can only imagine what it’s going to be like with 88,000 in there. Especially for guys who have not been he

articles.title: Cullman FBC fitness class helping local seniors
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –A new strength and fitness class at Cullman First Baptist Church aims to help local seniors improve their balance, build strength and connect with others.

Jeanne Madara said she started the class after she fell in the church parking lot last year, which made her think more about her own health. “I am 62, and I realized as we age, our balance and strength leaves us if we don’t use it consistently,” Madara said.

After her fall, she said, she was surprised to learn that many others

articles.title: COLUMN: 2 dogs, 1 bed, no dignity
articles.body:
I woke up at 3 a.m., which is not unusual. At my age, the bladder rules. It was time to take a bathroom break, return to bed and then think of something boring until I conked out again. That’s normal. What is not normal, however, is what I saw when I turned over and opened my eyes.

Once they focused, there it was, no more than 6 inches away. I was staring at the business end of my dog, Roscoe. He was lying between me and my wife – and believe me, she had a much better view than I did. I felt li

articles.title: Kennedy named Wallace dean of workforce development
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Brantley native Chris Kennedy has been named Dean of Workforce development at Wallace Community College in Dothan. Kennedy grew up in Brantley and graduated from Brantley High School in 1998.

“Brantley is such a great place to be from,” Kennedy said. “It gives a young person a foundation of a good work ethic and good morals. A lot of other places I’ve lived haven’t had that.”

Following graduation from Brantley, Kennedy attended Lurleen B. Wallace (LBW) Community College in Andalusia for two ye

articles.title: Jesus doesn’t leave us where we are
articles.author: Dunford Cole
articles.body:
We live in a world full of gadgets, and I do not know anyone who has not benefited from them. One of the greatest modern tools is the GPS.

We all remember when they first came out. They were big and expensive. Now, with cell phones, Google and Apple Maps make it simple and easy for everyone.

When I was at Emory University in Atlanta, my GPS often told me, “Turn around when possible.” It noticed when I went off course and kept trying to guide me back. The GPS didn’t care if I made a wrong turn

articles.title: Jesus paid our eternal debt
articles.author: Dean Kelly
articles.body:
April 15 is one of the most hated days of the entire year for most folks. At some point since the beginning of the year almost all of us have had to fill out those dreaded forms, whether we do it ourselves or get someone else to do it for us, and submit them to the government of the United States – our taxes we pay.

Some are anxious to fill out the forms and look for a substantial check for their tax refund. Some turn the forms in with great trepidation because they will have to be writing a ch

articles.title: Power unchecked decides what truth is
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Power was never meant to stand alone. So what happens when it stops answering to anything beyond itself and begins defining truth on its own terms?

In this week’s “This Matters,” Bill Britt examines a growing clash between Donald Trump and Pope Leo XIV, and what it signals about the dangerous shift from accountability to unchecked authority. Because once power no longer accepts limits, the question is no longer what’s right, it’s who decides.

Subscribe to “This Matters with Bill Britt” onYouTu

articles.title: Opinion | Maybe you should move to California
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
People tell me to move a lot.

Each time I write something that suggests a progressive solution to a problem or criticize the backwards, Alabama-method for addressing issues, I receive the same comments from the same group of dopes.

“Why don’t you move to California or somewhere where people think like you?!?!?!”

Apparently, believing that collective group-think is always right is a thing around here. Which probably explains why certain deplorable biases and other embarrassing beliefs manage t

articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
A super PAC spending heavily in Alabama’s 1st Congressional District is funded entirely by out-of-state political committees, according to federal campaign finance records, with multiple contributors sharing the same address and treasurer.

Joshua McKee is one of several Republican candidates competing in a crowded primary for Alabama’s 1st Congressional District, an open seat that has drawn significant national political attention. He faces better-known and better-funded opponents, including fo

articles.title: The V: Session ends as key bills fail and primaries remain unsettled
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
This week on “The Voice of Alabama Politics,” the Legislature closes its 2026 session, but the real story is what didn’t pass.

Several high-profile bills collapsed in the final hours, including a push to close primaries, a restructuring of the Archives Board and expanded local cooperation with ICE. Together, they reveal a pattern: momentum without resolution, and proposals that faltered when final decisions had to be made.

Bill Britt, Susan Britt and Josh Moon break down the biggest developmen

articles.author: Staff
articles.body:

Jones’ response came after Hunt criticized the department’s handling of undocumented immigrants and suggested the office was not doing enough, pointing in part to Lee County’s lack of participation in the federal 287(g) program.

articles.title: Opinion | The Halifax Resolves: the first step toward independence
articles.author: Will Sellers
articles.body:
In April 1776, as tensions with Great Britain escalated into full-scale war, North Carolina took a bold and historic step toward independence.

The Halifax Resolves constituted the first official action by an American colony authorizing its delegates to vote for independence. They were adopted 250 years ago by the Fourth Provincial Congress of North Carolina while meeting in Halifax, a town along the Roanoke River.

The key passage of the Resolves empowered delegates:

“to concur with the delega

Imari and Amarion Lemon have bright futures ahead.

Imari and Amarion are twins, and Escambia County High School’s valedictorian and salutatorian, respectively.

Both said it took a lot of effort to become the school’s top two.

“It just took a lot of hard work, from freshman year, taking all honors classes and staying on top of our grades,” Imari said. “That’s very important, and not procrastinating.”

Amarion said he’s worked hard to stay on top of his grades.

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Dollie Albritton Edgar recently celebrated her 100th birthday.

The Canoe resident was born on March 24, 1926 in Nokomis.

She has always loved tending her garden, planting and nurturing each harvest in the same way she has lovingly grown a beautiful family.

Her family includes five children, 15 grandchildren, 35 great-grandchildren and 27 great-great grandchildren, who are her greatest pride and joy.

articles.title: The Right Time — Jemison Police Chief Fulmer to retire at end of April
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
“It seems like yesterday.”

On April 16, like any other day, Jemison Police Chief Shane Fulmer got up early and was in his office by 6 a.m. Maybe a bit too early for some people, but for Fulmer, it’s something he has done every day during his 30-year career. Now, that career is coming to an end at the end of April following a lifetime dedicated to law enforcement.

“I am optimistic on how it is going to work, and I will miss a lot of it, because it is all I have ever done and ever known,” Fulmer

articles.title: Column: Living in the Already and the Not Yet
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
By April J. Buchanan | Religion Columnist

The Christian life is lived in the tension of the already and the not yet.

There are promises that are truly ours now. We have been justified, reconciled to God, and made alive together with Christ. His righteousness has been imputed to us, and our sins laid upon Him. This is not partial. It is finished.

Yet there are promises that await their full realization. We are not yet glorified or free from the presence of sin. We do not yet see Him as He is.

The Lady Lions finished the year 26-0 after going 4-0 in the tournament hosted by Clemson University. articles.title: Obituary: Kay Anderson Niezer
articles.body:
Kay Anderson Niezer, a beloved mother, grandmother, and lifelong educator, passed away peacefully on April 15, 2026, surrounded by the love of her family.

Kay dedicated her life to teaching and shaping young minds, serving as an elementary school teacher at Jones Chapel, West Elementary, and East Elementary. She was deeply committed to her students and took great pride in the difference she made in their lives.

She was preceded in death by her parents, G.O. Anderson and Lola Mae Anderson, and

articles.title: Obituary: Christina Ann Jones
articles.body:
Christina Ann Jones, age 51, of Vinemont, passed away too soon on Wednesday, April 15, 2026, at Cullman Regional Medical Center. A Celebration of Life will be planned by Christina’s family at a later date.

Cullman Heritage Funeral Home is honored to serve the family.

Christina was born on October 23, 1974, to Jack Richard Duncan and Rowena Ann Banks Duncan.

She is preceded in death by her father, Jack Richard Duncan; brother, Hillman Richard Duncan; grandmother, Velma Lee Twilley; mother-in-l

articles.title: Alleged off-campus incident under active criminal investigation, Barnette says
articles.body:
Updated April 20, 2026, at 9:58 a.m.

CULLMAN, Ala.– Cullman County Schools Superintendent Dr. Shane Barnette on Monday morning said an alleged incident that has circulated publicly through social media screenshots is the subject of an active criminal investigation being led by the Cullman County Sheriff’s Office.

The incident involves the alleged abuse of one student by a group of other students. Barnette said the alleged victim is not a Cullman County Schools student, though the boys accused

articles.title: Cullman County PALS calls for volunteers, urges residents to stop littering
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –Cullman County People Against a Littered State (PALS) is in need of volunteers to assist in cleaning local roadways. The organization’s president, Cherrie Haney, said cleanup can take anywhere from 1-4 hours depending on the condition.

You can volunteer by contacting Haney at 256-531-4222. She helps volunteers sign up and assigns roadways for cleanup.

In March 2025 the Alabama Department of Transportation (ALDOT) reported, “In 2024, ALDOT spent more than $9.4 million statewide o

articles.title: Wallace State foundation laid path to lifelong career for alumna Melissa Stricklin
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Melissa Stricklin knew she wanted to start her career as soon as possible, and that Wallace State was the best way to do that.

“Wallace was close to home, and I knew it would provide a great foundation for a career path,” Stricklin said.

Stricklin attended Wallace State from 1983-1985, and again in 2014. She majored in Business/Finance and earned her Business Technical Degree, AAS, and Business Finance degree. Her time at Wallace State allowed her to learn the fundamentals of

articles.title: Mavens & Makers hosting pretzel-making classes
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Mavens & Makers recently hosted a pretzel-making class, where instructor Anett Schlabach showed attendees how to make two kinds of pretzels: single servings and a larger braided style. She also taught the group how to make Obatzda, a classic German cheese spread that’s often served with pretzels.

Schlabach explained that the class shows how German pretzel styles use the same dough but are shaped and made in different ways. Anyone can join, even without baking experience, so the c

articles.title: Kiwanis learn about efforts to help homeless community
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
No one knows the situation involving homelessness in the local area better than Kevin Pigg, and he talked about that at Wednesday’s meeting of the Kiwanis Club of Valley.

In addition to being the pastor at Fairfax Methodist Church, Pigg is a case manager with the Chambers County Circle of Care Center for Families, specializing in homelessness and the need for public transportation.

Pigg said that homelessness is a social problem that continues to get worse. A homeless person, he said, is someo

articles.title: GVACC Junior Ambassador Program jumpstarts futures
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The Greater Valley Area Chamber of Commerce’s (GVACC) Junior Ambassador program offers students a unique opportunity to explore the inner workings of their community and set themselves up for success.

Registration for the program is now open to students in grades 9-12 from all local area schools. GVACC Director Carrie Wood described the program as a way to introduce students to soft skills and get involved and active in their community. During her time as director, she has witnessed the program

articles.title: Old Gin Creek to host barbecue competition
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Citizens of Crenshaw County and the surrounding area are cordially invited to Old Gin Creek in Brantley for a barbecue cooking competition. The competition is scheduled for April 25. “We’ll hand out the meat at 5 p.m. in the morning,” said Pit Master Paul Williamson. “It’s a $100 entry fee; the winner will get $1,000.00. There will be just one winner.”

Three judges will sample entries at 3 p.m. to select a winner. There will also be live music during the day, with two bands set to perform.

The

articles.title: The daily screen time standoff
articles.author: Amy Lewis
articles.body:
Dear Amy,

My 9-year-old has become very attached to screens. If it’s not a tablet, it’s TV. If it’s not TV, it’s a game. I try to limit it, but every attempt turns into a negotiation or a meltdown.

I’m also starting to notice they’re less active than they used to be. I want to encourage healthier habits without turning into the “no fun” parent or starting a daily battle. How do I handle this?

— Trying to Unplug

Dear Trying,

This one is easy enough. It's time to lay down the law. You should

articles.title: Alabama Arise reviews wins and disappointments in 2026 session
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
​​Members of Alabama Arise wrapped up the 2026 Legislative Session with a virtual forum that mixed celebration with disappointments, as advocates reviewed what passed, what failed and what comes next.

The coalition, which includes more than 150 member groups and about 1,900 individual members, is member-led and focused on advancing public policies affecting Alabamians marginalized by poverty. Organizers reminded attendees that policy priorities are selected by members each year.

David Stout, l

articles.title: Democrats tout individual donors’ role in District 5 race fundraising efforts
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
Democratic candidates seeking to unseat 5th Congressional District incumbent, U.S. Representative Dale Strong, R-Alabama, highlighted grassroots fundraising efforts after filing their first quarterly finance reports of 2026.

In a statement made after the filing of the first campaign finance reports of 2026, Democratic congressional hopeful Andrew Sneed celebrated raising more than $100,000 in campaign contributions for the fourth quarter in a row, emphasizing the role of individual donors in fu

articles.title: U.S. Senate candidate Craig Jelks slams Moore over Musk donations
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
U.S. Representative Barry Moore, R-Alabama, is facing new criticism after it was recently revealed that the congressman received $6,600 in donations from billionaire Elon Musk last year before announcing his campaign for Alabama’s open U.S. Senate seat.

According toAL.com, Moore—who currentlyleadsthe GOP primary field in Alabama’s Senate race—received two donations from Musk in 2025 as Congress debated President Donald Trump’s “One Big Beautiful Bill,” which Musk opposed at the time. Moore went

articles.title: Alabama 2026 Legislative Report: Sine die
articles.author: Beth Lyons
articles.body:
The Alabama Legislature convened for Week 12 of the 2026 Regular Session, meeting Tuesday through Thursday. Committees met Tuesday, and both chambers held floor sessions all three days.

The Legislature adjourned sine die on Day 30, Thursday, April 9, ending the 2026 Regular Session. Unless Governor Kay Ivey calls a special session, lawmakers will next meet for the 2027 Organizational Session on January 12, 2027. The 2027 Regular Session begins February 2, 2027.

As the session ended, Week 12 br

articles.title: Opinion | Alabama takes a stand for small business fairness
articles.author: Rosemary Elebash
articles.body:
Until this month, small businesses and other merchants in Alabama had to pay sales tax on money that didn’t actually belong to them.

When you buy something with a credit card, the business has to pay a “swipe fee” to the bank. These fees are usually 2 percent to 3 percent of the total price. Even though that money goes straight to the credit card company, Alabama was one of the few states that still taxed businesses on those fees.

It’s like if someone asked you to set their paycheck on the tab

articles.title: Wes Allen reminds Alabamians to register to vote before primary election
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Alabama Secretary of State Wes Allen released a statement Monday reminding residents that the deadline to register to vote before the May 19, 2026, primary election is Monday, May 4, 2026.

“The right of eligible United States citizens to vote in fair and secure elections is one of the hallmarks of our constitutional republic,” Allen said. “I encourage all eligible Alabamians to register to vote or verify their voter registration status on AlabamaVotes.gov ahead of the May 19 primary election.”

articles.title: Alabama AGC honors Mobile contractors for safety excellence
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Mobile Section of the Alabama Associated General Contractors hosted its 24th Annual Construction Safety Excellence Awards on April 9, 2026, at the Country Club of Mobile, honoring member companies with strong safety records in 2025.

More than 125 industry professionals attended the event, where organizers recognized 32 commercial, industrial and specialty contractors and suppliers from the Mobile area for their commitment to safety excellence.

The annual awards program recognizes AGC membe

articles.title: Alabama doctors urge safe drug disposal on Take Back Day
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Doctors with the Medical Association of the State of Alabama are urging Alabamians to participate on National Drug Take Back Day on Saturday, April 25 by safely disposing of expired, unused and unwanted prescription drugs.

Held in April and October each year, Take Back Day is to encourage people to rid their homes of unneeded medications, which are a leading source of misuse and can contribute to addiction and accidental overdose.

In 2025, Alabamians turned in 6,657 pounds of unused medication

articles.title: Alabama spring home sales rise, but trail last year
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Despite rising interest rates, the start of the spring buying season pushed home sales up across Alabama in March, though sales continued to trail last year’s pace, according to the Alabama Economic and Real Estate Report released Monday by the Alabama Association of Realtors.

“Home sales were up month over month in March, increasing 8.2 percent, but they lagged the same period last year by 12.5 percent,” Alabama Realtors economist Evan Moore said. “Median home prices increased as well in both

articles.title: Gov. Ivey congratulates first ADOC graduating class of 2026
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Monday hosted the 132 Correctional Officer Trainees, COTs, who are set to graduate Thursday from the Alabama Department of Corrections Academy.

Ivey met the graduates on the front steps of the Governor’s Mansion and offered her thanks and congratulations.

“Serving in any corrections system is tireless, serious law enforcement work, and I commend these 132 graduates for answering the call. When I took office in 2017, I made it a priority to address the longstanding challeng

articles.title: Opinion | When politics becomes performance and reality fades
articles.author: Mike Ball
articles.body:
Almost everybody—besides the Montgomery hospitality industry—was surely relieved to see the recent legislative session end. A lot of water has flowed under the bridge since 2002, when I entered the political world as a freshman legislator.

During that first term, my son accompanied me to the sausage factory in Montgomery to spend a week trying to make sense out of the nonsense. When we headed home on I-65, I asked what he thought about the legislative process after a week of wandering around th

articles.body:
Rep. James Lomax, R-Huntsville (left) and former U.S. Rep. Mo Brooks, R-Huntsville (right) are campaigning for the Republican nomination for House District 20, located in the Huntsville suburbs. (Left: Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector; Right: Andrea Tinker/Alabama Reflector)

HUNTSVILLE — If you were looking to his house in Huntsville for guidance, you might find it hard to tell that former U.S. Rep. Mo Brooks is running for state legislature.

Aside from the lone “Re-elect Mo Brooks” sign in his y

articles.author: Anna Barrett
articles.body:
Rep. Joe Lovvorn, R-Auburn, stands on the floor of the Alabama House of Representatives on March 3, 2026 at the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. Lovvorn, who sponsored a post-election audit law, said Monday he would like to make some changes after a last-minute executive amendment was added to the legislation. (Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector)

The sponsor of a new law requiring audits of general elections in Alabama said Monday he wants to make some changes in the future.

HB 95, sponso

articles.title: Evictions fell slightly in 2025, but some areas saw upticks, report finds
articles.author: Robbie Sequeira
articles.body:
Landlords filed more than 1.23 million eviction cases in the 10 states and 38 cities tracked by Princeton University's Eviction Lab. (Nevada Current file photo)

Eviction filings fell in 2025 for the second straight year in the cities and states tracked in a new report — areas home to roughly a third of the country’s renters — though some of those places saw increases.

Lori Chavez-DeRemer, at the time a member of the U.S. House from Oregon, speaks to reporters on Oct. 9, 2024. (Photo by Julia Shumway/Oregon Capital Chronicle)

WASHINGTON — Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer will step down from her post, the Trump administration announced Monday, following multiple reports alleging work misconduct including misuse of funds and more.

articles.title: Federal judge blocks reworked Arkansas law restricting minors’ social media
articles.author: Antoinette Grajeda
articles.body:
(Joe Raedle/Getty Images)

A federal judge has temporarily blocked an Arkansas law set to take effect Tuesday that would have restricted minors’ social media access.

Tech industry trade group NetChoice in Januaryfiled a lawsuitchallengingAct 900 of 2025, which sought to amend a 2023 law found to be unconstitutional.

The earlier law and the reworked version would have required age verification to create new social media accounts. A federal judge declared the 2023 law unconstitutional and perman

articles.title: Medicaid rule targeting abortion providers set to expire
articles.author: Sofia Resnick
articles.body:
Kaitlyn Joshua, co-founder of Abortion in America and a resident of Baton Rouge, La., said she feared many patients would go without care after Planned Parenthood closed its only Louisiana health centers in response to a new federal rule included in the broad tax and spending measure President Donald Trump signed last summer. (Photo by Greg LaRose/Louisiana Illuminator)

A controversial rule enacted last year that denies federal Medicaid funding to abortion providers is likely to expire this sum

articles.title: What to look forward to as baseball playoffs approach
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Three local baseball powerhouses are moving into the postseason this week as Dallas County High School, Keith High School and Morgan Academy secured spots in their respective state playoffs.

The upcoming matchups mark a critical point for the programs, each of which have navigated a challenging regular season to earn a seat at the table in the AHSAA and AISA brackets.

The Dallas County Hornets are set to travel to Excel County for the opening round of the AHSAA Class 3A playoffs. The series be

articles.title: East Selma Block Club hosts second annual Post-Easter Fun Day to revitalize community spirit
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
The East Selma Neophyte Community Block Club brought the neighborhood back to life Saturday, April 18, hosting its second annual post-Easter fun day to encourage outdoor play and community unity.

The event, featured traditional yard games, snacks and a cake walk designed to get children away from screens and into the fresh air.

“It’s just exciting to see something going on in the community again,” said Daphne H. Martin, captain of the East Selma Community Block Club. “It used to be where East

articles.title: Former Southside standout shines with opening touchdown in Auburns A-Day game
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
AUBURN –Redshirt freshman wide receiver Duke Smith made the most of his spring training at Jordan-Hare Stadium on Saturday, April 18, hauling in the first touchdown of the afternoon to lead the effort in Auburn’s 2026 A-Day spring game.

Smith, a former standout at Southside, ignited the scoring with a 28-yard touchdown reception from quarterback Tristan Ti’a. The play was one of only two third-down conversions allowed by the Tigers defense. Helping the offense secure a 66-43 victory under the m

articles.body:

On April 19, Charles Henderson High School teacher and assistant football coach Demond “Dee” Brundidge was arrested on charges stemming from an alleged sexual relationship with a student.

articles.title: Pike County Schools awarded grant for LED lighting at athletic complex
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On April 17, Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey announced the ware of 11 grants totaling more than $500,000 for energy-efficient upgrades, which includes a grant for new lighting at Pike County High School.

The grants have been awarded to fund projects that include upgraded lighting, HVAC and control equipment at schools, community centers and other locations in nine different Alabama counties, including Pike County.

“These funds will facilitate modern updates that will significantly reduce energy costs ov

articles.title: Troy Softball sweeps Georgia Southern on the road
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans (28-17, 8-10) earned a dominant three-game sweep over the Georgia Southern Eagles (19-26, 6-12) in a Sun Belt Conference Softball series this weekend on the road.

Troy opened the series with a 5-1 win on April 17. Megan Loftis led Troy at the plate, going 2-for-4, while Presley Ivey went 2-for-3. Makaley Boswell, Kiley Huffman, Mia Tidmore, McKinnon Howard and Sophia Jenkins each scored one run.

Abby Lovell (8-4) got the win in the circle, striking out seven batters and giving

articles.title: Troy Baseball secures series win in exciting fashion
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans (20-20, 10-8) notched a Sun Belt Conference Baseball series win over the Louisiana Ragin’ Cajuns (22-17, 7-11) this weekend, taking game three in a frantic come-from-behind series-clinching victory.

Troy opened up the series with a 7-6 win on April 17 in a game that saw NFL Hall of Famer Demarcus Ware kick off by throwing out the first pitch. The Trojans trailed 6-2 going into the bottom of the sixth when Troy rallied to score five runs to go up 7-6. Troy held Louisiana scorele

articles.body: Explore the full digital version of our April 22, 2026 issue and stay updated with the latest news and features.

articles.title: ANDY REVERES HISTORY
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
DAR hosts “Two Lanterns for Tomorrow” on the square

The Andalusia Town Square came alive with patriotism and community spirit April 18 as residents gathered for the “Two Lanterns for Tomorrow” event, a celebration connecting local history with the national America 250 commemoration.

Held 251 years after Paul Revere’s historic midnight ride, the event brought history to life through reenactments, colonial games, educational displays and family activities, drawing strong community participation.

articles.title: Congressional GOP Forum held at LBW
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
All seven District 1 candidates participate in local forum

The Republican Party held a forum for citizens to hear from its candidates running for the State of Alabama’s first Congressional District at Lurleen B. Wallace Community College on April 16 at 5:30 p.m. The forum was held to give the seven candidates an opportunity to share about their platforms, and it was moderated by Lenise Ligon, Beyla Walker, and Mark Bullock.

The open congressional seat is currently held by Congressman Barry Moo

articles.body:
The 2026 Miss Andalusia Pageant was held last month as a preliminary program for the National Peanut Festival Pageant, and many local young women shined on stage at the Lurleen B. Wallace Martha Dixon Center for the Performing Arts.

The Opp Bobcat and Straughn Tiger softball programs are both preparing for the upcoming area tournament after recent strong performances, including Opp’s 2-1 win over Straughn on April 14 and a dominant 15-0 victory for Straughn over New Brockton on April 20.

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
If it is sticky, slimy or gross, chances are children will find it at Gross Out Camp this summer. Registration is now open for the popular hands-on science camp in Andalusia, Opp and Florala, offering young campers the opportunity to explore the outdoors through field biology, discovery and plenty of messy fun.

articles.title: Bulldog soccer gains momentum before playoffs
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
After battling through a three-game losing streak against Charles Henderson, Carroll and Satsuma, the Andalusia varsity soccer team regained momentum with back-to-back victories, defeating New Brockton 6-5 on April 14 and Northside Methodist Academy 10-0 on April 20. The Bulldogs now turn their focus to the playoffs in Eufaula on Thursday, April 23.

Head coach Alan Torres said the win over New Brockton was a crucial one for the team.

articles.title: Hubbard seeks female State Democratic Executive Committee seat
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Harriet Hubbard is seeking election to the State Democratic Executive Committee, saying she wants to promote unity within the Alabama Democratic Party and help represent the interests of District 92.

Hubbard, a former chair of the Covington County Democratic Party and a delegate to the 2020 Democratic National Convention, said her experience has prepared her to serve on the governing body of the state party and help shape its future direction.

If elected, Hubbard said she plans to communicate

articles.title: Florala baseball heads into the playoffs with a 15-7 record
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The Florala Wildcats are preparing for round one of the 2026 baseball playoffs as they travel to Leroy on April 24 to begin postseason play. Game one of the series is scheduled for 5 p.m., followed by game two at 7:30 p.m. A third game, if necessary, will be played Saturday, April 25 at 1 p.m.

The Wildcats will enter the playoffs with a 15-7 record and momentum on their side following several key wins this season. Florala has picked up victories against Freeport once; Dale County, Paxton, and P

articles.title: America’s 250th anniversary reminds citizens the importance of voting
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
National pride is in the air.

I’ve been reflecting on recent events, political debates, the celebration of our nation’s 250th anniversary, and concerns about our economy.

When you weave it all together, one truth remains: we earned our independence and established elections to free ourselves from the rule of an overbearing king. Let us never forget the sacrifices made then—and all the sacrifices since—to preserve our freedom, independence, and the rights and privileges we enjoy today.

As citi

articles.title: William Larry Foley Obituary
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
William Larry Foley

William Larry Foley, age 84, of Red Level passed away at home on April 18, 2026, surrounded by his family. He was born and spent his early childhood in Coffee County. His family purchased a farm in Red Level when he was 12 years old and later he and his wife made their home and raised their family on the nearby farm of her grandfather. He loved being a farmer. Through the years, he employed and mentored many young men who worked on the family farm.

Mr. Foley was a faithful

articles.title: The Square, reimagined: Athens Main Street, county commission unveil restoration plan for courthouse grounds
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens locals know it simply as “The Square,” a scenic, centrally located outdoor space that serves as the civic backdrop for dozens of public-facing events throughout the year.

More formally known as the grounds surrounding the Limestone County Courthouse, The Square is set to receive a significant makeover, thanks to a new partnership between the Limestone County Commission and local nonprofit Athens Main Street, the city’s downtown merchant advocacy organization.

articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
An Athens man arrested in Madison on Monday has been charged in connection with the alleged sexual abuse of a minor under the age of 12, according to the Limestone County Sheriff’s Office (LCSO).

Caleb Allen Gandy, 31, was arrested on Monday, April 20 following a weekend-long investigation after LCSO deputies responded to a report of alleged sexual abuse of a child on Friday, April 17. “The report initiated an investigation by the Criminal Investigation Division,” the sheriff’s office said in a

articles.title: Deborah J. Bray
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Deborah Joyce Bray, 71, of McCullough, gained her heavenly wings on April 17, 2026.

Deborah was born on December 9, 1954, to James “Jim” and Frances Louise Lowery.

She is preceded in death by her parents, James and Frances Lowery; and her sister, Karen Lowery.

She is survived by her devoted husband of 53 years, Charles “Bo” Bray; her son, Randy (Annmarie) Bray; her daughter, Regina (Chad) Herlehy; and her beloved fur baby, Daisy.

She is also survived by her sisters, Patricia Kelley, Sandra (

articles.title: Main Street Atmore holds downtown Atmore 5:05 networking event at The Publican
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Main Street Atmore held its Downtown Atmore 5:05 event Monday night at The Publican.

The event, which will be held bi-quarterly, is an opportunity for residents to gather, netowkr, hear about updates and learn ways to get involved in downtown Atmore.

Complimentary drinks, nibbles and appetizers were provided by event sponsor First National Bank and Trust.

Kyle Dorriety provided musical entertainment for the event.

By Jeroslyn JoVonn |Black Enterprise

An HBCU alumna from Miles College in Alabama hasmade historyas the first Black woman towin NBC’s “The Voice.”

articles.title: ‘An Absolute Shame’: Judge Rules Central Alabama Water Can End Fluoridating Water
articles.body:
By Joseph D. Bryant |jbryant@al.com

The court battle over fluoride use by Birmingham’s regional water utility is over.

A Jefferson County judge late Monday ruled that Central Alabama Water may continue with its plans to end water fluoridation, over the objections of the city of Birmingham whichsued the utility March 27.

The ruling from Judge Frederic Bolling comes after a hearing and an extension to let the city present additional evidence in their efforts to force the utility to restore fluo

articles.body:
From April 24–26, Sloss Furnaces will transform into a dynamic hub of art, culture, and connection, bringing together artists, food lovers, families, and creatives from across the region.

Magic City Art Connection marks its 43rd annual celebration, and it’s shaping up to be one of the most exciting yet.

articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Escambia County Alabama Community Hospitals Inc. (ECACH) officials filed a lawsuit against the Escambia County Healthcare Authority and Wes Nall on April 7 for discovery and accounting purposes, according to court records.

ECACH is an Alabama non-profit corporation that’s responsible for and manages Atmore Community Hospital (ACH). ACH is a 49-bed care hospital that’s been a part of the Atmore community for more than 50 years. ACH’s services include Atmore Community Home Care, Atmore Urgent Car

articles.title: Mudbugs, music draw big crowd
articles.author: Lisa Tindell
articles.body:
A gloomy start to the day on Sunday didn’t dampen the spirits of the Greater Brewton Area Chamber of Commerce’s staff, board of directors and volunteers as they prepped for one of the biggest parties of the year.

The Chamber hosted the annual Kick It At The Creek event on Sunday afternoon as the clouds parted and gave way to a beautiful afternoon at Jennings Park.

Cool temperatures didn’t keep the crowd away with the park filling quickly as the crawfish trays were filled.

For the first time,

articles.title: State unemployment rate holding steady
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Alabama Department of Workforce Secretary Greg Reed announced Monday that Alabama’s preliminary, seasonally adjusted February unemployment rate is 2.7 percent, unchanged from January’s rate. It is below February 2025’s rate of 3.0 percent.  The rate represents 64,831 unemployed persons, compared to 64,057 in January and 71,929 in February 2025.

The number of people counted as employed increased by 14,603 over the year to 2,321,473. The civilian labor force also increased to 2,386,304, with 7,50

articles.title: City, County plans week long cleanup event
articles.author: Lisa Tindell
articles.body:
With Earth Day set for April 22, Escambia County officials have set a clean-up for the entire week.

In addition to plans by the county, a day-long event by the Greater Brewton Chamber of Commerce and the Brewton City Council’s Earth Day Committee is calling for clean-up in Brewton and East Brewton municipalities.

Escambia County will have special collection sites throughout the county with sites in the Brewton area included on Ridge Road, Pea Ridge Road and in the Boykin community. Other areas

articles.title: Column: My favorite local lunches, so far
articles.body:
On my first day of working atThe Clanton Advertiser, the person who hired me took me out to lunch at Valle Grande Mexican Grill. It was delicious, but that was 10 months ago.

Now, I have not gotten the chance to try every lunch place, but I have gotten the chance to try a few. I am usually a pack-my-lunch type of person, but when I forget to, or just do not want to, I always try to eat at a local place.

Obviously, Valle Grande is a great place to get lunch, but it does tend to draw a pretty bi

articles.title: Column: Artemis II moon mission is historic, humbling
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
By Scott Mims | Community Columnist

If you happened to catch the launch of the Artemis II on April 1 as it took off for the first manned mission around the moon in over 50 years, then you witnessed history in the making.

Having missed the most recent moon landing in 1972, due to the simple fact of being born too late, I was a little let down when I learned that the April 2026 mission was not going to physically land on the moon. However, the brave Artemis II crew members have already achieved

articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:

An agreement between the Chilton County Board of Education and the City of Clanton became official on April 20, putting the plans for the new Clanton Senior Community Center in motion. The Adair property in downtown Clanton will be revitalized into the new senior center and will incorporate the historic pieces of the original school that still stand today.

Former Cold Springs High School girls basketball coach Paige Parker Adams has been charged with more than 30 counts of sexual crimes involving a student.

According to the Cullman County Sheriff’s Office, Adams is facing one felony count of engaging in a sex act or deviant sexual intercourse with a student, one felony count of having sexual contact with a student under the age of 19, and 30 misdemeanor counts of distributing obscene material to a student.

articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
The Cullman City Council was divided on its decision to approve a one-time $400,000 incentive package for Rural King to develop a new location.

The motion to approve the incentive package was ultimately approved, but not without opposition from two councilmembers.

Councilmember Jason Willoughby said he met with several small, local business owners who opposed the development shortly after taking office in November 2025 alongside Mayor Woody Jacobs and Councilmember David Moss. During Monday’s

articles.title: Single finding in CCBOE annual audit
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
The Cullman County Board of Education was found to have a single “significant deficiency” during the presentation of its annual financial audit on Thursday, April 16.

John Posey, representing the Alabama Examiners of Public Accounts, said that during the audit period — between October 1, 2024 and September 30, 2025 — an organization had violated the board’s rental policy when hosting tournaments at Fairview High School, Good Hope High School, Vinemont High School and West Point High School.

articles.body:
One week after a fire destroyed nearly a dozen businesses located in the historic Stiefelmeyer building in downtown Cullman, the local community is finding ways to support many of the business owners as they begin to rebuild their livelihoods.

The final walking tour will be Saturday, April 25. articles.title: Active Pattern Friday into Next Week
articles.body:
CLANTON – Wednesday, 6:00 am, April 22, 2026

Models continue to indicate a series of upper-level disturbances will bring much-needed rainfall to the state Friday through early Tuesday.

The first disturbance will bring showers and thunderstorms to the western portions of Alabama on Friday afternoon or evening, spreading across the entire state Saturday morning.  A few storms may produce wind gusts from 40-60 mph and hail to the size of quarters north of I-59, but widespread severe weather will

The store is under construction on Cullman’s south side at 309 Olive St. SW across the street from Rock N Roll Sushi, near similar established businesses like Tractor Supply and Lowe’s. articles.title: PREP SOFTBALL: Bates’ dominant two-way game gives Cullman a 10-0 run rule win over Jasper
articles.body:
JASPER, Ala. –With the end of the regular season rapidly approaching, Cullman looked to continue an incredible season (34-7-1) on the road against area rival Jasper on Tuesday night. After scoring two across the first three innings, the Bearcats blew the game open in the fifth and went on to win 10-0 in a six-inning game.

Taking the mound for the Bearcats was Dracen Bates, usually known for her bat and fielding skills, but also an excellent pitcher. For the Vikings, pitcher Lilly Banks started.

articles.title: Shannon named principal of West Elementary School; Gragg named assistant principal of Cullman Middle School
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. – The Cullman City Board of Education at its April meeting approved two administrative moves. Dr. Chad Shannon has been named principal of West Elementary School for the 2026–2027 school year. Nic Gragg, current assistant principal at East Elementary School, will transfer to Cullman Middle School in the same role, effective July 1, 2026.

articles.title: What It Takes to Support Students with Unique Learning Needs
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
You see it almost right away. One student drifts, another moves too fast, and someone sits there trying not to look confused, which usually means they are. If you have been in classrooms long enough, you stop ignoring those signals. Supporting different learning needs is no longer something extra. It is built into the day, into planning, into how lessons shift on the fly.

That is partly why many teachers look toward structured training, often flexible programs that fit around work, focused more

articles.title: Breaking Barriers: Why Getting a Degree Is Easier Than Ever
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Getting a degree used to follow one path. You finished school, enrolled full-time, attended classes on campus, and stayed committed for years without many breaks.

That path still exists, yes, but it’s no longer the only option.

Today, education fits around life more than ever before. People work full-time, manage families, and still find ways to study.

Barriers that once made higher education feel out of reach have started to shift. Time, location, and even cost don’t limit people the same wa

The LaFayette City Council held its regularly scheduled meeting on April 20.

Michele Walden, vice president of public affairs, and Jennifer Stinson, senior accountant, from Energy Southeast (formerly AMEA) were on hand to present the 2026 scholarships to four students. The LaFayette area recipients are: Autumn Kiana Blackall, Jack Everett Holcomb, McKenzie Cole Johnson and Jacob Peyton Yerta.

To qualify for the scholarship, students must reside in a household that receives electric service fro

Donegan was crowned the 98th Miss America on September 7, 2025, representing her adopted home state of New York. Born in Virginia, she has been competing in pageants since the age of six. At nine, she discovered the Miss America Little Sisters Prog

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Michael Brooks: Platform Failures
articles.author: Michael J. Brooks
articles.body:
We grew up as high school friends and he became a music leader for church congregations. He told me the most embarrassing day was when he mounted the steps to the platform to begin worship, tripped and fell headlong onto the floor. Fortunately, he wasn’t hurt, but there are better ways to begin worship.

I thought of this lately when a conference speaker told a group of us about a famous pulpiteer from another generation who said as he mounted each stair he would pray, “Fill me. Fill me,” beseec

articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Team WHIP (Working to Help those In Pink) is hosting a virtual wellness tour next month to educate women on all kinds of health issues, just in time for National Women’s Health Month.

The tour, entitled “Her Body. Her Health. Her Power.,” will be streamed on Team WHIP’s PINK Portal webcast from 7 to 8 p.m. ET on May 6 and 7 to 8 p.m. ET on May 7. Women will learn a wide variety of health topics between both dates. The May 6 event will focus on reproductive health, sexual health, HIV, HPV and br

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Dean Collins: The Narrow Path of True Leadership
articles.author: Dean Collins
articles.body:
If you have never read Good to Great by Jim Collins, it is well worth your time. And if you have, you won’t be disappointed to revisit some of his research. In particular, it is interesting to note that only 11 out of 1,435 companies’ CEOs led their company to the kind of longitudinal success he described in his research. I am not suggesting that 99 percent of the CEOs were bad, but rather how hard it is to stay focused on doing the right and best thing as a leader.

Collins suggests that the ch

Each student recognized earned a $1,000 scholarship from the Paths for Success Foundation to be used at any public or private college of their choice. Scholarship awards totaled $61,350.

The Crenshaw County Children's Policy Council (CPC) held a working session on Monday, April 13. During the event, representatives from area agencies described services they provide to area children and families.

The session opened with a presentation to Sandy Walker, who received the CPC Patron Award. Walker was recognized for her many years of loyal services and dedication to the organization.

Crenshaw County Sheriff Terry Mears was on hand and described services provided by his department. F

articles.title: Learn to backburner the unbeautiful
articles.author: Andy and Renie Bowman
articles.body:
Living in a desert in the summertime, a real actual desert, you have to learn to make choices, choices that can make or break how well you thrive in this rather harsh environment.

You can focus your thoughts and attention on the unrelenting “cook your soul” heat in the summer, or you can anticipate the six months of near perfect temperatures in wintertime. You can easily resent the burning hot sand and jagged rocks, or instead, you can choose to raise your eyes to those beautiful, hazy, purple-

articles.title: Report: Alabama barriers leave 815,000 voters missing
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
A new report has found that more than 815,000 Alabamians—roughly 21 percent of the state’s voting-age population—do not regularly participate in elections.

Thereport, entitled “Missing Voters: The Real Threat to Alabama’s Democracy,” was published by Stand Up Mobile, the Southern Leadership for Voter Engagement Network, and Dēmos, a national nonprofit public policy organization. The authors argue that structural barriers–rather than voter apathy or indifference–are responsible for driving a sig

articles.title: Waters opposes gaming—and voters deciding—in District 22 race
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
In Alabama Senate District 22, one of the state’s most consequential economic debates is already settled on the ground—but not in this race. Terry Waters, an ALFA-backed nursery owner and first-time candidate, says he opposes not only gambling expansion, but allowing voters to decide the issue at all.

“I’m against gambling in any form, period,” Waters said in a recent interview with APR, adding that his opposition extends even to a statewide vote on a lottery or broader gaming legislation.

Wat

articles.title: Opinion | The SPLC indictment is exactly what you think it is—pure BS
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
It has been said that a prosecutor can indict a ham sandwich. Apparently, the way to do so is with pure bullsh*t.

We know this to be true because the Trump Department of Justice, using grade-A BS, managed to sucker a Montgomery grand jury into a multi-count indictment alleging fraud, conspiracy and wire fraud against the Southern Poverty Law Center on Tuesday.

To put that another way, the guys who don’t have time to bring charges against a single human male for the multitudes of child rape all

articles.title: Judge lets Birmingham water utility end fluoridation
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
A Jefferson County judge has issued a ruling allowing Birmingham’s regional water utility to continue its plan to permanently discontinue fluoride use in its drinking water supply.

Judge Frederic Bolling of the Circuit Court of Jefferson County issued arulingon Monday dismissing a temporary restraining order issued in the City of Birmingham’s case against Central Alabama Water. The city sued CAW on March 27 and called on the court to order CAW to reintroduce fluoride into its drinking water fac

articles.title: Mancuso builds grassroots independent campaign across House District 7
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
Dr. Angelo Mancuso says this year’s state House race in North Alabama was not supposed to unfold the way it did.

When he first entered the Republican primary for Alabama’s House District 7, Mancuso, a physician and former state representative, expected a traditional campaign built around policy differences and voter outreach.

Instead, a ballot challenge derailed his candidacy in the GOP primary, setting off a chain of events that ultimately led him to relaunch his campaign as an independent.

articles.title: Former state Senator Dell Hill claims Alabama Values PAC role after APR report
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
Hours after Alabama Political Reporterdetaileda coordinated political campaign targeting Republican incumbents across the state, a newly issued press release has identified a previously undisclosed figure publicly claiming a leadership role in the effort.

In a statement released Tuesday, former state Senator Dell Hill announced that he had launched Alabama Values PAC, the political action committee responsible for a series of attack mailers, websites and text messages aimed at at least five GOP

articles.title: Out-of-state operative tied to attack mailers in Alabama GOP primary
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
In the closing weeks before Alabama’s Republican primary, a series of attack mailers targeting GOP incumbents has begun appearing in voters’ mailboxes across parts of the state.

Those mail pieces, along with accompanying websites and a text message blast using similar language and allegations, are targeting at least five Republican incumbents, all of whom are also facing primary challengers supported by ALFA. The incumbents—Matt Simpson, Greg Albritton, Andrew Jones, Frances Holk-Jones and Phil

articles.title: Medical cannabis expected in Alabama dispensaries in early May
articles.author: Anna Barrett
articles.body:
Antoine Mordican, CEO of Native Black Cultivation, holds part of a cannabis plant at his growing facility in Bessemer. articles.title: Sean of the South | Who is God?
articles.author: The Observer Staff
articles.body:
OPINION —

The question was simple. “Who is God?”

CHRIS (5): He’s a big, big thing, but you can’t see him. He has big hands and he can pick you up and carry you around. And he won’t drop you, not even if you try to jump. He can fly.

ANDREA (5): Nobody’s ever seen a picture, so we don’t know what color he is.

MITCH (7): A lot of people don’t believe in God, these people are called ATMs.

VERA (6): but two months shy of 7): You can do anything to God and basically he’ll still love you.

articles.title: The Cultural Impact of Music and Theater in Popular Travel Destinations
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Live music and theater often shape the identity of the world’s most memorable travel destinations. Cities and towns known for entertainment rarely rely solely on attractions. Instead, they build a reputation through performances that bring visitors together and reflect local culture. Whether it is a historic theater district or a lively show venue, performance arts create experiences that feel personal, shared, and rooted in tradition.

Travelers today look for more than photo opportunities or q

articles.title: Planning the Perfect Family Getaway Without the Stress
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body: Have you ever noticed how planning a family vacation can feel more exhausting than the trip itself? Between budgets, schedules, and everyone’s opinions, even a simple getaway can turn into a logistical puzzle. Yet, with travel rebounding after years of uncertainty and families prioritizing shared experiences over things, the stakes feel higher. Whether you're heading to a popular spot like Sevierville or exploring somewhere new, a well-planned trip can create lasting memories, while a chaotic on

articles.title: Lady Senators season comes to an end
