articles.body:
Morgan Academy’s season came to a heartbreaking conclusion Monday, April 20, as the Senators fell in a double-header to Monroe Academy in the opening round of the AISA Class AA playoffs.

Despite a dominant offensive effort that saw the Senators rack up 20 hits across the doubleheader, they were unable overcome the Volunteers, losing the first game 8-0 and the nightcap 13-9. The sweep officially ends the 2026 campaign for Morgan Academy, a season defined by growth but ultimately cut short by an

articles.title: How Small Businesses in the Deep South Are Using Data to Stay Competitive
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Small businesses across Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana, Georgia, and Arkansas face slower employment growth, uneven income distribution, and persistent rural broadband gaps. At the same time, cloud computing, Data as a Service (DaaS), and affordable analytics platforms have lowered adoption barriers.

Retailers, service providers, and small manufacturers now use POS analytics, CRM systems, and AI tools to improve margins and revenue.

In this guide, we’ll examine how they do it.

Small business

The Dallas County System of Services (DCSOS), in partnership with the City of Selma, is proud to recognize April 2026 as Child Abuse Prevention Month, reaffirming the community’s shared commitment to protecting children and strengthening families.

articles.title: A Food Lover’s Guide to Exploring and Documenting New Food
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
For many travelers, food has become one of the most exciting parts of visiting a new place. Instead of simply eating wherever it is convenient, food lovers now treat dining like an adventure. Every meal becomes a chance to try something unfamiliar, learn about regional flavors, and capture moments worth remembering. Exploring new dishes often turns into a personal journey where curiosity guides the next restaurant choice.

Food-focused destinations make this experience even more enjoyable. Towns

articles.title: Action-Packed Family Travel Ideas for Your Next Epic Vacation
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Family vacations feel more meaningful when every day holds something exciting. Action-packed trips bring energy into the schedule and give everyone a shared goal: have as much fun as possible. Theme parks, mountain coasters, rafting trips, and zipline courses create moments that pull the whole family into the experience.

Parents get to step out of routine, kids get to test their courage, and everyone returns home with stories worth retelling. Pigeon Forge captures that spirit in a way few desti

articles.title:
Creating Meaningful Travel Experiences Through 
Nature-Focused Stays
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Modern travel has shifted. People are moving away from crowded city itineraries and tourist traps, choosing instead to slow down and reconnect with the natural world. In Pigeon Forge, Tennessee, this shift is especially visible, as more travelers seek out stays that place them directly in the heart of the landscape rather than at a distance from it. A nature-focused trip is not just about where you go. It is about how deeply you engage with the place once you arrive.

The Accommodation Choice Th

articles.title: The Rising Importance of Cybersecurity Education in the Digital World
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Every part of life now connects to the internet. People bank online, businesses store sensitive data in the cloud, and governments rely on digital systems to function. This shift has made things faster and more convenient, but it has also opened the door to constant cyber threats. Data breaches, ransomware attacks, and identity theft no longer feel rare. They show up in headlines almost every day.

What stands out is not just the rise in cyberattacks, but the growing gap in awareness and prepare

articles.title: Troy Athletics announces Clear Bag Policy beginning next athletic season
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Troy University Athletics announced that the school will be implementing a Clear Bag Policy beginning with the 2026-2027 athletics season for all ticketed events on campus.

The decision to move to the new policy is being done in an effort to enhance safety and security features, according to a release by the university. The policy will take effect Aug. 1 and will cover all ticketed athletic events at Veterans Memorial Stadium, Trojan Arena and Riddle-Pace Field. The policy, while not limited to

articles.title: Trojans earn Player of the Week honors
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
A pair of Troy Trojan baseball players earned Player of the Week honors as Aaron Piasecki was named Sun Belt Player of the Week and Jimmy Janicki was named Buster Posey National Catcher of the Week.

The Buster Posey Catcher of the Week spotlights one catcher from across the country every week, named in honor of Posey, considered one of the best catchers in modern baseball.

Last week, Janicki was a star at the plate and behind it for the Trojans. He earned a .450 batting average in four games w

articles.title: Pike Lib student selected as finalist for National Civics Bee Regional Competition
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Pike Liberal Arts School student Maddie Sellers has been selected as a finalist for the 2026 National Civics Bee Regional Competition.

The National Civics Bee is a nationwide initiative designed to encourage middle school students to explore how government works, why civic participation matters and how they can make a difference in their communities. The program aims to inspire the next generation of informed and engaged citizens.

Sellers is one of just 20 students chosen from across the regio

articles.title: Local teams gear up for baseball playoffs
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The AHSAA Baseball Playoffs will begin this week and both the Charles Henderson Trojans and Pike Liberal Arts Patriots will be hosting first round action as area champs.

The Pike Lib Patriots (18-12) are coming off a pair of wins heading into the tournament, along with an area crown. On April 17, Pike avenged a loss earlier in the week to Class 4A Opp with a 10-6 win. Jack Baggett went 2-for-3 in the win with an RBI, while Braden Shirley went 2-for-4 with two RBIs and a double. Garrett Whitaker

articles.title: Local Softball: CHHS outscores Luverne, teams battle in Geneva tournament
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Over the weekend a number of local softball teams were in action, including the Charles Henderson Lady Trojans (10-14) earning a 12-10 win over Luverne on Monday, as well.

Keirstin Gamble had a big day at the plate for CHHS in the win, going 2-for-2 with two stolen bases, two RBIs and three runs, while Isabella Turner went 2-for-3 with three RBIs and two runs. Erika Young also collected two hits and scored one run, while Camille Lewis drove in two runs. Lewis also pitched all seven innings and

articles.title: Deadline for voter registration is May 4
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
The deadline to register to vote in the 2026 Alabama primary elections is fast approaching.

Alabama’s Republican and Democratic Primaries are both Tuesday, May 19. In order to vote in the primary election, new voters must be registered by May 4.

Alabama voters will have the opportunity to elect a U.S. Senator, all seven seats of the U.S. House of Representatives as well as all of the state’s constitutional officers in primaries that will be heavily contested on both the federal and state level

articles.title: Kathryn L. Beasley presents The Proof is in the Dough at Pioneer Museum of Alabama
articles.author: Submitted Article
articles.body:
In the years before the Great Depression, rural women in the South, like today, looked for ways to make extra money for themselves and their families. This is the topic that will be presented at The Pioneer Museum of Alabama, who will host Dr. Kathryn L. Beasley on Sunday, April 26, 2026, at 2:00pm.

Beasley is the author of “The Proof is in the Dough: Rural Southern Women, Extension, and Making Money”, published by the University of Georgia Press. The event, which is co-sponsored by the Pike Co

articles.title: Ivey approves photo enforcement pilot program with Alabama Work Zone Safety Act
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Alabama Governor Kay Ivey this month signed into law Senate Bill 341, the Alabama Work Zone Safety Act. The bill’s signing comes just ahead of National Work Zone Awareness Week.

The legislation, sponsored by Sen. Josh Carnley and Rep. Mike Kirkland, establishes a pilot program to authorize the procurement, installation and operation of a photographic speed enforcement system within an interstate highway work zone.

“Currently in Alabama, there is roadwork ongoing in all 67 counties, including o

articles.title: Limestone County Sports Hall of Fame announces 2026 class
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
The Limestone County Sports Hall of Fame Board of Directors recently unveiled its Class of 2026 inductees.

This year’s induction ceremony will be held June 13 at the Alabama Veterans Museum in Athens.

Tickets for the event, which begins at 6 p.m., can be purchased through the United Way of Athens-Limestone County.

“We are excited to induct six high-quality individuals into the Limestone County Sports Hall of Fame,” President Jeff Hodges said. “They are all extremely worthy and will make excel

articles.title: New all-abilities playground opens at Joe Wheeler State Park
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
For nature lovers, Joe Wheeler State Park has long been known as a great place to get away. But now, for kids of all abilities, it’s also a coming into its own as a destination to simply go outside and play.

Local leaders and Alabama State Parks staff cut the ribbon this month on a new playground at Joe Wheeler State Park, showcasing a play area that represents “a dynamic transformation of one of the park’s most popular gathering spaces,” according to park officials.

“Designed to inspire play

articles.title: Randolph’s four RBIs fuel Northview Lady Chiefs’ 16-3 win over T.R. Miller in Brewton
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Northview’s Daviona Randolph went 3-for-4 with four RBIs to help lead the Lady Chiefs to a 16-3 victory over T.R. Miller Tuesday night in Brewton.

Randolph added three runs on the night and a double.

Kylee Langham also had a three-hit night in five at bats, and included an RBI.

Riley Brooks, Addysen Bolen and Aubrey Hadley went 2-for-4 each. Brooks added two RBIs.

Northview scored five runs in the first inning, one run in the second, four runs in the third and six runs in the sixth.

articles.title: ‘We’re All One’: Band of Brothers Mentality Was Key to Fairfield’s Championship Success
articles.body:
By Solomon Crenshaw Jr. |For the Birmingham Times

Milton Jones took the prize as the most valuable player, leading Fairfield High Preparatory School to the Class 4A boys state basketball championship.

The senior forward could have also taken the prize of having the most siblings among players of the Fairfield athletics program, with 11 — eight sisters and three brothers. But even if he didn’t have a single sibling by birth, he’s part of a brotherhood with teammates on the Tigers squad.

“That’

articles.title: Coach Maurice Ford Helped Put Fairfield On The Road To Athletic Success
articles.body:
By Solomon Crenshaw Jr. |For the Birmingham Times

Maurice Ford was walking from the office of Fairfield High Preparatory School when he pondered the journey he’s taken the past 17 years.

“This is a long way from Parker,” said Ford, the Tigers’ athletic director and head boys basketball coach.

In truth, Ford’s trip from legendary Arthur Harold Parker High School to the high school on Valley Road in Fairfield isn’t that far, a bit more than six miles elapsing a scant 11 minutes. But Ford’s jour

articles.title: Birmingham’s Negro Southern League Museum Celebrates Jackie Robinson Day
articles.body:
By Sym Posey |The Birmingham Times

The crack of the bat, the echo of history, and the weight of untold stories filled the air on Saturday, April 18, as the Negro Southern League Museum hosted its annual Jackie Robinson Day — an event that continues to grow in both meaning and impact.

Held every year since the museum first opened its doors in 2015, the celebration honors not only Jackie Robinson’s historic breaking of Major League Baseball’s color barrier, but also the countless Negro League pl

articles.title: Registration opens for 2026 Brewton Area YMCA Babe Ruth baseball and softball league
articles.author: SPONSORED CONTENT
articles.body:
Registration Opens for Brewton Area YMCA Babe Ruth Baseball & Softball League

The Brewton Area YMCA is now accepting registrations for its 2026 Babe Ruth Baseball and Softball league, offering local teens ages 13–16 the opportunity to hit the field this summer.

Registration is open through May 8, 2026, with a fee of $45 per player. The cost includes a team shirt, belt, and hat. Participants must fall within the age range as of May 1, 2026.

Practices are scheduled to begin the week of May 11,

articles.title: Price makes intersection change
articles.body:
Clanton Mayor Dr. Jeffrey Price detailed the removal of a Sixth Street parking spot that has blocked drivers' vision making a left-hand turn from Second Avenue difficult. Price worked with the Public Works Director to make this change after he received feedback from citizens.

“It was residents who would just see me out and say, ‘It’s just a little thing, but that little parking spot needs to be looked out because it’s dangerous,’” said Price. “(Responses are) really positive, people were really

articles.title: Thorsby fireworks show to be moved this summer
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
During the Thorsby City Council's regularly scheduled April 20 meeting, council members discussed a state ordinance that would prohibit their July 4 fireworks show from being where it is usually held — Richard Wood Park.

“It shall be unlawful to explode or ignite fireworks within 600 feet of any church, hospital, asylum, public school, any enclosed building, or within 200 feet of where fireworks are stored, sold, or offered for sale,” the ordinance said.
Richard Wood Park is located directly ac

articles.title: Need to knows for Absentee Voting in Chilton County
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
The Alabama Primary Election will take place on May 19, but all absentee ballot applications must be turned in by May 14, five days before the election. Mail-in absentee ballot applications must be delivered seven days before the election by May 12.

A voter can request an absentee ballot from now until May 14 at the Circuit Clerk’s Office, Monday through Friday, 8:30-11:30 a.m. or 1:30-4:00 p.m.

A good resource for frequently asked questions about absentee voting can be found athttps://www.sos

articles.title: Battle of Highway 31 — Second derby yields CCHS girls win, another boys stalemate
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
The second Highway 31 Derby between Chilton County and Jemison High Schools on April 21 went nearly the exact same way the first one did a month and a half before, with a CCHS girls’ win and a draw in the boys’ match.

Entering the derby, the Jemison varsity girls picked up a 5-4 win over Calera High School on April 2 for their second win of the season. However, they have struggled to find good form over a stretch of matches this season. CCHS entered the girls’ match having locked up a spot in t

articles.title: FAITH: Cullman’s Kaylee Johnson talks faith, religious influences
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –Cullman junior basketball player Kaylee Johnson is so blessed and grateful to have God in her life, and despite some challenges that she has faced in her life, He has helped her stay grounded in her faith. She leans on a certain Bible verse and a certain Christian song to help her get through every day life.

“Having God in my life helps me stay grounded, and knowing I have a relationship with Him helps me get through the tough moments in life. God has changed my life completely.

articles.title: Head coach Kalen DeBoer’s contract extended through 2033 season
articles.body:
TUSCALOOSA, Ala. –A contract extension for Alabama head football coachKalen DeBoerwas approved Wednesday by The University of Alabama System Board of Trustees Compensation Committee.

The new agreement will extend his current contract by two seasons, keeping him in charge of the Crimson Tide football program through January 31, 2033.

“My family and I would like to thank the Board of Trustees, Chancellor Trant, President Mohler, Director of AthleticsGreg Byrne, our athletics administration and f

articles.title: Allen: Deadline to register to vote in May 19 Primary Election is May 4
articles.body: MONTGOMERY, Ala.– Alabama Secretary of State Wes Allen reminded Alabama residents that the deadline to register to vote before the May 19, 2026, Primary Election is Monday, May 4, 2026.“The right of eligible United States citizens to vote in fair and secure elections is one of the hallmarks of our constitutional republic,” Allen said. “I encourage all eligible Alabamians to register to vote or verify their voter registration status on AlabamaVotes.gov ahead of the May 19 Primary Election.”Electr

articles.title: Planning Commission calls for Comprehensive Plan update from city
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –At the City of Cullman Planning Commission’s April meeting, Commissioner  Michael Sullins announced the Commission would be requesting an updated Comprehensive Plan from the Cullman City Council.

“It is recommended by the code of Alabama that the Comprehensive Plan be updated every 5-10 years, and we are outside of that number. Therefore, we are overdue,” Sullins stated. “Statute 11-52-8 of Alabama Code requires a planning commission to adopt a master plan. What we are recommendi

articles.title: Wallace State to present Spring Under the Stars and Stripes concert April 23
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Wallace State Community College will celebrate the 250th birthday of the United States with its Spring Under the Stars and Stripes concert on Thursday, April 23.

The free concert will feature performances from the college’s Performing Arts ensembles, including the Wallace State Concert Choir, Singers, Jazz Band, Concert Band, Percussion Ensemble, Theatre and Pride Line.

“We invite everyone to come out and enjoy a night of music in the great outdoors,” said Ricky Burks, chair

articles.title: 2024 Alabama law changed what out-of-state boaters need before hitting the water
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Lake season is coming, and, as a reminder, Alabama’s rules for out-of-state boaters changed in 2024. The change ended a longtime exemption that allowed some nonresidents to operate on Alabama waters without certification for up to 45 days.

According to information provided by Rep. Tim Wadsworth, R-Arley, that exemption ended Oct. 1, 2024. State records show the change came through HB375, enacted as Act 2024-394. The law amended Section 33-5-52 of the Code of Alabama and took effe

articles.title: Harmony House gears up for 10th Dragon Boat Race
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Harmony House’s annual Dragon Boat Race will return to West Point Lake on July 11. They’re gearing up for the big race, with team registration open until May 1.

The race promises a fun day for spectators to watch teams from area businesses, churches and community organizations paddle in authentic Hong Kong-style dragon boats. Registration will be capped at 30 teams, and so far teams scheduled to compete include Duracell, First Baptist Church on the Square, the LaGrange Fire Department, the LaGr

articles.title: RUN FOR THE ROSES: Exceptional Derby returns to Troup County next week
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Exceptional Way is preparing for the third annual Exceptional Derby on May 2, which will celebrate the Kentucky Derby while raising money for a good cause.

All proceeds from the Exceptional Derby go to support The Exceptional Way, which provides day program activities for adults with disabilities in Troup County. The Exceptional Way emphasizes socialization and building relationships, with program participants doing many activities with their peers, including sports, arts and crafts, and co

articles.title: RIDERS, GET READY: Cattlemen’s barrel race returns this week
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Chambers County Cattlemen’s Association will bring back the Eugene Blair Memorial Barrel Race this weekend. The Cattlemen’s third annual barrel race will be held this Saturday, April 25, at the Chambers County Ag Center, located at 1200 AL Hwy. 77, LaFayette.

There will be time to practice your skills with open arena from 8 to 9 a.m. CT/9 to 10 a.m. ET and exhibitions from 9 a.m. to 12 p.m. CT/10 a.m. to 1 p.m. ET. Peewee riders will kick things off at 12:30 p.m. CT/1:30 p.m. ET, followed b

articles.title: Alabama Lions Sight provides eye screenings for locals
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body: On Friday morning, the Alabama Lions Sight Mobile Eye Clinic was in Valley Medical Park doing vision checks for local people outside the Chambers County Health Department. Dr. Ryan Price of Anniston and assistant Trenton Sadler travel 22East Alabama counties performing comprehensive eye exams and, if needed, offering new prescriptions for glasses. Dr. Price also checks local eyes for cataracts, glaucoma and diabetic eye disease. ‘We look for anything you should be going to your local optometrist

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Cathy Hunt: I’m Knackered But Chuffed!
articles.author: Cathy Hunt
articles.body:
In celebration of Shakespeare’s (supposed) birthday tomorrow, I thought it might be appropriate to have a little cheeky (mischievous) fun with British versus American vocabulary today. Admittedly, I’m an Anglophile who is enamored of British literature, history, and even the royal family. Adding that to my love of language and my penchant for British mystery novels and television shows, I find myself incorporating more and more of their common vocabulary and slang into my own speech.

I’ve also

articles.title: Lanett hears concerns of water meter readings, water use
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
A major issue facing the City of Lanett emerged in a work session prior to Monday’s regular council meeting. It appears that the city is purchasing substantially more water from the Chattahoochee Valley Water Supply District (CVWSD) than it’s selling to its utility customers. This translates to a water loss of nearly 70 percent.

This came up in a report by Phillip Morgan of Phillip Morgan and Company, Birmingham, in reviewing the annual audit his company had prepared for the city. The audit for

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Lynn Gendusa: Not Just Another Day
articles.author: Lynn Gendusa
articles.body:
It was Easter Sunday, April 9, 1939. Of course, Easter is never “just another day,” but is any day?

Every day, something is happening somewhere to someone, every single second. Events—sad or glorious, troublesome or jubilant—swirl together to form the richness of life.

The air was cool in the mountains of Tennessee, and Elizabeth prayed the rain would stop before church. After the service, a ray of sunlight broke through a stained-glass window as she walked down the aisle and married my father

articles.title: MOBIS, West Point hosts Community Conservation Day
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Hyundai MOBIS Georgia (MGA) and the City of West Point are partnering for a Community Conservation Day this Saturday, April 25.

MOBIS Georgia will be helping the city with some cleanup and beautification efforts underway at the River Park Trails. Work during the Conservation Day will include picking up trash along the trails, helping to clear foliage that has been cut down and other park improvements.

Jacob Holcomb, general administration specialist for MOBIS Georgia, said the Conservation Day

articles.title: Luverne library emphasizes National Library Week
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Luverne Public Library is observing National Library Week which runs through April 25. During the emphasis, the facility is offering community involvement and fun all week, culminating in a special Saturday fun-day celebration.

“We are so excited to highlight our programs and services that are available to the community during National Library Week,” said library director Kristen Sanders. "We were lucky in that we had several of our regular monthly programs fall this week, so we were able t

articles.title: Lauren Duffell rings 'bell of success'
articles.author: Lanell Downs Smith
articles.body: Highland Home school graduate Lauren Duffell rang the "bell of success" Tuesday, April 21 in a ceremony honoring her acceptance into the Auburn Veterinary School program. She delivered a speech during the ceremony announcing her achievement. Duffell has also been accepted to join Omicron Delta Kappa, a national honor society recognizing service and academic excellence.

articles.title: Squadron advances to post-season play
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Highland Home Flying Squadron baseball team finished up area play on Wednesday, April 15, defeating The Calhoun School, 17 to 0 and 22 to 0.

In the first game, Tanner Sexton started by striking out 10 batters. Kent Beasley had four runs batted in (RBIs) on three hits.

In the second game, Sexton made three hits and a double. Conner Sampley started the game giving up no hits and no runs in three innings of play. He also struck out six batters.

“This put us the area champions,” said head coa

articles.title: Civil liberty, legal groups condemn indictments against SPLC
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
A slew of nonprofit and legal organizations slammed the U.S. Department of Justice following its announcement of indictments against the Southern Poverty Law Center.

The 11indictmentsreturned by a Middle District of Alabama federal jury on Tuesday relate to the organization’s hiring of paid informants in extremist groups.

The indictments allege that SPLC committed wire fraud, made false statements to a federally insured bank and engaged in a conspiracy to conceal money laundering to obscure pa

articles.title: Jefferson County Commissioner Sheila Tyson endorses Alicia Escott Lumpkin in District 60
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
On Wednesday, Jefferson County District 2 Commissioner Sheila Tyson announced her endorsement of Alicia Escott Lumpkin’s campaign for Alabama House District 60.

“I’ve never backed down from a fight for our community. I know the kind of leadership it takes to deliver. Alicia is ready to step up. That’s why I’m proud to endorse her,” Tyson said.

Lumpkin touted Tyson’s endorsement as a significant backing just weeks ahead of Alabama’s May 19 primary elections.

“Commissioner Tyson has spent her c

articles.title: Gulf Coast rail revival exceeds forecasts, tops 100,000 riders
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Joint Legislative Transportation Committee received an update Wednesday on the return of passenger rail service along the Gulf Coast, as state and Amtrak officials highlighted strong ridership and economic impact less than a year after launch.

Stephen McNair, director of policy and legislative affairs for the City of Mobile, told lawmakers the restored service marks the first time passenger trains have operated between Mobile and New Orleans since Hurricane Katrina disrupted service in 2005

articles.title: Opinion | Are we eating ourselves to death?
articles.author: Tom Greene
articles.body:
In 1900, the average life expectancy in the United States was49 years old. When you went “toes up,” it was likely caused bypneumonia, tuberculosis or diarrhea. Yes, diarrhea. Today, our life expectancy far exceeds age 48. We are more aware than ever of healthy behaviors and are inundated with information on how to lead a healthier life. You know—eat right, exercise, and stay away from lung darts and booze.

To some degree, we are following this advice. Alcohol consumption is at an all-time low.

articles.title: Opinion | How Alabama leaders can stop predatory gambling
articles.author: Graham Hodges
articles.body:
Sports gambling is everywhere, especially on college campuses like ours. Any student will tell you that. If you turn on ESPN, get on X or listen to a sports podcast, all you hear is “What is the line?”

It’s so prevalent that it may come as a surprise that Alabama doesn’t allow legal, regulated online sports betting companies to operate in our state—which means the only options for my classmates are illegal operations and shady offshore platforms.

The reality is that the unregulated and uncheck

articles.title: Stillman announces Magic Johnson as 150th anniversary commencement speaker
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Stillman College announced this week that it will welcome business leader, philanthropist and NBA legend Earvin “Magic” Johnson as the keynote speaker for its 2026 commencement ceremony on Saturday, May 9, 2026.

The ceremony comes during the college’s sesquicentennial, or 150th anniversary, celebration. Founded in 1876, Stillman said it has remained committed to educating and empowering students to lead and serve in communities across the nation and beyond.

The college will hold commencement o

articles.title: Revenue Commissioner Vernon Barnett to retire, replacement tapped
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey announced Wednesday that Alabama Department of Revenue Commissioner Vernon Barnett will retire from state service at the end of April. Ivey selected Mary Martin Mitchell, a senior attorney in the department, to succeed him.

“Vernon has been an outstanding member of the Ivey administration from the beginning and has served the people of our state faithfully. When I took office in 2017, I promised the people of Alabama I would leave this state better than we found it. Vernon’s l

articles.title: Opinion | Alabama’s outdoors is key to our state’s economic growth
articles.author: Chris Blankenship
articles.body:
From the mountains of the Tennessee Valley to the shores of the Gulf Coast, and everything in-between, our state is second to none in the country when it comes to beautiful outdoors and recreation.

Whether it’s camping and hiking across Alabama’s state parks, fishing our streams, rivers, coastlines and bays, or hunting in our state’s sprawling forests—there is truly something for everyone who has a love and appreciation for the outdoors.

But Alabama’s natural beauty is about more than just rec

articles.title: Alabama recognized for pre-K quality for 20th year
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
A new national report released Wednesday highlights Alabama as one of only six states in the country to meet 10 of 10 research-based quality benchmarks for preschool programs—an achievement that underscores the state’s commitment to effective early childhood education.

Alabama served 40 percent of 4-year-olds in state-funded pre-K during the 2024-2025 school year and continued to expand access to high-quality early learning statewide. The state ranked 18th nationally in preschool access for 4-y

articles.title: Grand jury indicts SPLC in federal case on paid informants
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
A federal grand jury on Tuesday returned 11 indictments against the Southern Poverty Law Center in a case based on the organization’s hiring of paid informants within extremist groups.

The indictments allege that SPLC committed wire fraud, made false statements to a federally insured bank and committed conspiracy to conceal money laundering to obfuscate payments to informants.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche went further and accused the anti-extremism group of actually funding the groups

articles.title: Legal experts skeptical of DOJ’s criminal case against Southern Poverty Law Center
articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
The headquarters of the Southern Poverty Law Center in Montgomery, Alabama on February 8, 2023. Legal experts Wednesday expressed doubts about the U.S. Department of Justice's attempt to prosecute the civil rights organization over allegations of fraud. (Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector)

articles.title: On Harper Lee’s 100th birthday, ‘To Kill A Mockingbird’ still feels relevant
articles.author: Catherine Dorrough
articles.body:
Pulitzer Prize winner and "To Kill A Mockingbird" author Harper Lee smiles before receiving the 2007 Presidential Medal of Freedom in the East Room of the White House November 5, 2007 in Washington, DC.  April 28th is the 100th anniversary of the birth of the "To Kill A Mockingbird" author. (Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images)

I owe two apologies.

The first is to Virginia L. Allison, a middle school English teacher whose classroom copy of “To Kill a Mockingbird” I stole around 1999. My desk sat nex

articles.title: Alabama’s Pre-K program wins national praise — with a caveat
articles.author: Andrea Tinker
articles.body:
Jennifer Frazer, an auxiliary teacher, plays musical sticks with students at Faulkner University Pre-K in Montgomery, Ala.,  Tuesday, May 21, 2024. A new report praised the quality of Alabama's Pre-K program, but urged officials to work to make the program universal.  (Alabama Reflector Photo by Stew Milne)

A new report praised the quality of Alabama’s Pre-K program for a 20th consecutive year, though theNational Institute for Early Education Research (NIEER)did have criticisms for the state.

articles.title: Congress struggles with questions about ethics investigations after 3 members resign
articles.author: Jennifer Shutt
articles.body:
Sen. James Lankford, R-Okla., speaks with the press about ethics investigations at the U.S. Capitol on April 21, 2026 in Washington, D.C. (Photo by Heather Diehl/Getty Images)

WASHINGTON — U.S. Senate Republican leaders Tuesday defended the secretive process used in that chamber to investigate allegations of wrongdoing, though they did confirm referring a complaint made against Arizona Democratic Sen. Ruben Gallego to the Ethics Committee.

articles.title: Trump’s ‘dummymandering’ leaves US House remap in stalemate after Virginia vote
articles.author: Jacob Fischler
articles.body:
The U.S. Capitol on the evening of Sept. 30, 2025. (Photo by Ashley Murray/States Newsroom)

The race by each party to redraw U.S. House districts in their favor could be headed for a draw after Tuesday’s big win for Democrats in Virginia, though major shifts are still possible before crucial midterm elections in November.

Virginia voters approved a constitutional amendment that clears the path for the state’s legislature, controlled by Democrats, to redraw congressional district lines to benef

articles.title: US Senate Dems to force votes on rising costs, immigration crackdown in marathon session
articles.author: Jennifer Shutt
articles.body:
Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer talks to reporters at the U.S. Capitol on Feb. 7, 2024. (Photo by Jennifer Shutt/States Newsroom)

WASHINGTON — U.S. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said Wednesday that Democrats will use the unlimited number of amendment votes they are allowed on Republicans’ budget resolution to illustrate policy differences on cost-of-living issues and immigration activities.

Dr. Mehmet Oz, administrator of the federal Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, speaks at the Department of Health and Human Services in Washington, D.C., in December. Oz said Tuesday that the Trump administration will require every state within 30 days to turn in a plan to revalidate the health care providers that participate in their Medicaid programs.

articles.title: Parents plead guilty in death of child, 3
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The parents of 3-year-old Bentley Fowler, who died in 2020 after being left in a hot car for more than 15 hours, have both pled guilty to manslaughter charges.

Rather than going to trial, which was slated to begin last week, Brandi Burks, 27, and Dakota Fowler, 25, pleaded guilty to their respective charges on April 13, according to court documents.

As a result of the plea agreement, both Burks and Fowler will serve six-year prison sentences.

A sentencing hearing is set for Oct

articles.title: LEAVING A LASTING LEGACY
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
THARPTOWN – Over the past 21 years, Tharptown schools have seen a plethora of changes as students and teachers alike come and go and the education landscape continues to evolve.

One constant over those two decades, however, has been high school librarian Melissa Harrison, who has watched the school system blossom from a junior high school to one of the fastest-growing institutions in Franklin County.

Now, as the school nears the graduation of its 20th class as a high school, it prepares to say

articles.title: Lawsuit’s dismissal is tied to written promise
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Franklin County Schools is waiting on a written commitment from the Alabama Department of Conservation and Natural Resources before dropping a lawsuit tied to school land. Legislation intended to resolve the issue is now on the governor’s desk. Superintendent Greg Hamilton said in a board of education meeting last week that SB 337 would allow the school system to obtain deeds to indemnity land connected to Section 16 school trust property.

Attorney Danny McDowell said the measure

articles.title: Investigator details charges in child porn case
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Details from an interview between Abigail Roberts and an investigator regarding the child pornography and sodomy charges against the 22-year-old were disturbing enough to move two inmates to tears during a preliminary hearing Thursday.

In accordance with Aniah’s Law, which allows violent offenders to be held without bond, Roberts will remain in the Franklin County Jail after District Judge Jamie Sumerel denied her request for bond.

In total, Roberts faces at least 106 charges, i

articles.title: Hwy. 36 access project approved
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Franklin County commissioners have agreed to move forward with a a Highway 36 industrial access project.

Assistant County Engineer Caleb Sherrill said the project will create a new entrance to serve 4E Aggregates.

“It was kind of a larger project, but once we worked with the state this is what we’ve got,” Sherrill said of the project, which will be funded by a $121,600 grant that requires no local match.

A March 10 letter from the Alabama Department of Transportation shows the

articles.title: Generational investment has regional impact
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
On March 20 we marked the beginning of something truly significant, not just for one community, but for all of north Alabama. The announcement of a $2 billion investment in northwest Alabama is more than a milestone. It is a generational opportunity that will help reshape north Alabama’s economy for decades to come.

Projects like this do not happen overnight. They are the result of years of preparation, partnership, and persistence. This happened because people believed in this community, and b

articles.title: Her’s is a legacy few can claim
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body: Most school officials have an impact on the lives of the students they encounter over the years. Long after their professional careers have begun, students can quickly recall the names of their favorite teachers and which grade they taught in. They can recount specific instances of when the actions of those teachers helped them overcome a problem. They can remember with affection the compassion of a particular teacher or school administrator. The blessings of watching impressionable children gro

articles.title: Broadway salute takes stage April 23-26
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
“The Roxy’s Salute to Broadway” will be held April 23-26 at the historic Roxy Theatre in downtown Russellville. The production features music from five Broadway shows, with many selections presented as medleys.

Shows are April 23-25 at 7 p.m. and April 26 at 2 p.m. Tickets are available at itickets.com or at the door. Doors open one hour before showtime. Tickets are $15 for ages 11 and up; $10 for ages 4 to 10; and children under the age of 4 are admitted free but must sit on an adult’s lap.

T

articles.title: RHS softball goes 3-1 in NW Alabama Bash
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville High softball team went 4-2 during a week of games that included participation in the Northwest Alabama Bash at the Sportsplex in Florence.

RHS started the week with a trip to Austin High School to face the Lady Bears. Austin got the 7-6 extra-inning win by scoring twice in the bottom half of the eighth inning after the Lady Golden Tigers scored one run in the top of the frame.

Russellville’s Chloe James led the team with three hits and one RBI. Khristyan Huerta and Sadie Odom

articles.title: Red Bay wins 3 of 4; Tharptown wins 2
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RedBayandTharptown led the way last week in softball with Red Bay winning 3 of 4 games and Tharptown earning a couple of big wins, downing Phil Campbell 6-2 in the Northwest Alabama Bash held Friday at the Sportsplext in Florence, after topping Lindsey Lane 10-2 earlier in the week.

Red Bay 15 Sheffield 0

Charley Lindley was a perfect 3-for-3 at the plate to lead Red Bay to a 15-0 win over Sheffield.

Lindley also earned the win in the circle for the Tigers, pitching a one-hit shutout and stri

articles.title: Competitive eater completes challenge
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — A local restaurant is getting attention after a competitive eater finished a massive meal with just seconds to spare, turning a simple food challenge into a popular attraction for the community.

Catfish Springs recently introduced its “Big Catch Catfish Challenge,” a meal so large many customers now come just to watch others attempt it. The challenge includes 20 pieces of catfish, a pound of fries, 8 hush puppies and 3 pints of coleslaw, all to be eaten within a limited time.

Co

articles.title: BTCPA closes season with ‘The Red Velvet Cake War’
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RED BAY — The Bay Tree Council for the Performing Arts will close its season with a production of “The Red Velvet Cake War,” a comedy by Jessie Jones, Nicholas Hope and Jamie Wooten.

Scotty Kennedy will be directing.

Remaining performances are Thursday through Saturday at 7 p.m. with a matinee Sunday at 2 p.m.

Tickets are available at the Weatherford Centre in Red Bay on weekdays from 2 to 4 p.m. Tickets are $10. Reservations can be made by calling 256998-6781.

Set in the small town of Sweet

articles.title: Internet crimes unit wants Romanian predator
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Romanian police are searching for a man who is believed to have contacted a special needs 16-year-old female in Franklin County via the internet and allegedly started talking about meeting her to have sex.

Sheriff’s Sgt. Kyle Palmer, who is a member of the Internet Crimes Against Children Task Force, said the teenager contacted the sheriff’s office in March about the messages she was receiving via the internet.

“They began chatting and that led to further conversations,” Palmer

articles.title: $4.2M paving project nears end
articles.author: tecnavia

articles.title: Rambo wins state Farm-City essay contest
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — Phil Campbell High School senior Emma Rambo has been named the state winner of the Alabama Farm-City Essay Contest, earning first place after advancing from the county level.

Her essay, written on this year’s theme “Grow Alabama,” focused on the role farmers and communities play in sustaining agriculture and supporting one another.

“It was mostly about how farmers grow Alabama,” Rambo said. “I talked about the way I raise dogs for duck hunting, the way I plant a garden, and the

articles.title: County settles land sale violations for $60K
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The Franklin County Commission is moving forward with a settlement tied to a controversial land development after officials said the company violated subdivision rules by selling property before getting approval.

During a work session last week, commissioners discussed an agreement with Classic Country Land, the company behind a large development near the Coon Dog Cemetery area. County Attorney Evan Hargett told commissioners the issue centers on the company selling lots before t

articles.title: Franks wins Bryant-Jordan Achievement Award
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Natalee Franks, a senior at Tharptown High School, received the Bryant-Jordan Achievement Award as a regional winner and received a $3,000 scholarship.

“That award means so much,” Franks said. “It shows that I can do what I set my mind to, and I can get through anything because of what I got through to get there.”

Franks said she faced challenges from an early age, having to spend part of her childhood in foster care before later living in an environment she described as difficu

articles.title: Kids were hopping to be healthy
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Chalk prompts and hopscotch squares lined the sidewalk outside the Russellville Public Library recently, forming a short course of movement challenges.

Chalk directions listed activities such as toe touches and arm circles with numbered squares and arrows marking the route.

A spin-the-wheel game added additional challenges, including jumping jacks, squats, dancing and balancing on one foot.

The library partnered with the Franklin County Extension Office to host the “Move Across

Raymond McKinney, 66, of Phil Campbell passed away April 14. Visitation took place at Spry Memorial Chapel on April 17 from noon to 2 p.m. with the service immediately following. Burial was in Dempsey Cemetery.

articles.title: RHS wins Area 14; hosts playoffs Friday
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Russellville High took 2 of 3 games from rival West Point last week to clinch first place in the AHSAA Class 5A Area 14 baseball standings.

The Golden Tigers will host the Sardis Lions in the first round of the Class 5A playoffs with the series scheduled to begin Friday.

Russellville traveled to West Point on April 14 and fell to the Warriors 5-4. The Golden Tigers were ahead 4-3 going into the bottom half of the sixth, but West Point scored twice during the frame to take the lead.

Three of t

articles.title: Belgreen punches way into playoffs
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Belgreen Bulldogs pummeled the Tharptown Wildcats in two straight games to sweep their way into the first round of the Class 2A playoffs.

Belgreen (13-12; 2-2) beat Tharptown 12-3 and 19-3 to earn the runner-up spot in Class 2A Area 14. The Bulldogs will travel to Pleasant Valley for first-round playoff action. They will play Thursday at 7 p.m. and Friday, if necessary, at 2 p.m., at Ohatchee High School.

Ty Hamilton gave up just two hits, one earned run and struck out eight through five i

articles.title: Shot clock arrives For AHSAA games
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The shot clock, part of college basketball for the last four decades, is migrating to Alabama high schools.

The Alabama High School Athletic Association announced April 15 that it is mandating a 35-second shot clock starting with the 2026-27 season.

Stall ball is out. The AHSAA previously had allowed games to use a shot clock only if both head coaches agreed to it.

The cost will be a key factor.

Deshler has a shotclock system that was used in the annual post-Christmas Bracy Invitational and

articles.title: Red Bay sweeps Sulligent; wins Area 10 title
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Red Bay Tigers swept the Sulligent Blue Devils last week to go undefeated and win the Class 2A Area 10 championship. Red Bay easily beat Sulligent 17-1 and 14-3 to capture the title.

Red Bay will host W e s t m i n s t e r – O a k Mountain in the first round of the playoffs this week.

In game one, Carson McGee hit two home runs and drove in eight to lead Red Bay’s 17-1 blowout of the Blue Devils. McGee homered in the first and third innings. McGee’s first home run was a threerun shot to le

articles.title: Bobcats win area title
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Phil Campbell Bobcats captured the Class 3A Area 14 championship with a decisive win over the Colbert Heights Wildcats Friday night.

The Bobcats (21-11; 5-1) needed only one win going into the series to claim the title but lost game one 9-7 at Colbert Heights on Thursday. Phil Campbell responded with a 7-1 win on Friday to win the championship.

Phil Campbell, after winning its ninth area title in 10 playoff seasons, hosts Geraldine in the first round of the playoffs.

Phil Campbell struggl

articles.title: Interim Police Chief states his intentions while addressing recent gun-related incident
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
Selma’s Interim Police Chief Michael Perry released a statement to the residents of Selma stating his intention as the interim police chief of the Selma Police Department and addressing a weekend incident that had the potential to turn deadly.

Mayor Johnny Moss III announced Perry’s appointment to the position on April 14 after Police Chief Kenta Fulford was placed on administrative leave during a Selma City Council meeting on April 10.

In his statement, Perry said he’s honored to serve as int

articles.title: BBCF awards $336K in grants to local organizations, nonprofits
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
The Black Belt Community Foundation awarded more than $335,000 at its 2026 Arts and Community Grants Awards Ceremony on Saturday, April 18, at the Carl C. Morgan Convention Center.

These grants aim to support projects that strengthen, uplift and empower communities across Alabama’s Black Belt.

A total of about $336,000 was awarded to 147 different community organizations and nonprofits across the BBCF’s 12-county service area–Bullock, Choctaw, Dallas, Greene, Hale, Lowndes, Macon, Marengo, Per

articles.title: TROY environmental clubs to host Earth Day Festival April 24
articles.author: Savanah Weed
articles.body:
Troy University is celebrating Earth Day on Friday, April 24 with an afternoon of activities on the Academic Quad and at the Troy University Arboretum.

A joint venture between the Troy Naturalist Club, Troy University Environmental Club and Troy University Marine Biology Club, the Earth Day Festival will feature live animals cyanotyping art with natural and found materials, edible insects, educational zones and a swap meet for students to exchange items.

“The environment affects every aspect o

articles.title: TroyFest set for this weekend
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The annual TroyFest event will be held in Downtown Troy beginning Saturday, April 25, and continuing through Sunday, April 26.

TroyFest was originally conceived as the Jean Lake Memorial Art Show in 1982 in honor of legendary Troy artist Jean Lake. In 2023, the annual festival moved to The Square in downtown Troy and was renamed TroyFest. This year will be the 44th overall festival and the 23rd since being named TroyFest.

This year’s TroyFest is sponsored by a number of local businesses and mu

articles.title: Troy BOE approves lease for new STEM academy
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
In a busy agenda, the Troy City Schools Board of Education approved the lease agreement for the future Academy Street Career Innovation Academy at its meeting on April 22.

Previously, the Troy City Council and Pike County Schools Board of Education agreed to the lease, as well. The lease agreement will see Pike County Schools pay 100 percent of the utilities and pay the city $10 per year for the 50-year agreement. The agreement will also see the city house an IT office in the building and hire
